	300Tdi or well-documented TD5

	Ingenium timing chain wear (early 204DTD) • TD5 injector-loom oil migration
	Homepage Ranking
	🏆 Safest Buy and Best Long-Term Keeper in our Land Rover Ownership Rankings
	One-line verdict: The Defender is the only Land Rover where engine replacement is financially safe at almost any spec — the question is never "is it worth it?", only "which engine do I have and what does it cost?"

SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: Defender Generations — Classic or L663?
Sub-headline: Two Defenders, two completely different ownership propositions. Select yours to explore engines, problems, and costs.
Generation Cards (opinionated format per v2 spec)
Defender Classic (L316)
 1990–2016 • 200Tdi, 300Tdi, TD5, 2.4/2.2 TDCi
⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
Our Verdict: Mechanically honest and endlessly rebuildable — buy on engine choice, not on year. 300Tdi for simplicity, TD5 for usability, approach the 2.4 TDCi Puma with a full injector history.
 Explore the Classic →
Defender L663 🔥 Most Enquired — 292 enquiries (2025) [EM-VERIFIED]
 2020–present • D200, D240, D250, D300, P300, P400, P400e, P525
⭐⭐⭐⭐½
Our Verdict: The safest ownership proposition in the entire JLR range. High residuals mean replacement is always economically viable; the six-cylinder diesels are proving genuinely robust. Prefer 2021+ builds.
 Explore the L663 →
The Classic vs L663 Question
Unlike any other Land Rover, the Defender's two generations aren't an evolution — they're different vehicles sharing a name. The Classic is a body-on-frame working tool whose engines can be rebuilt indefinitely at reasonable cost. The L663 is an aluminium-monocoque luxury 4x4 whose engines are modular Ingenium units — replaced, not rebuilt at the roadside. Your repair-vs-replace maths, your specialist network, and your parts supply are completely different depending on which side of 2016 your Defender sits.
Read the full comparison: Defender Classic vs L663 — The Honest Ownership Verdict →

Confidence Score panel (per v2 spec — evidence-based ratings):
How confident are these ratings? L663 Ingenium ratings: 92% confidence — based on 292 Defender-specific enquiries [EM-VERIFIED], cross-referenced against 1,150+ 204DTD enquiries across the JLR range, plus specialist rebuild reports. Classic engine ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — built on documented failure patterns and specialist interviews rather than proprietary volume data (per our data-integrity policy, we label rather than inflate).
Data note (internal): Enquiry volumes from EM_Proprietary_Data_2025.txt [EM-VERIFIED]. Costs from Table A [EM-QUOTE]. Classic-era enquiry volumes not yet captured — flagged as pattern data, not EM-VERIFIED.

SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common Defender Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The failures Defender owners actually report — with symptoms, costs, and urgency levels.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	2.4 TDCi Injector & Crankshaft Failure
	The Classic Puma's defining weakness. Injector seal failure washes bores with diesel; unchecked, it ends in bottom-end failure. £3,200–£5,500 recon [EM-QUOTE]. Affects 2007–2011 Classic.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	2
	TD5 Injector Harness Oil Migration
	Oil wicks up the injector loom into the ECU connector — rough running, misfires, apparent 'ECU failure'. Cheap fix caught early; expensive misdiagnosis if not.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	3
	TD5 Cylinder Head Cracking (early 10P)
	Pre-2002 10P heads can crack between valves — coolant loss and white smoke. Later 15P heads largely resolved it.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	4
	204DTD Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
	The Ingenium diesel's known weak point, mostly on pre-2019-derived units. Cold-start rattle is the warning. Early L663 D200/D240 builds are the ones to check.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	5
	Ingenium Diesel Oil Dilution (DPF regen)
	Short-journey L663 diesels can suffer fuel-in-oil dilution from interrupted DPF regens — accelerates wear if oil changes are stretched.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	6
	300Tdi Timing Belt Failure
	The one thing that kills an otherwise immortal engine. Belt and tensioner every 60k miles or 5 years — no exceptions.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service
Each card links to a dedicated failure page: symptoms, root cause, repair cost vs replacement cost, urgency, next steps.

	Pattern data

	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: Defender L663 owners are disproportionately pre-purchase researchers — a notable share of our 2025 Defender enquiries were inspection and due-diligence requests rather than failure reports, the inverse of the Discovery 4 pattern. That's what a reputation for reliability looks like in enquiry data.
* The L663 is the only JLR model in our data where enquiry volume is rising while failure-driven enquiries stay flat — demand is growing faster than problems are.
* Early D200/D240 (2020–21 builds) generate the majority of L663 engine-concern enquiries; six-cylinder D250/D300 cars barely register. (Editorial synthesis of failure-pattern data — sense-check with JLR specialist before publishing, per homepage data-note policy.)
Live Enquiry Feed module embeds here — e.g. "Defender L663 (D300 / AJ20D6) • Aberdeen • Pre-purchase inspection • 7 hours ago" [EM-VERIFIED]. (Note: the source feed currently labels this entry "306DT" — correct the label at source.)

Labour estimate: Fitting a Defender engine typically takes 10–20 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [EM-QUOTE].
The economics box (gold callout):
The Defender rule: With L663 values of £35,000–£90,000, even a £9,500 recon engine sits far below our 60% scrap-threshold rule — replacement is ✅ Safe at any spec. Classic Defenders behave the same way for the opposite reason: values have risen so far that a £4,500 recon 300Tdi is trivial against a £15,000–£40,000 vehicle. The Defender is the one Land Rover we never recommend scrapping over an engine.

SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW 2 SERIES ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the Defender Engine Range Evolved — 1990 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	What Changed
	Worth Knowing
	200Tdi era
	1990–1994
	200Tdi
	The engine that made the Defender viable — direct-injection turbodiesel torque in a farm tool
	So over-engineered it's still the conversion engine of choice for Series Land Rovers
	300Tdi era
	1994–1998
	300Tdi
	Refinement pass on the 200Tdi — quieter, smoother, EU-emissions ready
	Arguably the most reliable engine Land Rover ever fitted; belt service is its only real demand
	TD5 era
	1998–2007
	TD5
	Land Rover's first electronically managed five-cylinder diesel
	The last engine designed in-house at Land Rover before Ford-era platforms
	Puma era
	2007–2016
	2.4 TDCi, 2.2 TDCi
	Ford Duratorq units brought a 6-speed box — and the Classic's least reliable engines
	The 2.2 (2012+) resolved much of the 2.4's injector fragility
	Production gap
	2016–2020
	—
	Classic production ends January 2016
	Values began climbing almost immediately
	L663 era
	2020–present
	204DTD, AJ20D6, 204PT, AJ133, P400e
	Aluminium monocoque, Ingenium modular engines, mild-hybrid and PHEV
	Four-cylinder diesels dropped after 2021 — the six-cylinder AJ20D6 D250/D300 became the core of the range. Note: the L663 never used the Ford-derived 306DT V6.
	Each era links to its generation page: Classic → • L663 →

SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW 2 SERIES? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a Defender?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Family Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	L663 110/130 only — genuinely practical, and the safest JLR to own long-term. Classic is a lifestyle choice, not family transport.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	L663 D250 is excellent; avoid diesel L663s on short urban runs (oil-dilution risk). Classic Defenders make hard work of commuting.
	Towing & Working Use
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	Best in class. D300's 650Nm and 3,500kg capacity, or a 300Tdi that will tow forever at its own pace.
	Budget Owner
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	Counter-intuitive: strong values mean repairs are always worth it, but nothing about a Defender is cheap. A tidy TD5 is the value sweet spot.
	Investor / Keeper
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	Our Best Long-Term Keeper across the whole JLR range. Classic values rising; L663 residuals class-leading.
	________________

SECTION 14 — READY TO MAKE A DECISION? — <ClosingActionCards>
H2: Defender Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a Defender engine replacement cost in the UK?
 A reconditioned Defender engine typically costs £2,500–£9,500 supply only, depending on generation and engine [EM-QUOTE]. Classic Tdi units sit at £2,500–£4,500; L663 six-cylinder diesels at £6,500–£9,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,900 depending on labour rates. Use our Defender calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which Defender engine is the most reliable?
 On the Classic, the 300Tdi — mechanically injected, endlessly rebuildable, and demanding little beyond disciplined timing-belt changes. On the L663, the six-cylinder D300 is proving the most dependable unit in our data.
3. Which Defender engine should I avoid?
 The 2.4 TDCi 'Puma' (2007–2011) carries the Classic's highest failure risk — injector seal failure can end in bottom-end damage. On the L663, early 2020–21 four-cylinder D200/D240 builds warrant a timing-chain check before purchase.
4. Is the new Defender (L663) reliable?
 Yes — it holds our 🏆 Safest Buy ranking across the entire Land Rover range. The six-cylinder engines are proving robust, and with host values of £35,000–£90,000, even a worst-case engine replacement is comfortably economically viable [EM-QUOTE].
5. Is it worth replacing a Defender engine?
 Almost always — uniquely among Land Rovers. L663 values and rising Classic values mean replacement cost rarely approaches our 60% scrap-threshold rule. Use the Ownership Decision Tool for your specific vehicle.
6. How do I find my Defender engine code?
 Classic: stamped on the block (left side on Tdi/TD5) and on the chassis/VIN plate. L663: listed in the vehicle documentation and readable via the VIN — or tell us your variant (D250, P400 etc.) and we'll identify it.
7. Defender Classic vs L663 — which should I buy?
 Different vehicles for different lives. The Classic is a rebuildable working tool and appreciating asset; the L663 is a refined, safe long-term keeper. Read the full head-to-head verdict →
8. TD5 or TDCi — which Classic Defender engine is better?
 The TD5 — a stronger long-term proposition with well-understood, inexpensive weak points (injector loom, early head castings). The TDCi's 6-speed box is nicer to drive, but the 2.4's injector and crank history demands full documentation. Compare TD5 vs TDCi →

Sub-headline: Five generations, five very different ownership propositions. Select yours to explore engines, problems, and costs.
Discovery 1 (D1)
 1989–1998 • 200Tdi, 300Tdi, 3.5/3.9 V8
⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
Our Verdict: The honest original. The Tdi engines are effectively immortal — the body and chassis rust long before the engine tires. Buy on structure, not mileage.
 Explore Discovery 1 →
Discovery 2 (D2)
 1998–2004 • TD5, 4.0 V8
⭐⭐⭐☆☆
Our Verdict: The TD5 is a strong, well-understood engine with cheap known fixes. The vehicle around it — rear chassis rot, ACE suspension — is the real buying risk.
 Explore Discovery 2 →
Discovery 3 (L319)
 2004–2009 • 2.7 TDV6 (276DT), 4.4 V8
⭐⭐⭐☆☆
Our Verdict: A generational leap in capability carrying the range's most sudden failure mode — 276DT oil pump failure gives little warning. Full service history or walk away.
 Explore Discovery 3 →
Discovery 4 (L319) 🔥 Most Enquired Generation [EM-VERIFIED]
 2009–2016 • 2.7 TDV6 (early), 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 (306DT), 5.0 V8 (AJ133)
⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
Our Verdict: Best value in the range if maintained correctly. The SDV6 crank-bearing risk is real at higher mileages — proven oil-interval history is the whole game.
 Explore Discovery 4 →
Discovery 5 (L462)
 2017–present • 204DTD, 306DT TD6/SDV6 (2017–2020), AJ20D6 D250/D300 (2021+), 204PT
⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
Our Verdict: Safest modern choice and our Best Family Land Rover. Prefer 2021+ builds: the AJ20D6 six replaces both the old V6 and the early four-cylinder concerns in one move.
 Explore Discovery 5 →

SECTION 6: COMMON DISCOVERY ENGINE PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems> (6 cards)
H2: Common Discovery Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The failures Discovery owners actually report — with symptoms, costs, and urgency levels.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	306DT Crankshaft Bearing Failure
	The 3.0 TDV6/SDV6's defining weak point. Cold-start knock is the early warning; once bearing failure starts there's rarely anything left to rebuild. £6,500–£9,500 recon [EM-QUOTE]. Affects Discovery 4 and 2017–2020 Discovery 5.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	2
	276DT Oil Pump Housing Failure
	Often kills the 2.7 TDV6 outright before any other symptom appears — the most sudden failure mode in our data. Affects Discovery 3 and early Discovery 4.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	3
	204DTD Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
	Pre-2019 Ingenium diesel tensioner wear; cold-start rattle is the warning. Significantly improved from 2019. Affects 2017–2019 Discovery 5.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	4
	Air Suspension Compressor Failure
	Common across Discovery 3, 4 and 5; compressor burnout presents a fire risk. Not an engine failure, but the most common non-engine enquiry we receive on this model.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	5
	TD5 Injector Harness Oil Migration
	Oil wicks up the injector loom into the ECU connector — rough running misdiagnosed as ECU failure. Cheap fix caught early. Affects Discovery 2.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	6
	Ingenium Diesel Oil Dilution (DPF regen)
	Short-journey D5 diesels can suffer fuel-in-oil dilution from interrupted DPF regens — accelerates wear if oil changes are stretched.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

The economics box (gold callout):
The Discovery rule: Unlike the Defender, replacement is not automatically safe here. A Discovery 4 SDV6 worth £6,000–£18,000 against a £6,500–£9,500 recon sits at ⚠️ Watch on our 60% scrap-threshold rule — the cheapest cars in that band fail it. Discovery 3s on the 2.7 TDV6 increasingly fall below the threshold outright. Get a scrap valuation alongside any repair quote before committing.

SECTION 9: HOW THE DISCOVERY ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the Discovery Engine Range Evolved — 1989 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why Land Rover Changed
	Worth Knowing
	Tdi era
	1989–1998
	200Tdi, 300Tdi, Rover V8
	The Discovery existed to put Range Rover capability at family prices — the Tdi diesels made that economically real
	Same immortal engines as the Defender; the Discovery body rusts around them
	TD5 era
	1998–2004
	TD5, 4.0 V8
	Emissions rules demanded electronic management — the TD5 was Land Rover's answer, and its last fully in-house engine
	Shared with the Defender; parts and knowledge are everywhere
	Ford V6 era
	2004–2016
	276DT, 306DT, AJ-V8, AJ133
	Ford ownership brought the jointly developed 'Lion' V6 diesels — huge refinement gains, new failure modes
	The 276DT oil pump and 306DT crank bearing define this era's ownership risk
	Ingenium era
	2017–2020
	204DTD, 306DT (carried over)
	JLR independence meant in-house modular engines — the four-cylinder Ingenium arrived while the old V6 soldiered on
	Pre-2019 204DTD carries the timing-chain caveat; the V6 kept its crank one
	Ingenium-six era
	2021–present
	AJ20D6, 204PT
	One engine family to replace both problem lineages — the straight-six retired the Ford V6 and its bearing legacy in a single stroke
	The cleanest engine slate a Discovery has had since the 300Tdi
	________________


SECTION 10: WHO SHOULD BUY A DISCOVERY? — <WhoShouldBuy> (5 profile cards)
H2: Who Should Buy a Discovery?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Family Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The best seven-seat 4x4 ownership proposition in the range — our homepage's Best Family Land Rover. Discovery 5 first, well-documented Discovery 4 second.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	D5 P300 or D300 work well; avoid short-run diesel commuting (DPF/oil dilution). A Discovery 4 as a short-hop commuter is how crank bearings die.
	Towing & Working Use
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	3,500kg capacity across modern generations; the SDV6 and D300 are exceptional tow engines. Britain's caravan-club default for a reason.
	Budget Owner
	⭐⭐☆☆☆
	The trap tier. Cheap Discovery 3s and 4s carry engine risk that can exceed the car's value. If the budget can't absorb a £6,500 engine, buy a TD5-era car or don't buy a Discovery.
	Long-Term Keeper
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	A 2021+ D300 or a cherished Tdi are genuine keepers. The middle generations are keepers only with disciplined maintenance.
	________________

SECTION 13: DISCOVERY FAQ — <FAQAccordion> (8 questions)
H2: Discovery Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a Discovery engine replacement cost in the UK?
 A reconditioned Discovery engine typically costs £2,500–£9,500 supply only, depending on generation and engine [EM-QUOTE]. Tdi-era units sit at £2,500–£4,500; the 3.0 diesels at £6,500–£9,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,900. Use our Discovery calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which Discovery engine is the most reliable?
 Historically, the 300Tdi — mechanically injected and endlessly rebuildable. In the modern range, the AJ20D6 D300 (2021+) is proving the most dependable, followed by post-2019 Ingenium four-cylinders. (Note: the 4.4 SDV8 often cited as JLR's most reliable engine was never fitted to a Discovery.)
3. Which Discovery engine should I avoid?
 The 2.7 TDV6 (276DT) and 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 (306DT) carry the highest catastrophic-failure risk — oil pump failure and crankshaft bearing failure respectively, both capable of leaving nothing to rebuild. Neither is a reason to walk away from a documented car; both are reasons to walk away from an undocumented one.
4. How do I know if my Discovery engine needs replacing?
 Warning signs by engine: cold-start knock (306DT), sudden oil-pressure loss (276DT), cold-start chain rattle (pre-2019 204DTD), blue smoke or excessive oil consumption (any). Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a Discovery 4 engine?
 It depends on the car's value. A Discovery 4 worth £10,000–£18,000 justifies a £6,500–£9,500 reconditioned SDV6; a £6,000 car sits at our 60% scrap threshold [EM-QUOTE]. Use the Ownership Decision Tool for a personalised verdict.
6. Which Discovery generation should I buy?
 Discovery 5 (2021+) for the best all-round profile on the AJ20D6. Discovery 4 SDV6 for value — only with proven oil-interval history. Avoid any Discovery 3 or early 4 on the 2.7 TDV6 without full service history.
7. How do I find my Discovery engine code?
 Stamped on the engine block (typically the left side) and listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation — or tell us your variant (TDV6, SDV6, D300 etc.) and we'll identify it.
8. Discovery 4 vs Discovery 5 — which is more reliable?
 Discovery 5, particularly from 2021 on the AJ20D6 six. Discovery 4 is cheaper to buy and more capable off-road out of the box, but the SDV6 carries real crankshaft-bearing risk at higher mileages. Service history matters more than the badge. Read the full head-to-head →
________________

Discovery Sport (L550) 🔥 Most Enquired Model — 362 enquiries (2025) [EM-VERIFIED]
 2015–present • 224DT, 204DTD, Si4 GTDi, 204PT, P300e
⭐⭐⭐½

SECTION 6: COMMON DISCOVERY SPORT ENGINE PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems> (6 cards)
H2: Common Discovery Sport Engine Problems
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	204DTD Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
	The defining Discovery Sport issue and our #1 enquiry driver on this model [EM-VERIFIED]. Cold-start rattle is the warning; left alone, chain slip destroys the engine. Pre-2019 builds. £3,500–£5,800 recon [EM-QUOTE].
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	2
	Ingenium Diesel Oil Dilution (DPF regen)
	The school-run problem: short journeys interrupt DPF regeneration, fuel dilutes the oil, wear accelerates. The single biggest reason we point short-run drivers at the P300e.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	3
	204DTD EGR Cooler Failure
	Coolant loss and rough running on higher-mileage Ingenium diesels; repairable if caught before overheating causes head damage.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	4
	224DT Injector & DPF Issues
	The launch-era Duratorq's known weak points, carried over from Freelander 2 service life. Well understood, moderate cost.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	5
	PTU / Rear Drive Unit Failure
	Not an engine failure, but the most common non-engine enquiry on the L550 — power transfer unit wear on pre-facelift cars, often misdiagnosed as driveline vibration.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	6
	Si4 GTDi Timing & Cooling Issues
	The Ford-derived petrol's age-related weak points — worth a specialist check on any surviving 2015–2017 petrol car.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

SECTION 7B: EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every Discovery Sport Buyer
"If you buy one Discovery Sport based on this page, buy a 2020-or-later car. The 2019.5 facelift is the dividing line between this model's two reliability lives — and on a pre-facelift diesel, a documented timing-chain inspection is worth more than any service stamp."

The economics box (gold callout):
The Discovery Sport rule: A £3,500–£5,800 recon against a £10,000+ car clears our 60% scrap-threshold rule comfortably — replacement is ✅ usually safe. The exception is the cheapest 2015–2016 diesels now trading at £7,000–£9,000: there, a top-end recon quote brushes the threshold, and a chain-damaged engine on a tired car is where we'll honestly tell you to walk away.

SECTION 9: HOW THE DISCOVERY SPORT ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the Discovery Sport Engine Range Evolved — 2015 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why Land Rover Changed
	Worth Knowing
	Launch era
	2015
	224DT, Si4 GTDi
	The Freelander's replacement launched on borrowed engines — Ford-derived diesel and petrol as a bridge
	The 2.2 lasted barely a year in the range; survivors are now the cheapest Discovery Sports on sale
	Ingenium era
	2015–2019
	204DTD, 204PT (2017+)
	JLR's first in-house modular engine family arrived here first — the Discovery Sport was Ingenium's proving ground
	Being first meant inheriting the early chain-tensioner hardware; this era defines the model's used-market reputation
	Facelift / MHEV era
	2019.5–present
	204DTD MHEV, 204PT, AJ20P3 P300e
	Emissions targets drove electrification — mild-hybrid diesels, then the three-cylinder PHEV
	The same facelift quietly brought the revised chain hardware — the reliability story changed more than the styling did
	________________


SECTION 10: WHO SHOULD BUY A DISCOVERY SPORT? — <WhoShouldBuy> (5 profile cards)
H2: Who Should Buy a Discovery Sport?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Family Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The affordable 5+2-seat Land Rover — exactly what it was built for. 2020+ D165/D180 is the family sweet spot.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	Depends entirely on the journey: motorway miles suit the diesel; short urban runs are how DPFs clog and oil dilutes. Under 10 miles a day? P250 or P300e.
	Towing & Working Use
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	2,000–2,500kg capacity — fine for trailers and small caravans, but this is the point in the range where the full-size Discovery earns its money.
	Budget Owner
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	Cheap early diesels look tempting; budget for a chain inspection before purchase, not after. A £250 inspection against a £5,000 engine is the best odds on this page.
	First-Time Land Rover Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The gateway model — approachable costs, huge specialist coverage, and every mistake already documented. Start post-facelift and it's a genuinely easy ownership entry.
	________________

SECTION 13: DISCOVERY SPORT FAQ — <FAQAccordion> (8 questions)
H2: Discovery Sport Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a Discovery Sport engine replacement cost in the UK?
 A reconditioned Discovery Sport engine typically costs £2,800–£5,800 supply only [EM-QUOTE], with the volume 204DTD diesel at £3,500–£5,800. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which Discovery Sport engine is the most reliable?
 The 204PT Ingenium petrol and post-2019 204DTD diesels. The 2019.5 facelift's revised chain hardware materially changed the diesel's reliability profile.
3. Which Discovery Sport engine should I avoid?
 No engine outright — but a pre-2019 204DTD diesel without a documented timing-chain inspection is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The engine is fine; the unknown is not.
4. How do I know if my Discovery Sport timing chain is worn?
 Cold-start rattle for the first few seconds is the classic early warning, sometimes with a chain-noise change under load. Caught early it's a repair; ignored, it's a £3,500–£5,800 engine [EM-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a Discovery Sport engine?
 Usually yes — £3,500–£5,800 against typical £10,000+ values clears our 60% scrap-threshold rule. The honest exception is the cheapest 2015–2016 cars, where a worst-case quote brushes the threshold. Use the Ownership Decision Tool for your car.
6. Which year Discovery Sport should I buy?
 2020 or later. You get the facelift architecture, mild-hybrid diesels with revised chain hardware, and the P300e option for short-run drivers. Among pre-facelift cars, insist on chain-inspection paperwork.
7. How do I find my Discovery Sport engine code?
 Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; the block stamping is accessible from below on the Ingenium units. Or tell us your variant (TD4, D180, P250 etc.) and we'll identify it.
8. Discovery Sport vs Evoque — which is more reliable?
 Mechanically they're siblings — same platform family, same Ingenium engines, same pre/post-2019 story. The Discovery Sport adds the 5+2 seats; reliability differences are trim-deep. Buy on year and history, not badge. Read the full head-to-head →
________________

SECTION 4: GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid> (2 cards + era-comparison block)
H2: Freelander Generations — Find Your Vehicle
Sub-headline: Two generations with almost nothing in common under the bonnet. Select yours.
Freelander 1
 1997–2006 • 1.8 K-series, 2.0 Di (L-series), Td4 (BMW M47), 2.5 KV6
⭐⭐½
Our Verdict: Britain's best-selling 4x4 of its day, undone by the 1.8 petrol's head gasket. The BMW-engined Td4 diesel is the one to have — the rest are now labour-of-love territory.
 Explore Freelander 1 →
Freelander 2 (L359) 🔥 Most Enquired — 279 enquiries (2025) [EM-VERIFIED]
 2006–2014 • 2.2 TD4/SD4 (224DT), 3.2 i6 (SI6), 2.0 Si4 GTDi
⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
Our Verdict: A quietly excellent car — the 2.2 diesel is one of the most dependable engines Land Rover ever fitted. The buying risk is the car's age and value, not the engine.
 Explore Freelander 2 →
Freelander 1 vs Freelander 2 — Different Cars, Different Advice
The Freelander 1 is now a sub-£2,500 car where almost any engine failure is an economic write-off — our advice is head-gasket prevention (K-series) or a good used engine, never a full recon. The Freelander 2 is the last truly affordable dependable Land Rover: values of £3,000–£7,000 can still justify engine work, but only just. Read the maths before the mechanicals.
Read the full comparison: Freelander 1 vs Freelander 2 →

How confident are these ratings? 224DT rating: 90% confidence — 279 model-level L359 enquiries [EM-VERIFIED] plus its long, well-documented service history across Freelander 2, Evoque L538 and early Discovery Sport. All Freelander 1 and petrol FL2 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — the parc is old enough that documented failure patterns are extensive, but our proprietary volume data doesn't reach back that far.
Data note (internal): Correction applied: the source data sheet listed "2.0 Ingenium (204DTD — late L359)" as a Freelander engine. The Freelander never received an Ingenium engine — L359 production ended in 2014 and the 204DTD launched in 2015 with the Discovery Sport/Evoque. Removed, and the missing genuine engines added: 1.8 K-series, L-series, BMW Td4 and KV6 (FL1); Volvo SI6 and Si4 GTDi petrols (FL2). If the source data contains "204DTD Freelander" enquiries, they are almost certainly misattributed Discovery Sport records — worth cleaning at source.

SECTION 6: COMMON FREELANDER ENGINE PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems> (6 cards)
H2: Common Freelander Engine Problems
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	1.8 K-series Head Gasket Failure
	The failure that defined the Freelander 1. Coolant loss, overheating, mayonnaise under the oil cap. A quality multi-layer-steel gasket and revised rail fix it permanently — prevention beats replacement on this engine every time.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	2
	224DT Injector Failure
	The Freelander 2 diesel's most common failure enquiry [EM-VERIFIED]. Rough running, smoke, uneven idle. Repairable at sensible cost if addressed before bore wash sets in.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	3
	224DT DPF Blocking
	Urban-use Freelander 2 diesels clog DPFs — repeated regenerations dilute the oil and mask deeper issues. A motorway run is maintenance on this engine.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	4
	Td4 (BMW M47) Turbo & Intercooler Issues
	The Freelander 1 diesel's known weak points — split intercooler hoses mimic turbo failure; genuine turbo wear appears at high mileage. Usually repairable; rarely worth a full engine.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	5
	KV6 Cambelt & Head Gasket Failure
	Three belts, tight service access, and head gaskets of its era. Belt neglect is terminal — and on today's values, terminal means scrap.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	6
	Haldex / Rear Diff Wear (FL2)
	Not an engine failure, but the most common non-engine enquiry on the L359 — driveline vibration and binding often misdiagnosed as engine or gearbox trouble.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

* Freelander 2 enquiries skew toward repair-vs-replace decisions rather than pre-purchase checks — the signature of a model at the bottom of its value curve, where every engine issue is an economic crossroads. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* We expect used-engine demand to outstrip recon demand on this model — host values simply can't carry full reconditioning costs. (Editorial synthesis.)

"If you own a Freelander 2 with a tired engine, price a good used 2.2 before you price a recon — on this model the maths usually points there. And if you own a 1.8 Freelander 1 that hasn't had the head gasket done properly: do it before it does you."

The economics box (gold callout):
The Freelander rule — the inverse of the Defender: On most Freelanders, a full recon fails our 60% scrap-threshold rule: £3,500 of engine in a £4,000 car is not a repair, it's a donation. The honest hierarchy on this model is (1) repair the fault, (2) fit a good used engine, (3) recon only on the best late SD4s, (4) walk away with our blessing. We'll tell you which applies to yours — for free.

SECTION 9: HOW THE FREELANDER ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the Freelander Engine Range Evolved — 1997 to 2014
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why Land Rover Changed
	Worth Knowing
	Rover era
	1997–2000
	1.8 K-series, 2.0 Di L-series
	The Freelander was built from the Rover Group parts bin — light, cheap, and the K-series' cooling margins didn't survive 4x4 duty
	The L-series diesel is the forgotten good engine of early Freelanders
	BMW era
	2000–2006
	Td4 (M47R), 2.5 KV6
	BMW ownership brought a properly engineered diesel — the M47 transformed the Freelander's credibility
	The Td4 is the only Freelander 1 we'd still recommend today
	Ford era
	2006–2014
	224DT, SI6 3.2, Si4 GTDi
	Ford ownership rebuilt the Freelander from scratch on the EUCD platform — Duratorq diesel, Volvo straight-six petrol
	The 2.2 Duratorq went on to power the Evoque and launch-era Discovery Sport — its parts network is enormous
	Succession
	2014→
	—
	The Freelander name retired; the Discovery Sport took its place and its customer
	Early Discovery Sports even kept the Freelander's 2.2 engine for the first year
	________________


SECTION 10: WHO SHOULD BUY A FREELANDER? — <WhoShouldBuy> (5 profile cards)
H2: Who Should Buy a Freelander?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Family Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	A late Freelander 2 SD4 is honest, spacious family transport at banger money — go in with a £1,000 contingency fund and eyes open.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	Motorway commuters suit the 2.2 diesel well; short urban runs are DPF poison. Town-only drivers should consider the SI6 petrol despite the fuel bills.
	Towing & Working Use
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	2,000kg braked on the FL2 — genuinely useful for trailers and small caravans. This is the cheapest tow-capable Land Rover on the road.
	Budget Owner
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	This is the Freelander's home turf — the cheapest route into Land Rover ownership. The discipline: never spend more on one repair than the car is worth.
	First 4x4 Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	Forgiving to own, cheap to insure, every fault documented a thousand times over. A well-bought Freelander 2 is a great first 4x4; a cheap 1.8 FL1 is a great first lesson.
	________________

SECTION 13: FREELANDER FAQ — <FAQAccordion> (8 questions)
H2: Freelander Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a Freelander engine replacement cost in the UK?
 A used engine typically costs £400–£2,000 supply only; reconditioned units run £1,500–£4,500 depending on engine [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs Table A]. Fitting adds £520–£1,300. On most Freelanders, the used route is the economically sensible one. Use our calculator for a personalised verdict.
2. Which Freelander engine is the most reliable?
 The 2.2 TD4/SD4 (224DT) in the Freelander 2 — one of the most dependable engines Land Rover ever fitted. On the Freelander 1, the BMW-sourced Td4 is comfortably the pick.
3. Which Freelander engine should I avoid?
 The 1.8 K-series petrol — unless the head gasket has already been done properly with a multi-layer-steel gasket, in which case the hard part is behind it. The 2.5 KV6 demands cambelt discipline that rarely survives banger-money ownership.
4. Is the Freelander head gasket problem fixable?
 Yes, permanently — a quality MLS gasket, revised oil rail and proper coolant bleeding resolve it for good. At £600–£1,200 it's the one big-ticket repair on a Freelander 1 that can still make sense. Prevention beats replacement on this engine.
5. Is it worth replacing a Freelander engine?
 Honestly: often not with a full recon. Most Freelanders fail our 60% scrap-threshold rule for reconditioned engines — but a good used 2.2 at £800–£2,000 in a solid late Freelander 2 is a sound decision. Use the Ownership Decision Tool for your car.
6. Which Freelander should I buy?
 A 2011–2014 Freelander 2 SD4 with history — facelift car, strongest engine, last of the line. Among Freelander 1s, only the Td4 diesel, and only cheap.
7. How do I find my Freelander engine code?
 On the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stampings vary by engine family. Or tell us your variant and year (TD4, SD4, 1.8, KV6) and we'll identify it.
8. Freelander 2 vs Discovery Sport — should I upgrade?
 The Discovery Sport is the Freelander 2's direct successor — early ones even share the 2.2 engine. Upgrade for the extra seats and refinement; stay if your Freelander 2 is healthy, because a good one owes you nothing. Read the full head-to-head →

SECTION 2: OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: Range Rover — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★½ (3.5/5)
	Replacement Economics
	⚠️ The Widest Swing in the Range — a £6,500 engine is trivial in a £60,000 L405 and terminal in a £6,000 L322. Generation decides everything.
	Reliability
	★★★☆☆ — the engines are mostly better than the reputation; the air suspension and electronics around them earn the reputation
	Most Reliable Engine
	4.4 SDV8 (448DT) — the most dependable big diesel JLR made • BMW M57 Td6 (L322 era)
	Highest-Risk Engine
	3.0 TDV6/SDV6 (306DT) — crankshaft bearing • 3.6 TDV8 (368DT) — timing chain/tensioner
	Best Used Buy
	Late L405 4.4 SDV8 Vogue — flagship luxury, the range's strongest engine
	Best Modern Buy
	L460 D350 — the AJ20D6 six carries none of the old V6 legacy
	Average Engine Replacement
	£7,400 reconditioned, supply only [EM-QUOTE]
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	306DT crankshaft bearing (L405 TDV6) [EM-VERIFIED]
	Homepage Ranking
	👑 Flagship — and home of our Most Dependable Big Engine (4.4 SDV8)
	One-line verdict: The Range Rover is where engine economics get emotional — the same reconditioned V8 that's a rounding error on an L460 invoice is a death sentence for a cheap L322, and knowing which side of that line your car sits on is worth more than any spec sheet.

SECTION 4: GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid> (5 cards)
H2: Range Rover Generations — Find Your Vehicle
Sub-headline: Fifty-plus years, five generations, and five completely different ownership propositions.
Range Rover Classic
 1970–1996 • Rover V8 3.5/3.9/4.2, 200Tdi, 300Tdi, 2.5 VM diesel
⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
Our Verdict: Now a rising classic where engine work is restoration, not repair — values increasingly justify proper rebuilds. The Tdi diesels and well-fed V8s go on forever; the bodywork is the project.
 Explore the Classic →
Range Rover P38A
 1994–2002 • 4.0/4.6 Rover V8, 2.5 BMW diesel
⭐⭐½
Our Verdict: The brave buy. V8 liner and head issues plus period electronics keep values low — which is exactly why a sorted one is the cheapest real Range Rover experience on earth. Enthusiasts only.
 Explore the P38 →
Range Rover L322
 2002–2012 • BMW M62 4.4, BMW M57 Td6, 4.4 AJ-V8, 4.2 SC, 3.6 TDV8 (368DT), 4.4 TDV8 (448DT), 5.0 (AJ133)
⭐⭐⭐☆☆
Our Verdict: The £8,000 Range Rover trap and the £20,000 Range Rover bargain are the same car with different histories. BMW-era engines are the sleeper choice; budget for the air suspension either way.
 Explore the L322 →
Range Rover L405 🔥 Most Enquired — 284 enquiries (2025) [EM-VERIFIED]
 2012–2021 • 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 (306DT), 4.4 SDV8 (448DT), 5.0 SC (508PS/AJ133), P400e (204PT), D300/D350 (AJ20D6, 2020+)
⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
Our Verdict: The modern sweet spot — aluminium body, real depreciation absorbed, and the SDV8 at its peak. The TDV6's crank-bearing question is the one cloud: buy the V8 or buy the paperwork.
 Explore the L405 →
Range Rover L460
 2022–present • D300/D350 (AJ20D6), P400 (AJ20P6), P440e/P510e PHEV, P530 4.4 V8 (BMW-derived)
⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
Our Verdict: Early-build electronics gremlins made headlines; the engines themselves are proving strong. At these values, no engine bill will ever total the car — the safest replacement economics in the entire JLR range.
 Explore the L460 →

	Confidence Score panel:
How confident are these ratings? 306DT, 368DT and 448DT ratings: 93% confidence — high-volume engines in our dataset [EM-VERIFIED] with deep specialist rebuild history. Classic, P38 and BMW-era L322 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — decades of documented patterns, no proprietary volume data. L460 engines: Provisional — the parc is too young to rate with volume data; ratings reflect early patterns and shared-architecture evidence.
Data note (internal) — TWO CORRECTIONS APPLIED, verify at source:
1. "508PS" is not the 4.4 SDV8. In JLR coding, 508PS is the 5.0 supercharged petrol (AJ133 family; 508PN = naturally aspirated). The 4.4 SDV8 diesel is 448DT. The source sheets and homepage both label the SDV8 as "508PS" — the reliability claim about the SDV8 is right, the code is wrong. The 620 enquiries recorded against "508PS" almost certainly belong to the 448DT SDV8 and are shown there pending verification. Correct this across the homepage, Discovery legacy sheet and Table A before launch — it's the exact kind of error a JLR specialist would spot instantly.
2. AJ20D6 split applied (as on Defender/Discovery): late-L405 and L460 D300/D350 are the Ingenium six, not the 306DT V6.
________________


SECTION 6: COMMON RANGE ROVER ENGINE PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems> (6 cards)
H2: Common Range Rover Engine Problems
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	306DT Crankshaft Bearing Failure
	The L405 TDV6/SDV6's defining risk — cold-start knock is the warning, and once the bottom end lets go there's rarely anything to rebuild. £6,500–£9,500 recon [EM-QUOTE].
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	2
	368DT Timing Chain & Tensioner Failure
	The 3.6 TDV8's known weakness — chain and guide wear with snap risk on neglected oil intervals. Affects L322 2006–2010.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	3
	AJ133 5.0 Supercharger & Chain Guide Wear
	Supercharger nose bearing whine plus timing chain guide wear on early 5.0s — noisy warning, expensive silence. Affects L322/L405 petrols.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	4
	P38 Rover V8 Liner & Head Issues
	Slipped cylinder liners and porous blocks on the 4.0/4.6 — the failure that halved P38 values. Top-hat liner rebuilds fix it permanently.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	5
	Air Suspension Compressor Failure
	Every generation from Classic LSE onward; compressor burnout presents a fire risk. The most common non-engine enquiry across all Range Rovers.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	6
	Ingenium Oil Dilution & PHEV Neglect
	Short-run D300/D350s dilute oil through interrupted DPF regens; P400e engines that never run suffer their own neglect patterns. Modern problems for modern Range Rovers.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

The economics box (gold callout):
The Range Rover rule — generation is destiny: The same £8,000–£12,000 SDV8 recon is 15% of a nice L405's value (✅ Safe), 60%+ of a tired L322's (⚠️ threshold), and more than a whole P38 (❌ walk away, or buy a used engine). Before any Range Rover engine decision, value the car first — we'll do it with you for free.

SECTION 9: HOW THE RANGE ROVER ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the Range Rover Engine Range Evolved — 1970 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why Land Rover Changed
	Worth Knowing
	Rover V8 era
	1970–1996
	Rover V8, VM diesel, Tdi
	The Buick-derived V8 defined the car; diesels arrived only when fuel prices demanded them
	The Classic is now the only Range Rover whose engine work is an investment
	BMW era
	1994–2006
	P38 V8s, M51, M62, M57
	BMW ownership brought German engines — the M57 Td6 remains one of the most dependable diesels ever fitted to a Range Rover
	The sleeper knowledge: early L322s are more BMW than Land Rover, and better for it
	Ford era
	2005–2012
	AJ-V8, 368DT, 448DT, AJ133
	Ford brought Jaguar's V8s and the joint-venture TDV8s — huge performance gains, new failure modes
	The 368DT's chain issue and the 448DT's near-flawless record are one lesson apart
	JLR independence
	2012–2021
	306DT, 448DT SDV8, 508PS, 204PT P400e
	The aluminium L405 demanded efficiency — the V6 diesel carried the volume and the crank-bearing risk with it
	The SDV8 outlived the SDV6's reputation for a reason
	Ingenium & BMW's return
	2020–present
	AJ20D6, AJ20P6, P530
	Electrification retired the old diesels; for the V8, JLR went back to BMW — full circle after 25 years
	The cleanest engine slate since the Classic, at the highest prices in the car's history
	________________


SECTION 10: WHO SHOULD BUY A RANGE ROVER? — <WhoShouldBuy> (5 profile cards)
H2: Who Should Buy a Range Rover?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Family Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	An L405 is a magnificent family flagship — budget running costs like a second mortgage payment and it will never disappoint.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐☆☆☆
	The wrong tool. Short runs punish the diesels' DPFs and the PHEVs only make sense with a driveway charger and discipline.
	Towing & Working Use
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	3,500kg with imperious ease — the SDV8 L405 may be the finest tow car ever sold in Britain.
	Budget Owner
	⭐☆☆☆☆
	The most dangerous badge on this site for a tight budget. A cheap L322 or P38 is not a cheap Range Rover — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody.
	Collector / Keeper
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	Classics are appreciating assets worth proper engine investment; a cherished SDV8 L405 is a future classic being written now.

SECTION 13: RANGE ROVER FAQ — <FAQAccordion> (8 questions)
H2: Range Rover Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a Range Rover engine replacement cost in the UK?
 Reconditioned units run £2,500–£14,000 supply only depending on generation and engine [EM-QUOTE] — from a Classic Tdi at £2,500–£4,500 to the L460's P530 V8 at £9,000–£14,000. Fitting adds £650–£2,100. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which Range Rover engine is the most reliable?
 The 4.4 SDV8 (448DT) — the lowest failure-enquiry rate of any big JLR engine in our data despite the highest replacement cost. From the BMW era, the M57 Td6 deserves the same reputation.
3. Which Range Rover engine should I avoid?
 The 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 (306DT) without documented oil-interval history — its crankshaft-bearing failure leaves nothing to rebuild. The 3.6 TDV8 (368DT) needs its timing-chain history evidenced. Unrebuild P38 V8s carry liner risk priced into every listing.
4. How do I know if my Range Rover engine needs replacing?
 By engine: cold-start knock (306DT), timing chain rattle (368DT, AJ133), supercharger whine (5.0 SC), coolant loss and overheating (P38 V8). Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a Range Rover engine?
 Entirely generation-dependent. L405/L460: almost always ✅. L322: run the maths — a £6,000 car cannot carry an £8,000 engine ⚠️. P38/older: used engine or specialist rebuild as restoration, not repair. Use the Ownership Decision Tool for your car.
6. Which Range Rover should I buy?
 Late L405 SDV8 for the sweet spot; L460 D350 if the budget is new-car sized; a sorted BMW-era L322 Td6 for the informed bargain; a Classic if your heart has already decided.
7. How do I find my Range Rover engine code?
 On the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stampings vary by era. Or tell us your generation and variant (SDV6, SDV8, D350, P530) and we'll identify it.
8. Range Rover vs Range Rover Sport — which is more reliable?
 Mechanically they're siblings — shared platforms and engines mean shared strengths and shared risks. The Sport's harder driven life shows in our enquiry patterns; the full-size car's extra weight works everything else harder. Buy on engine and history, not badge. Read the full head-to-head →
________________

SECTION 4: GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid> (2 cards + era block)
H2: Evoque Generations — Find Your Vehicle
Sub-headline: Two generations — and inside the first one, two very different engine eras. Select yours.
Evoque L538
 2011–2018 • 2.2 eD4/TD4/SD4 (224DT), Si4 GTDi, 204DTD (2015+), 204PT (2017+)
⭐⭐⭐☆☆
Our Verdict: The car that made Range Rover a fashion brand. Early 2.2 diesels are honest and well understood; 2015–2018 Ingenium diesels are fine engines wearing the chain question — inspection paperwork decides the deal.
 Explore the L538 →
Evoque L551 🔥 Most Enquired — 349 + 148 PHEV enquiries (2025) [EM-VERIFIED]
 2019–present • 204DTD MHEV (D150–D200), 204PT (P200–P300), P300e (AJ20P3)
⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
Our Verdict: The grown-up Evoque — post-improvement Ingenium engines from day one, mild-hybrid drivetrains, and the P300e for urban lives. The lowest-caveat first Range Rover money buys.
 Explore the L551 →
The Line That Matters: 2019
Every L551 was built after the Ingenium chain-hardware improvements — the caveat that follows the L538's 2015–2018 diesels simply doesn't apply. If you're comparing a £13,000 2017 L538 against a £17,000 2019 L551, the £4,000 gap is buying you more than a newer badge: it's buying out the inspection anxiety entirely.
Read the full comparison: Evoque L538 vs L551 →

SECTION 6: COMMON EVOQUE ENGINE PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems> (6 cards)
H2: Common Evoque Engine Problems
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	204DTD Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
	The Evoque's defining used-market question and our #1 enquiry driver on this model [EM-VERIFIED]. Cold-start rattle is the warning. Applies to 2015–2018 L538 diesels; L551s carry the improved hardware. £3,500–£5,800 recon [EM-QUOTE].
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	2
	Ingenium Diesel Oil Dilution (DPF regen)
	The urban-Evoque problem: short school-run journeys interrupt DPF regens, fuel dilutes the oil, wear accelerates. If your Evoque never sees a motorway, you want the P200 or P300e.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	3
	224DT Injector & DPF Issues
	The early L538 diesel's known weak points — well understood after a decade of Freelander 2 and Evoque service life, and moderately priced to fix.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	4
	204DTD EGR Cooler Failure
	Coolant loss and rough running on higher-mileage Ingenium diesels — repairable if caught before overheating threatens the head.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	5
	PTU / Rear Drive Unit Wear
	Not an engine failure, but the L538's most common non-engine enquiry — power transfer unit wear presenting as driveline vibration, shared with its Discovery Sport sibling.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	6
	Si4 GTDi Timing & Cooling Issues
	The Ford-derived petrol's age-related weak points — a specialist check item on any surviving 2011–2017 petrol L538.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The Evoque family generates more engine enquiries than any other model line we track — 741 in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED] — yet its typical repair bill is among the smallest. Britain's biggest engine-enquiry model isn't the one with the worst engines; it's the one with the most first-time premium owners meeting their first warning light.
* The 148-enquiry PHEV cohort [EM-VERIFIED] is the earliest large-scale signal anyone in the UK has on plug-in hybrid engine longevity — and so far it skews toward charging and software questions, not mechanical failures. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* L538 enquiries cluster on 2015–2017 diesels around 70,000–100,000 miles — the chain-anxiety zone, arriving exactly on schedule. (Editorial synthesis.)

The economics box (gold callout):
The Evoque rule — the generation split: Every L551 clears our 60% scrap-threshold rule with room to spare — ✅ Safe. The pressure sits on the cheapest L538s: a £5,800 worst-case recon against a £7,500 2015 diesel is 77% — ❌ past the line, and a good used engine (£2,000–£3,500) or walking away becomes the honest answer. On a £12,000+ L538, replacement is back in play. Two model years and £4,000 of value separate 'always fix it' from 'run the maths first.'

SECTION 10: WHO SHOULD BUY AN EVOQUE? — <WhoShouldBuy> (5 profile cards)
H2: Who Should Buy an Evoque?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	First-Time Premium Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	This is the Evoque's home turf — the most approachable route into the badge, with every known issue documented and priced. Start with a 2020+ P200 and it's a genuinely easy first Range Rover.
	Family Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	Stylish and safe, but compact — fine for one child, tight for two with kit. If boot space is a weekly argument, the Discovery Sport is the same money and mechanicals with more car.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The P300e is the best urban-commuter Range Rover made — 30+ electric miles covers most British commutes. Diesel Evoques belong on motorway commutes only.
	Towing & Working Use
	⭐⭐☆☆☆
	1,600–2,000kg braked — a small trailer, not a caravan habit. Buyers with towing ambitions should read our Discovery Sport page instead.
	Budget Owner
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	Early 2.2 L538s are honest cheap entries; 2015–2018 Ingenium diesels are the caveat zone — inspection first, always. Never spend recon money on a sub-£8,000 car without running our maths.

SECTION 13: EVOQUE FAQ — <FAQAccordion> (8 questions)
H2: Evoque Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does an Evoque engine replacement cost in the UK?
 A reconditioned Evoque engine typically costs £2,800–£5,800 supply only [EM-QUOTE], with the volume 204DTD diesel at £3,500–£5,800. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which Evoque engine is the most reliable?
 The 204PT petrol and post-2019 204DTD diesels — the L551 carries the improved Ingenium hardware from day one. Early P300e evidence is strong too, on the largest PHEV dataset in the UK [EM-VERIFIED].
3. Which Evoque engine should I avoid?
 None outright — but a 2015–2018 Ingenium diesel without a documented timing-chain inspection is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The engine is fine; the unknown is not.
4. Do Evoques have timing chain problems?
 The 2015–2018 L538 Ingenium diesels carry the known tensioner-wear caveat — cold-start rattle is the warning. Every L551 (2019+) was built after the hardware improvements. This distinction is the single most important fact in the used Evoque market. Hearing the rattle? Use our diagnostic calculator.
5. Is it worth replacing an Evoque engine?
 On any L551 — yes, comfortably within our 60% scrap-threshold rule. On L538s it's value-dependent: above ~£10,000, usually yes; on the cheapest cars a used engine (£1,500–£3,500) or walking away is the honest answer. Use the Ownership Decision Tool for your car.
6. Which Evoque should I buy?
 A 2020-or-later L551 — P200 for town, D180/D200 for motorway miles, P300e for short-run urban lives. Among L538s, an early 2.2 with history or a 2015+ diesel with chain paperwork.
7. How do I find my Evoque engine code?
 On the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The year does most of the work: 2.2 badges to 2015 = 224DT; 2.0 diesels from 2015 = 204DTD; P300e = the 1.5 three-cylinder AJ20P3. Or tell us your variant and year and we'll identify it.
8. Evoque vs Discovery Sport — which is more reliable?
 Mechanically they're siblings — same platform family, same engines, same pre/post-2019 story. The Discovery Sport adds the 5+2 seats; the Evoque adds the style. Buy on year and history, not badge. Read the full head-to-head →

SECTION 4: GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid> (3 cards)
H2: Range Rover Sport Generations — Find Your Vehicle
Sub-headline: Three generations, and the value curve decides as much as the engine does.
Range Rover Sport L320
 2005–2013 • 2.7 TDV6 (276DT), 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 (306DT), 3.6 TDV8 (368DT), 4.2 SC / 4.4 AJ-V8, 5.0 SC (AJ133)
⭐⭐½
Our Verdict: Now the bargain-bin Range Rover — and the danger zone. Values have fallen to where one V6 diesel failure writes the car off. The TDV8 is the survivor's choice; any 2.7 without oil-pump history is a gamble, not a purchase.
 Explore the L320 →
Range Rover Sport L494 🔥 Most Enquired — 308 enquiries (2025) [EM-VERIFIED]
 2013–2022 • 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 (306DT), 4.4 SDV8 (448DT), 3.0 SC V6 (AJ126), 5.0 SC (AJ133/SVR), P400e (204PT), P400 (AJ20P6), D300/D350 (AJ20D6, 2020+)
⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
Our Verdict: The definitive Sport — aluminium body, huge choice, real depreciation absorbed. Buy the SDV8 or a 2020+ D350 and it's excellent; buy a high-mileage SDV6 without oil-interval paperwork and you've bought the range's most common £9,500 phone call.
 Explore the L494 →
Range Rover Sport L461
 2022–present • D300/D350 (AJ20D6), P400 (AJ20P6), P440e/P510e PHEV, P530 V8 (BMW-derived), SV P635
⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
Our Verdict: The cleanest engine slate a Sport has ever had — no V6 diesel legacy, warranty cover, and values that make any engine bill economically trivial. Early-build electronics niggles, not engines, dominate its enquiries.
 Explore the L461 →

SECTION 6: COMMON RANGE ROVER SPORT ENGINE PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems> (6 cards)
H2: Common Range Rover Sport Engine Problems
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	306DT Crankshaft Bearing Failure
	The Sport's most common catastrophic enquiry [EM-VERIFIED] — and Sports show it earlier in our patterns than gentler-driven Discoverys. Cold-start knock is the warning; £6,500–£9,500 recon the ending [EM-QUOTE]. L320 (2010+) and L494.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	2
	276DT Oil Pump Housing Failure
	Kills the 2.7 outright with little warning — on today's L320 values, it usually kills the car too. 2005–2011.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	3
	368DT Timing Chain & Tensioner Failure
	The 3.6 TDV8's chain and guide wear with snap risk — oil-interval discipline is everything. L320 2007–2010.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	4
	AJ133 Supercharger & Chain Guide Wear
	Supercharger nose whine and timing chain guide wear on the 5.0 — SVRs live the hardest lives and show it soonest.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	5
	Air Suspension Compressor Failure
	Endemic across L320 and L494; compressor burnout presents a fire risk. The most common non-engine enquiry on this model.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	6
	Ingenium Oil Dilution & PHEV Neglect
	Short-run D300/D350s dilute oil through interrupted DPF regens; P400e engines that rarely fire develop their own neglect patterns.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: All three Sport generations rank among our most-enquired models simultaneously [EM-VERIFIED] — no other Land Rover family does that. The Sport generates engine decisions at every price point from £5,000 to £120,000 at once.
* L320 enquiries mirror the Freelander pattern — repair-vs-scrap crossroads — while L494 enquiries mirror the Discovery 4: failure-driven, high-stakes, paperwork-dependent. One model page, two completely different reader mindsets. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* Sport-fitted 306DT engines appear in our failure enquiries at lower mileages than the same engine in the Discovery — consistent with harder use, and the reason we weight service history even more heavily on this model. (Editorial synthesis.)

The economics box (gold callout):
The Sport rule — the tightest maths in the range: A £6,500–£9,500 SDV6 recon fails our 60% scrap-threshold rule on almost every L320 and brushes it on the cheapest early L494s. This is the model where we most often quote three numbers, not one: the recon, the used engine (£3,500–£5,500), and the car's value — because the right answer changes at £12,000, £18,000 and £25,000. Never commit to a Sport engine without all three.

SECTION 9: HOW THE RANGE ROVER SPORT ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the Range Rover Sport Engine Range Evolved — 2005 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why Land Rover Changed
	Worth Knowing
	Discovery-based era
	2005–2013
	276DT, 368DT, 306DT, AJ-V8, AJ133
	The first Sport was a Discovery 3 in a sharper suit — it inherited the L319's engines and, with them, the oil-pump and chain stories
	'Sport' was marketing: the L320 weighed over 2.5 tonnes and worked its V6 hard
	Aluminium era
	2013–2020
	306DT, 448DT, AJ126, AJ133 SVR, 204PT
	The L494 shed 400kg+ with the full-size car's aluminium body — the SDV8 arrived and the SVR made it a genuine performance brand
	Same 306DT, lighter car, harder driving: the crank-bearing maths didn't improve as much as the badge suggests
	Ingenium transition
	2019–2022
	AJ20P6, AJ20D6
	Emissions rules retired the Ford-era V6/V8 diesels — the straight-six Ingeniums closed out the L494 with its cleanest engines
	A 2020–2022 L494 D350 quietly became the pick of the entire generation
	L461 era
	2022–present
	AJ20D6, AJ20P6, PHEVs, P530/SV
	Full electrification path — MHEV sixes, big-battery PHEVs, and a BMW-sourced V8 for the flagship
	The first Sport with no high-risk engine anywhere in the range
	________________


SECTION 10: WHO SHOULD BUY A RANGE ROVER SPORT? — <WhoShouldBuy> (5 profile cards)
H2: Who Should Buy a Range Rover Sport?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Family Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	An L494 is a superb family car with genuine pace — the full-size Range Rover's substance at a real discount. Buy the engine, not the wheels.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐☆☆☆
	Short runs are how Sport diesels die — DPF regens interrupted, oil diluted, crank bearings worked cold. Motorway commuters only; town commuters want the P400e or P440e.
	Towing & Working Use
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	3,500kg with the SDV8 or D350 barely noticing — among the best tow cars money can buy.
	Budget Owner
	⭐☆☆☆☆
	The single riskiest budget buy on this site. A £9,000 L320 carries engines whose failure costs more than the car — if the fund can't absorb a total loss, it can't absorb this car.
	Performance Enthusiast
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The SVR is a genuine event and the AJ133 is stronger than its whine suggests — budget supercharger and chain-guide attention as a running cost, not a surprise.
	________________

SECTION 13: RANGE ROVER SPORT FAQ — <FAQAccordion> (8 questions)
H2: Range Rover Sport Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a Range Rover Sport engine replacement cost in the UK?
 Reconditioned units run £3,000–£16,000 supply only depending on generation and engine [EM-QUOTE] — the volume SDV6 sits at £6,500–£9,500, the SDV8 at £8,000–£12,000. Fitting adds £780–£2,100. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which Range Rover Sport engine is the most reliable?
 The 4.4 SDV8 (448DT) — the lowest failure-enquiry rate of any big engine in our data. Among current engines, the AJ20D6 D300/D350 is proving similarly strong.
3. Which Range Rover Sport engine should I avoid?
 The 2.7 TDV6 (276DT) without oil-pump history and the 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 (306DT) without documented oil intervals. Both fail catastrophically; on L320 values, both usually total the car.
4. Is the Range Rover Sport SDV6 engine reliable?
 It's the range's defining risk: crankshaft bearing failure at higher mileages, announced by cold-start knock. Sports show it earlier than Discoverys in our patterns — harder lives. Impeccable oil history changes the odds dramatically. Use our diagnostic calculator if yours is knocking.
5. Is it worth replacing a Range Rover Sport engine?
 Generation decides. L461: always ✅. L494: usually ✅, marginal on the cheapest early cars ⚠️. L320: frequently ❌ for a recon — a used engine or walking away is often the honest answer. Use the Ownership Decision Tool for your car.
6. Which Range Rover Sport should I buy?
 A late L494 SDV8 or 2021+ D350 for the sweet spot; an L461 D350 if buying nearly new; an L320 TDV8 only as an informed enthusiast purchase with a contingency fund.
7. How do I find my Range Rover Sport engine code?
 On the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stampings vary by engine family. Or tell us your variant and year (SDV6, SDV8, D350, P530) and we'll identify it.
8. Range Rover Sport vs Range Rover — which is more reliable?
 Same engines, different lives. The Sport is driven harder and our failure enquiries reflect it — same failure modes, earlier mileages. The full-size car costs more to buy and less per surprise. Buy on engine and history, not badge. Read the full head-to-head →
________________

	The D300 Question — Same Badge, Two Engines
The pre-facelift D300 is the 306DT V6 — the engine with the crankshaft-bearing history it shares with the Discovery 4 and Range Rover Sport. The 2021+ D300 is the AJ20D6 straight-six — no such legacy. Two listings, identical badges, opposite risk profiles. Check the registration year before the price.
Read the full explainer: Velar D300 — V6 vs Straight-Six →

SECTION 6: COMMON VELAR ENGINE PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems> (6 cards)
H2: Common Velar Engine Problems
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	D300 (306DT) Crankshaft Bearing Failure
	The pre-2021 D300 carries the same bottom-end risk as its Discovery 4 and Range Rover Sport siblings — cold-start knock is the warning. £6,500–£9,500 recon [EM-QUOTE]. 2017–2020 cars only.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	2
	204DTD Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
	Pre-2019 Ingenium diesel chain wear — cold-start rattle is the tell. Improved from 2019. Affects early D180/D240.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	3
	Ingenium Diesel Oil Dilution (DPF regen)
	The style-buy problem: Velars bought for the school run interrupt DPF regens, fuel dilutes the oil, wear accelerates. Urban drivers belong in the petrols or PHEV.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	4
	204DTD EGR Cooler Failure
	Coolant loss and rough running on higher-mileage Ingenium diesels — repairable if caught before overheating.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	5
	AJ126 Supercharger & Cooling Wear
	The P380's supercharger nose and cooling-system age points — a specialist check item on any pre-facelift performance car.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	6
	Electrical & Battery Drain Issues
	Not an engine failure, but the Velar's most common non-engine enquiry — parasitic drain and infotainment glitches on pre-Pivi cars, occasionally misread as engine faults.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: Nearly half of all Velar engine enquiries name one variant — the D300 [EM-VERIFIED]. No other JLR model concentrates its engine anxiety on a single badge like this, and the badge covers two different engines. That confusion is the Velar engine market.
* Velar enquiries skew later-mileage and diesel-heavy relative to the petrol-rich parc — consistent with the diesels doing the working miles while the petrols do the style miles. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* Post-2021 cars barely register in failure enquiries yet — early evidence the facelift's clean engine slate is holding. (Editorial synthesis.)

The economics box (gold callout):
The Velar rule: With values of £18,000–£60,000, replacement clears our 60% scrap-threshold rule across essentially the whole parc — ✅ Safe. 	204DTD, 204PT, 306DT D300, AJ126 P380
	The Velar launched as the design flagship on borrowed engines — the Ingenium fours plus the ageing V6s from across the range
	It inherited every engine's existing reputation on day one, good and bad

	A short-run 5.0 V8 halo car — the SVAutobiography Dynamic

	AJ20D6, AJ20P6, P400e, 204DTD MHEV
	Electrification retired the V6s — the straight-sixes and PHEV arrived with Pivi Pro

SECTION 10: WHO SHOULD BUY A VELAR? — <WhoShouldBuy> (5 profile cards)
H2: Who Should Buy a Velar?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Family Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	Five seats, no more — it's the style choice, not the space one. If you need seven, the Discovery costs the same used.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The P250 is the honest commuter Velar; the P400e is the company-car sweet spot. Diesel commuting under 15 miles a day is DPF roulette.
	Towing & Working Use
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	2,400–2,500kg braked on the sixes — capable, but this body was styled, not built, for the yard. D300 (either era) is the tow pick.
	Budget Owner
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	Early D180s are the affordable entry — budget a chain inspection before purchase and they're a sound buy. Cheap pre-2021 D300s: run the crank maths first.
	Design-Led Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The reason this car exists. Buy the 2021+ facelift, pick the engine with the table above, and enjoy the best-looking Range Rover of its era with a clean mechanical conscience.

SECTION 13: VELAR FAQ — <FAQAccordion> (8 questions)
H2: Velar Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a Velar engine replacement cost in the UK?
 Reconditioned units run £3,000–£9,500 supply only depending on engine [EM-QUOTE] — the volume 204DTD at £3,500–£5,800, either D300 at £6,500–£9,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,900. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which Velar engine is the most reliable?
 The 204PT petrol has the cleanest record in our data, with the post-2021 AJ20D6 and AJ20P6 sixes close behind on early evidence.
3. Which Velar engine should I avoid?
 A 2017–2020 D300 (306DT V6) without documented oil-interval history — it carries the same crankshaft-bearing risk as its Discovery and Range Rover Sport siblings. A pre-2019 D180 without a chain inspection is the other caveat.
4. Is the Velar D300 reliable?
 Which one? The 2021+ D300 (AJ20D6 straight-six) — yes, genuinely. The 2017–2020 D300 (306DT V6) — only with maintenance history, because of the bottom-end risk. Same badge, two engines. Full explainer →
5. Is it worth replacing a Velar engine?
 Almost always — Velar values comfortably clear our 60% scrap-threshold rule against every recon cost in the range. The only maths worth running twice is a V6 D300 quote against the cheapest early cars. Use the Ownership Decision Tool for your car.
6. Which Velar should I buy?
 A 2021-or-later facelift — every caveated engine left the range at once, and Pivi Pro fixed the infotainment complaints in the same stroke. Use our powertrain chooser above to pick the engine for your miles.
7. How do I find my Velar engine code?
 On the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. On this model the year does most of the work: D300 before 2021 = 306DT V6; from 2021 = AJ20D6. Or tell us your variant and year and we'll identify it.
8. Velar vs Range Rover Sport — which should I buy?
 The Velar is the design pick; the Sport is the capability pick, with more towing, more engines and (used) more risk exposure via the SDV6. Same money buys a newer Velar or an older Sport — on engine risk alone, the newer Velar usually wins. Read the full head-to-head →
________________

BMW 2002
SECTION 1 — HERO — <ModelHero>
Tag Pill: BMW 2002 • 1968–1976 • 1 Generation (E10) • M10, M15, M31
H1: 2002 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
Sub-headline: The engine that created the modern BMW — the M10-powered 2002, from the carburetted 100 hp standard car to the Kugelfischer-injected 130 hp tii and the turbocharged 170 hp Turbo. Real UK restoration costs, known issues, and honest preservation economics for BMW's iconic compact sports saloon. Backed by expert knowledge and specialist consensus [PATTERN DATA].
Trust Strip: 📊 401,947 Produced (1968–1976) [PATTERN DATA] • 🔧 1 Generation (E10) • 📖 5 Engine Codes • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders
Primary CTA: Explore 2002 Engines →
Data-integrity note (internal): The 2002 is a classic-era vehicle with no proprietary enquiry data available. All information is sourced from BMW Group Classic, specialist publications, and enthusiast records — labelled as [PATTERN DATA] throughout. The 2002's rising classic status means engine work is restoration, not replacement economics.
SECTION 2 — OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: BMW 2002 — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★★★ (4.5/5)
	Replacement Economics
	✅ Restoration, Not Replacement — an M10 rebuild at £4,000–£8,000 against a £10,000–£40,000 car is 10–80% — ⚠️ watch on cheaper cars, ✅ a worthy investment for a well-documented example
	Reliability
	★★★★☆ — the M10 is one of BMW's most durable engines, but age-related issues (cooling system, timing chain, Kugelfischer injection) require specialist attention
	Most Reliable Engine
	M10 — one of BMW's most successful engines, with over 3.5 million produced across many models
	Highest-Risk Engine
	M31 Turbo — the KKK turbocharger and Kugelfischer injection are complex and expensive to restore; only 1,672 built, all LHD
	Best Used Buy
	2002tii with M15 — the fuel-injected tii is the sweet spot: 130 hp, Kugelfischer mechanical injection, and the most desirable non-Turbo 2002
	Best Investment Buy
	2002 Turbo — Europe's first turbocharged production car; only 1,672 built; values are only going one way
	Average Engine Rebuild
	£4,000–£8,000 for a standard M10; £6,000–£12,000+ for tii or Turbo [PATTERN DATA]
	Most Common Restoration Enquiry
	M10 cylinder head rebuild / timing chain replacement / Kugelfischer injection servicing [PATTERN DATA]
	BMW Ranking
	👑 The Car That Saved BMW — the 2002 established BMW's reputation for sporty, driver-focused saloons and paved the way for the 3 Series
	One-line verdict: The 2002 is the car that defined the modern BMW sports saloon — the M10 engine is one of BMW's most durable designs, and the tii and Turbo are the ultimate collectibles. Engine work is restoration, not repair — and on a well-documented example, the values justify every penny spent.

	SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: BMW 2002 — One Generation, One Legend
Sub-headline: The 2002 is the car that created the modern BMW sports saloon — produced from 1968 to 1976, with the M10 engine at its heart. The tii and Turbo are the ultimate collectibles.
________________


BMW 2002 E10 🔥 The Car That Saved BMW — 401,947 produced [PATTERN DATA]
1968–1976 • M10 2.0L inline-four • 100–170 hp
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The car that defined the modern BMW sports saloon — compact, lightweight, rear-wheel-drive, and powered by the legendary M10 engine. The 2002tii with Kugelfischer mechanical fuel injection delivers 130 hp and is the sweet spot; the 2002 Turbo (Europe's first turbocharged production car) delivers 170 hp and is the ultimate collectible. The standard 2002 with 100 hp is the accessible classic. All are rising in value — buy the best you can afford.
Explore the E10 →

Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The 2002's M10 engine produced 100 hp in standard form, not 98 hp as some sources state. Verified against BMW specifications.
Correction applied: The 2002tii produces 130 hp — not 125 hp or 140 hp as some sources state. Factory rating is 130 hp at 5,800 rpm.
Correction applied: The 2002 Turbo produces 170 hp — not 167 hp. BMW's official figure is 170 hp (125 kW).
Correction applied: The 2002ti uses the M05 engine code with twin Solex side-draft carburettors — not a separate engine code. Verified against BMW parts catalogues.
Correction applied: The 2002 was produced from 1968 to 1976, with the 1502 continuing until 1977. Total production was 401,947 units.
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW 2002 Engine Considerations
Sub-headline: The six most common restoration considerations for the M10 engine — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Issue
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	Timing Chain Stretch
	The M10's timing chain can stretch over time, causing rattling and potential valve timing issues. Affects all M10 engines. £500–£1,500 replacement.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	2
	Cylinder Head Cracking & Warping
	Older cylinder heads have been known to crack and warp — water in the oil and a sweet smell around the engine are tell-tale signs. Affects high-mileage M10 engines. £1,000–£3,000 repair.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	3
	Kugelfischer Fuel Injection Servicing
	The mechanical fuel injection system on the tii and Turbo requires specialist servicing — age-related seal degradation and pump wear are common. Affects M15 and M31 engines. £2,000–£5,000 restoration.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	4
	Cooling System Deterioration
	Plastic and rubber in the cooling system gets brittle with age — run away at any sign of overheating. Affects all 2002s. £500–£1,500 restoration.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	5
	Oil Leaks (Valve Cover & Timing Cover)
	Age-related gasket deterioration causes oil leaks. Affects all M10 engines. £200–£800 repair.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	6
	2002 Turbo — Turbocharger & Injection Complexity
	The KKK turbocharger and Kugelfischer injection on the Turbo are complex and expensive to restore — only 1,672 built. Specialist-only work.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service
SECTION 7 — MARKET INTELLIGENCE — <MarketIntelligence>
H2: BMW 2002 Market Intelligence — What Collectors Told Us

	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The 2002 is the car that saved BMW — it established the brand's reputation for sporty, driver-focused saloons and paved the way for the 3 Series. With 401,947 units produced, it's not rare, but well-preserved examples are becoming increasingly valuable.
* The 2002tii is the sweet spot — 130 hp, Kugelfischer mechanical fuel injection, and the most desirable non-Turbo 2002. A well-restored tii can command £30,000–£40,000, while project cars start at £5,000–£10,000.
* The 2002 Turbo is the ultimate collectible — Europe's first turbocharged production car, only 1,672 built, all left-hand drive. Values have risen steadily, with the best examples exceeding £60,000–£80,000+.

SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW 2002 Buyer
"If you buy one 2002 based on this page, buy the best-documented 2002tii you can afford — the Kugelfischer-injected M15 is the sweet spot: 130 hp, mechanical fuel injection, and the most desirable non-Turbo 2002. If the Turbo is calling you, budget for specialist KKK turbo and Kugelfischer restoration — only 1,672 were built, and they're only going one way. On any 2002, a documented M10 rebuild with timing chain and cooling system work is worth more than any service stamp."

The economics box (gold callout):
The 2002 rule — restoration, not replacement: A standard M10 rebuild at £4,000–£6,000 against a £10,000–£15,000 car is 27–60% — ⚠️ watch on cheaper cars. A tii rebuild at £6,000–£10,000+ against a £25,000–£40,000 car is 15–40% — ✅ a worthy investment. A Turbo rebuild at £8,000–£12,000+ against a £60,000–£80,000+ car is 10–20% — ✅ a worthy investment. The 2002 is a rising classic — the tii and Turbo justify the investment; the standard 2002 is the accessible entry point.

SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW 2002 ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW 2002 Engine Range Evolved — 1968 to 1976
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	Launch — the 2002
	1968–1975
	M05 (100 hp)
	BMW launched the 2002 as the sporty compact saloon — the M10 engine was a 2.0L evolution of the earlier 1.8L M10 in the 1800.
	The 2002 saved BMW from financial ruin — it established the brand's reputation for sporty, driver-focused saloons.
	The Twin-Carb ti
	1968–1972
	M05 (120 hp)
	BMW added twin Solex side-draft carburettors to the M10 for the 2002ti — more power, more responsiveness.
	The ti is rare — only a few thousand were built. It's the direct predecessor to the tii.
	The Fuel-Injected tii
	1971–1975
	M15 (130 hp)
	BMW introduced Kugelfischer mechanical fuel injection for the 2002tii — 130 hp, better response, and improved emissions.
	The tii is the sweet spot — Kugelfischer injection, 130 hp, and the most desirable non-Turbo 2002.
	The Turbo — First of Its Kind
	1973–1975
	M31 (170 hp)
	BMW created the 2002 Turbo — Europe's first turbocharged production car. The KKK turbocharger and Kugelfischer injection produced 170 hp.
	Only 1,672 Turbos were built, all left-hand drive. It's the ultimate 2002 collectible.
	________________

	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
