Labour estimate: Fitting a BMW 5 Series engine typically takes 8–22 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE].
The economics box (gold callout):
The 5 Series rule — generation is destiny: A £3,200–£5,500 N47 recon against a £6,000 E60 sits at 53–92% of the car's value — on a sub-£5k E60, that exceeds our 70% BMW threshold, and we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a £22,000 F10, the same recon is 15–25% — ✅ safe. Against a £35,000 G30, a £3,800–£6,000 B58 recon is just 11–17% — a routine repair. Value the car first, then the engine. The M57 in the E39 is the classic bargain — robust enough to justify a £3,000–£4,500 recon on a £6,000 car.

SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW 5 SERIES ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW 5 Series Engine Range Evolved — 1972 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	Launch era — E12/E28
	1972–1988
	M10, M20, M30, M21 diesel
	The 5 Series launched with the M10 four-cylinder and M30 six-cylinder from the E12; the M21 diesel arrived in 1983 as the first BMW diesel.
	The M30 six-cylinder is one of BMW's most durable engines — 200k+ miles is routine. The M21 diesel is robust but slow by modern standards.
	E34 — V8s arrive
	1988–1996
	M50, M60 V8, M51 diesel
	BMW introduced the M60 V8 for the E34 530i/540i — the first V8 in a 5 Series. The M50 six-cylinder replaced the M20; the M51 diesel replaced the M21.
	The M60 V8's Nikasil liners suffered from high-sulphur fuel issues on early cars — buy with a liner test or post-1995 build. The M50 is near-bulletproof.
	E39 — the peak
	1996–2003
	M52, M54, M57 diesel, M62 V8
	The E39 is considered the best 5 Series ever made — and the M57 diesel (530d) is one of the most dependable engines BMW ever built.
	The M57 regularly exceeds 250,000 miles with proper maintenance — this is the engine to seek. The M62 V8 is smooth but the timing chain guides are the caveat.
	E60 — N-series & turbos
	2003–2010
	N46, N52, N53, N54, N62 V8, M57, N47
	Emissions targets forced the move to direct injection and turbos. The N47 was BMW's first modern common-rail diesel; the N54 brought twin turbos to the 535i.
	The N47's rear-chain tensioner and the N54's HPFP issues defined this era. The M57 530d is the robust pick; the N52 six-cylinder is the smooth petrol.
	F10 — refinement & hybrid
	2010–2016
	N47, N55, N63 V8, B47 (LCI)
	The F10 returned to classic proportions and introduced the ActiveHybrid 5. The LCI (2016) quietly replaced the N47 with the B47 — the reliability story changed more than the styling.
	The B47 finally fixed the chain issue that defined the N47. The N55 is robust; the N63 V8 carries the "hot-vee" heat issues. The LCI B47 cars are the F10 to buy.
	G30 — B-series & electrification
	2017–2023
	B47, B48, B57, B58, N63 (updated)
	The CLAR-platform G30 brought B-family engines, mild-hybrid tech and the 530e plug-in hybrid. The 540i with B58 is the modern sweet spot.
	The B-family engines are the most dependable 5 Series powertrains ever made — no chain caveat, robust cooling systems. The 540i is the G30 to buy.
	G60 — the electric era
	2023–present
	B48, B58, B57, i5 electric
	The eighth-generation 5 Series — available with combustion, mild-hybrid, and fully electric i5 powertrains. The i5 is the future; the B58 is the combustion gold standard.
	The i5 is too new for volume reliability data, but the B-family record is impeccable. The G60 540i with B58 is the safe combustion choice.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW 5 SERIES? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW 5 Series?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Family Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The 5 Series Touring (E61/F11/G31) is one of the most capable family estates on sale — room for five, 500+ litres of boot, and a B57 diesel that returns 50+ mpg on the motorway. Buy 2017+ G31 530d for the modern sweet spot; an E39 530d Touring for the classic bargain.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	This is what the 5 Series does best — 50 miles a day on the motorway is the 530d's natural habitat. The G30 520d with B47 returns 60+ mpg; the G30 530i with B48 is the petrol choice. The F10 and G30 are commuting perfection.
	Budget Owner
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	A cheap E60 N47 is not a cheap 5 Series — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £8,000 E60 530d with the M57 is the bargain; a £5,000 520d without chain paperwork is the trap. The E39 530d is the classic value proposition.
	Performance Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The G30 540i with the B58 is the modern sweet spot — 335 bhp, rear-wheel-drive, and the most dependable modern BMW straight-six. The M5 (F10/G30) is the full-fat experience, but the 540i offers 80% of the performance for half the running costs.
	Long-Term Keeper
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	An E39 530d with the M57 or a G30 540i with the B58 are the 5 Series keepers — engines that will outlast the cars around them. The E39 is the rising classic; the G30 is the modern keeper. Buy the best example you can afford; maintenance is an investment, not an expense.
	________________

SECTION 13 — BMW 5 SERIES FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW 5 Series Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW 5 Series engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW 5 Series engine typically costs £2,800–£6,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], with the volume N47/B47 diesels at £2,800–£5,500 and the B58 petrol at £3,800–£6,000. V8 engines (N63/S63) cost significantly more — £6,000–£14,000. Fitting adds £650–£2,100. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW 5 Series engine is the most reliable?
The B58B30 straight-six in the G30 540i — the most dependable modern BMW engine in our dataset. From the classic era, the M57D30 in the E39/E60 530d is one of the most durable diesel engines ever built, regularly exceeding 250,000 miles. Both share one thing: regular oil changes are non-negotiable.
3. Which BMW 5 Series engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but a pre-2016 N47D20C without a documented timing-chain replacement is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The N54B30's HPFP issues are also costly — check history before buying any E60 535i. A 2016+ B47/B48/B58 has no such caveat.
4. How do I know if my 5 Series engine needs replacing?
By engine: cold-start rattle (N47), long cranking and limp mode (N54 HPFP), valve seal smoke on startup (N63). On the M54/M57, cooling system failure is the main cause of engine death — watch for coolant loss. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £2,800–£6,000 engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW 5 Series engine?
Depends entirely on generation. Against a £35,000 G30, a £5,000 B58 recon is just 14% of the car's value — ✅ safe. Against a £22,000 F10, a £4,500 N47 recon is 20% — ✅ safe. Against a £4,500 E60, a £4,000 N47 recon is 89% — ❌ walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW 5 Series generation should I buy?
2017+ G30 540i with the B58 — the modern straight-six in a depreciated executive chassis, no chain caveat, 335 bhp, and the sharpest 5 Series chassis ever. For the classic bargain, E39 530d with the M57 — the engine is bulletproof, the values are still sensible. For diesel economy, G30 520d with B47. Avoid early N47/N54 cars without documented history.
7. How do I find my BMW 5 Series engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation, model year and variant (520d, 530d, 540i) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW 5 Series vs 3 Series — which is more reliable?
Mechanically they share engines across the same eras — the N47/B47/B58 story is identical. The 5 Series is heavier, so it works the powertrain harder; the 3 Series is lighter but benefits from the same engineering. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The 5 Series offers more space and comfort; the 3 Series offers more agility. Read the full head-to-head →

BMW 6 SERIES
BMW 6 SERIES 
STEP 0 — MODEL CLASSIFICATION (internal working note, not published)
Classification: Widest Economics Swing
Justification: The 6 Series spans three distinct generations (E63/E64, F06/F12/F13, G32) across four body styles, with engines ranging from the N52 straight-six to the N63 V8 and S85 V10. The 6 Series carries some of the widest value swings in the BMW range — from £6,000 E63s to £60,000+ F13s — and the same engine can be a rounding error or a death sentence depending on which car it's in. This is exactly the Range Rover precedent.
Brand Voice: Precision & engineering — favour exact figures, technical confidence, "the numbers speak for themselves" tone.
SECTION 1 — HERO — <ModelHero>
Tag Pill: BMW 6 Series • 2003–Present • 3 Generations (E63/E64, F06/F12/F13, G32) • N52, N62, S85, N55, N63, S63, B58, B57
H1: 6 Series Engines — The Complete UK Guide
Sub-headline: Every BMW 6 Series engine across three generations and four body styles — from the N52 straight-six to the N63 V8, the S85 V10, and the B57 straight-six. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for the grand tourer that defined a segment. Backed by 200+ real UK 6 Series engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].
Trust Strip: 📊 200+ Enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED] • 🔧 3 Generations, 4 Body Styles • 📖 12+ Engine Codes • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders
Primary CTA: Explore 6 Series Engines →
Data-integrity note (internal): The 200+ figure is derived from EM_Proprietary_Data shared-platform enquiries for the 6 Series family. The 6 Series shares engines with the 5 Series (E60/F10), 7 Series (F01) and X5/X6. F06/F12/F13 and E63/E64 volumes are partially aggregated in the source data; where specific figures exist they are cited, otherwise marked as pattern-data-tier.
SECTION 2 — OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: BMW 6 Series — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★½ (3.5/5)
	Replacement Economics
	⚠️ The 6 Series rule — the grand tourer premium: a £6,000–£10,000 N63 recon against a £15,000 F06 650i is 40–67% of the car's value — ⚠️ watch. The same engine against a £50,000 F13 M6 is just 12–20% — ✅ safe. Generation and body style decide everything.
	Reliability
	★★★☆☆ — the engines range from the robust N52 and B57 to the notorious N63 V8; the S85 V10 is a masterpiece that demands deep pockets.
	Most Reliable Engine
	B57D30 (640d) and N52B30 (630i) — the straight-six diesels and naturally-aspirated petrols are the dependable choices
	Highest-Risk Engine
	N63B44 (650i) — hot-vee V8 with valve stem seal, timing chain and oil consumption issues • Early N62B48 with valve stem seal failure
	Best Used Buy
	F06/F12/F13 640d with B57 — the diesel straight-six is robust, economical, and the 6 Series experience without the V8 bills
	Best Petrol Buy
	F06/F12/F13 640i with N55 — the turbo straight-six is the sweet spot; the 650i's N63 is the one to avoid without a warranty
	Average Engine Replacement
	£5,500 reconditioned, supply only [BMW-QUOTE]
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	N63 oil consumption / valve stem seals — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
	BMW Ranking
	🏆 The Grand Tourer — style, presence, and the most expensive engine bills in the non-M BMW range
	One-line verdict: The 6 Series is BMW's grand tourer — beautiful, powerful, and expensive to keep. The N63 V8 is the defining risk: a £6,000–£10,000 recon is a routine repair on a £50,000 car and a death sentence on a £12,000 one. Buy the straight-six diesel or petrol unless you have the budget for V8 bills.

Sub-headline: Three generations, four body styles, and one grand touring mission. The right 6 Series depends on which engine you choose — and how much you're prepared to spend keeping it on the road.

First Generation — E63/E64 (2003–2010)
BMW 6 Series E63/E64 🔥 Most Enquired — shared with F06/F12/F13 [BMW-VERIFIED]
2003–2010 • N52, N62, M57, S85 V10 (M6)
★★★☆☆
Our Verdict: The return of the 6 Series after 14 years — and the N62 V8's valve stem seals are the defining issue. The 630i with the N52 straight-six is the robust pick; the 645Ci/650i V8s carry the seal risk; the M6 with the S85 V10 is a masterpiece that demands £8,000–£14,000 for a rebuild.
Explore the E63 →
________________


Second Generation — F06/F12/F13 (2011–2018)
BMW 6 Series F06/F12/F13 🔥 Most Enquired — 200+ enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED]
2011–2018 • N55, N63, B57 (LCI)
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The third-generation 6 Series — and the one most buyers are looking at today. The 640d with the B57 straight-six diesel is the sensible choice; the 640i with the N55 straight-six petrol is the sweet spot; the 650i with the N63 V8 is the one to avoid without a warranty. The M6 with the S63 is the full-fat experience.

Third Generation — G32 Gran Turismo (2017–2023)
BMW 6 Series Gran Turismo G32
2017–2023 • B47, B48, B57, B58
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The controversial 6 Series Gran Turismo — a rebadged 5 Series GT with a liftback body. The B57 diesel and B58 petrol are the most dependable engines ever fitted to a 6 Series. The G32 is the sensible 6 Series; the F06 is the beautiful one.

The Badge Ambiguity — "640i" Means Different Things
Correction applied (internal): The "640i" badge covers two different engines depending on generation. On the F06/F12/F13 (2011–2018), it's the N55B30 (3.0L, 320 PS) — the turbo straight-six. On the G32 Gran Turismo (2017–2023), it's the B58B30 (3.0L, 340 PS) — the modular straight-six. The N55 is robust; the B58 is even more so. The "650i" badge covers the N63 V8 across both F-generation and G-generation cars — the same reliability caveats apply.

	Confidence Score panel:
How confident are these ratings? N63B44, S63B44, B57D30 and B58B30 ratings: 90% confidence — high-volume engines with deep specialist rebuild history and clear failure patterns from BMW TSB data and 100+ specialist interviews. N52B30, N55B30 and M57D30 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — decades of documented patterns from the E60/F10 eras. N62B44/N62B48 ratings: 85% confidence — well-documented valve stem seal failure patterns. S85B50 rating: Pattern-data tier — the V10 is a masterpiece but low-volume in our dataset; specialist knowledge is the primary source.
Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The source data lists "6 Series" as a single entry, but the 6 Series is three distinct generations across four body styles. The E63/E64, F06/F12/F13 and G32 share engines with the 5 Series (E60/F10) and 7 Series (F01). Enquiry figures for the 6 Series are partially aggregated with 5 Series data in the source file.
Correction applied: The N63B44 "hot-vee" V8 is the defining risk of the F-generation 6 Series — valve stem seals, timing chain stretch, and excessive oil consumption are well-documented. BMW issued multiple technical service bulletins and a class action was filed over N63 oil consumption.
Correction applied: The S85 V10 in the E63/E64 M6 is shared with the E60 M5. It's a masterpiece but carries rod bearing and SMG pump failure risks — budget £8,000–£14,000 for a rebuild if either fails.
Correction applied: The G32 Gran Turismo is the third-generation 6 Series — a rebadged 5 Series GT on the CLAR platform. It uses B-family engines only (B47, B48, B57, B58) — no N-series engines, so no chain caveat.
Correction applied: The 635d (E63/E64) used the M57D30 — one of BMW's most dependable diesel engines. The 640d (F06/F12/F13) used the B57D30 — the modern straight-six diesel that's equally robust.
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW 6 Series Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the 6 Series range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	N63 Valve Stem Seal & Oil Consumption
	The defining 6 Series issue — the N63 V8 suffers from premature valve stem seal wear, causing blue smoke on startup and excessive oil consumption. Affects F06/F12/F13 650i (2011–2018). BMW faced a class action over this defect. £6,000–£10,000 recon [BMW-QUOTE].
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	2
	N62 Valve Stem Seal Failure
	The predecessor to the N63 — the N62 V8 in the E63/E64 645Ci/650i suffers from the same valve stem seal issues. Blue smoke on startup is the classic symptom. £4,000–£8,000 recon.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	3
	N63 Timing Chain Stretch
	The N63's hot-vee design leads to timing chain stretch and tensioner failure. Rattling noise on cold start is the early warning; ignored, chain slip destroys the engine. Affects F06/F12/F13 650i.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	4
	S85 Rod Bearing & SMG Pump Failure (M6)
	The E63/E64 M6's V10 is a masterpiece — but rod bearing wear and SMG III pump failure are the defining risks. Rod bearing replacement is £3,000–£5,000; SMG pump failure is £2,000–£4,000. Specialist-only work.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	5
	E63/E64 Cooling System & Battery Drain
	The E63/E64 era's common non-engine issues — cooling system plastic components degrade, and the complex electronics cause battery drain. Check coolant system condition on any E63/E64.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	6
	B57/B58 — Generally Robust
	The modern B-family engines in the G32 Gran Turismo and late F-generation cars are the most dependable 6 Series engines ever made. No major failure patterns — just standard DPF and EGR maintenance on the diesels.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The 6 Series generates fewer enquiries than the 3 Series or 5 Series — but the enquiries it does generate are more expensive. The N63 V8 drives the majority of the volume, with owners facing £6,000–£10,000 recon bills on cars worth £12,000–£25,000. The diesel 640d owners, by contrast, barely feature in the data — the B57 is proving as robust as the M57 before it. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* F06/F12/F13 650i owners are the core of the 6 Series enquiry data — and the N63's valve stem seal and timing chain issues are the primary drivers. These cars are now entering affordable territory (£12,000–£18,000 for a 650i), but the £6,000–£10,000 recon bill means buyers need to budget accordingly. The 640d is the sensible choice; the 650i is the emotional one. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The G32 Gran Turismo generates the fewest enquiries — and that's a good thing. The B-family engines are proving the most dependable 6 Series powertrains ever made. The G32 is the 6 Series for people who want the badge without the bills. (Editorial synthesis.)

SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW 6 Series Buyer
"If you buy one 6 Series based on this page, buy an F06/F12/F13 640d with the B57 straight-six diesel — it's the most dependable engine in the range, returns 45+ mpg, and delivers the grand tourer experience without the V8 bills. If the V8 is calling you, budget £6,000–£10,000 for an N63 recon and buy the best warranty you can find. And on an E63/E64 N62, a documented valve stem seal replacement is worth more than any service stamp."

Labour estimate: Fitting a BMW 6 Series engine typically takes 10–22 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE].
The economics box (gold callout):
The 6 Series rule — the grand tourer premium: A £6,000–£10,000 N63 recon against a £15,000 F06 650i sits at 40–67% of the car's value — ⚠️ watch. Against a £50,000 F13 M6, the same S63 recon at £9,000–£14,000 is just 18–28% — ✅ safe. Against a £6,000 E63 650i, a £4,500–£8,000 N62 recon is 75–133% — ❌ walk away, or buy a used engine and accept the risk. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Value the car first, then the engine. The 640d's B57 recon at £5,000–£8,000 sits at 33–53% of a £15,000 F12 — safe and sensible.

SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW 6 SERIES ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW 6 Series Engine Range Evolved — 2003 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	E63/E64 — the V8 era
	2003–2010
	N52, N62 V8, S85 V10, M57 diesel
	BMW revived the 6 Series after 14 years — the N62 V8 was the flagship engine, the S85 V10 was the M car, and the M57 diesel arrived in 2007 for economy.
	The N62's valve stem seals are the defining issue — every E63/E64 V8 owner faces this risk. The 630i with the N52 straight-six is the robust pick; the 635d with the M57 is the sensible diesel.
	F06/F12/F13 — the turbo era
	2011–2018
	N55, N63 V8, B57 diesel, S63 V8
	The F-generation brought turbocharging to the 6 Series — the N55 straight-six replaced the N52, the N63 twin-turbo V8 replaced the N62, and the B57 diesel arrived in the LCI.
	The N63's hot-vee design created new failure modes — valve stem seals, timing chain stretch, and oil consumption. The B57 diesel is the reliable choice; the N55 is the sweet spot.
	G32 — the Gran Turismo era
	2017–2023
	B47, B48, B57, B58
	The G32 Gran Turismo moved the 6 Series to the CLAR platform — B-family engines only, no N-series carryover. The 640i with the B58 is the petrol pick; the 630d/640d with the B57 is the diesel.
	The B-family engines are the most dependable 6 Series powertrains ever made — no chain caveat, no V8 valve seal issues. The G32 is the sensible 6 Series; the F06 is the beautiful one.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW 6 SERIES? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW 6 Series?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Grand Touring Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The 6 Series is built for this — long-distance cruising in luxury. The F06/F12/F13 640d with the B57 is the perfect GT: 45+ mpg, 600-mile range, and wafting comfort. The 640i with N55 is the petrol alternative; the V8s are for the emotionally committed.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	The 6 Series is a grand tourer, not a commuter car — it's long, wide, and thirsty (especially the V8s). The G32 Gran Turismo is the most practical, but the 5 Series does the commuting job better. Buy a 640d if you must, but the 5 Series is the sensible choice.
	Budget Owner
	⭐☆☆☆☆
	The most dangerous badge on this site for a tight budget. A £6,000 E63 650i is not a cheap 6 Series — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £15,000 F06 640d is the bargain; a £8,000 650i without a warranty is the trap.
	Performance Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The M6 with the S63 V8 is the full-fat experience — 560–600 bhp, 3.9s to 60, and a V8 roar that justifies the bills. The F13 M6 is the one to buy; the E63 M6 with the S85 V10 is a masterpiece but carries rod bearing risk. Buy the best warranty you can find.
	Collector / Keeper
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The E63/E64 630i with the N52 is a rising classic — the last naturally-aspirated straight-six in a 6 Series. The F13 M6 with the S63 is a future classic. Both are worth proper engine investment; the N52 is the keeper, the M6 is the investment.
	________________

SECTION 13 — BMW 6 SERIES FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW 6 Series Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW 6 Series engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW 6 Series engine typically costs £2,800–£10,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the B47 diesel at £2,800–£4,500 to the N63 V8 at £6,000–£10,000. The S85 V10 (M6) and S63 V8 (M6) cost £8,000–£14,000. Fitting adds £650–£2,100. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW 6 Series engine is the most reliable?
The B57D30 straight-six diesel in the F06/F12/F13 640d and G32 630d/640d — the most dependable engine ever fitted to a 6 Series. The N52B30 in the E63/E64 630i is the classic robust choice. The N55B30 in the F06/F12/F13 640i is also strong. Avoid the N63 V8 unless you have a warranty.
3. Which BMW 6 Series engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but the N63B44 V8 (650i) without a documented valve stem seal or timing chain history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The N62 V8 in the E63/E64 carries the same valve stem seal risk. The S85 V10 (E63 M6) and S63 V8 (F13 M6) are masterpieces but require M-car budgets for maintenance. A 640d with B57 has none of these caveats.
4. How do I know if my 6 Series engine needs replacing?
By engine: blue smoke on startup (N62/N63 valve stem seals), rattling on cold start (N63 timing chain), rod bearing knock (S85 V10), SMG pump failure (E63 M6). On the N62/N63, excessive oil consumption (1L per 1,000 miles) is a warning sign. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £4,000–£14,000 engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW 6 Series engine?
Depends entirely on generation and body style. Against a £50,000 F13 M6, a £9,000–£14,000 S63 recon is 18–28% — ✅ safe. Against a £15,000 F06 650i, a £6,000–£10,000 N63 recon is 40–67% — ⚠️ watch. Against a £6,000 E63 650i, a £4,500–£8,000 N62 recon is 75–133% — ❌ walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW 6 Series should I buy?
F06/F12/F13 640d with the B57 diesel — the most dependable engine in the range, 45+ mpg, and the grand tourer experience without the V8 bills. For petrol, F06/F12/F13 640i with the N55 — the turbo straight-six is the sweet spot. For the full experience, F13 M6 with the S63 — but budget £9,000–£14,000 for a recon and buy the best warranty. Avoid early E63 N62 V8s without documented seal history. The G32 Gran Turismo is the sensible 6 Series; the F06 is the beautiful one.
7. How do I find my BMW 6 Series engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation, body style, model year and variant (630i, 650i, 640d, M6) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW 6 Series vs 5 Series — which is more reliable?
Mechanically they share engines across the same eras — the N52/N62/N55/N63/B57/B58 story is identical. The 6 Series is heavier and more complex (especially the convertibles and Gran Coupés), so it works the powertrain harder. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The 5 Series offers more practicality; the 6 Series offers more style and presence. Read the full head-to-head →

A 640d with B57 has none of these caveats."

BMW 7 SERIES
BMW 7 SERIES 
STEP 0 — MODEL CLASSIFICATION (internal working note, not published)
Classification: Widest Economics Swing
Justification: The 7 Series is BMW's flagship — six generations (E23, E32, E38, E65, F01, G11, G70) over nearly 50 years. It carries the widest value swing in the BMW range — from £3,000 E23s to £100,000+ G70s. The same N63 V8 recon at £7,000–£10,000+ is a rounding error on a £70,000 car and a death sentence on a £6,000 one. This is exactly the Range Rover precedent.
SECTION 1 — HERO — <ModelHero>
Tag Pill: BMW 7 Series • 1977–Present • 7 Generations (E23, E32, E38, E65, F01, G11, G70) • M30, M60, M62, N62, N63, B58, B57, S68
H1: 7 Series Engines — The Complete UK Guide
Sub-headline: Every BMW 7 Series engine across seven generations — from the carburetted M30 six-cylinder to the N63 twin-turbo V8 and the electrified B58 of the G70. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's flagship. Backed by 300+ real UK 7 Series engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].
Trust Strip: 📊 300+ Enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED] • 🔧 7 Generations Covered • 📖 15+ Engine Codes • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders
Primary CTA: Explore 7 Series Engines →
Data-integrity note (internal): The 300+ figure is derived from EM_Proprietary_Data shared-platform enquiries for the 7 Series family. The 7 Series shares engines with the 5 Series (E60/F10) and X5/X6. F01/F02, G11/G12 and G70 volumes are partially aggregated in the source data; E23/E32/E38/E65 volumes are not captured and are marked as pattern-data-tier throughout.
SECTION 2 — OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: BMW 7 Series — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★☆☆ (3.4/5)
	Replacement Economics
	⚠️ The 7 Series rule — flagship bills, bargain-bin values: a £7,000–£10,000+ N63 recon against a £8,000 F01 750i is 88–125% of the car's value — ❌ scrap threshold. Against a £70,000 G70 760i, the same repair is just 10–14% — ✅ safe. Generation decides everything.
	Reliability
	★★★☆☆ — the M30 and M57 are bulletproof; the N62 and N63 V8s define the risk; the B58 and B57 are the modern gold standard.
	Most Reliable Engine
	B58B30 (G70 740i) and M57D30 (E38/E65 730d) — the modern and classic straight-six diesels
	Highest-Risk Engine
	N63B44 (F01 750i) — timing chain guides, valve stem seals, turbo failures, HPFP • N62B48 (E65 750i) — timing chain guides, valve stem seals
	Best Used Buy
	G11/G12 740i with B58 — the modern straight-six in a depreciated flagship chassis, no V8 caveats
	Best Diesel Buy
	G11/G12 730d with B57 — 50+ mpg, 600-mile range, and the most dependable 7 Series powertrain
	Average Engine Replacement
	£5,500 reconditioned, supply only [BMW-QUOTE]
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	N63 timing chain guides / valve stem seals — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
	BMW Ranking
	👑 The Flagship — BMW's technology leader and the most expensive engine bills in the non-M BMW range
	One-line verdict: The 7 Series is BMW's flagship — the car that introduces every new technology before any other model. The engines range from bulletproof sixes to V8s that can cost more to replace than the car is worth. Buy the straight-six unless you have the budget for V8 bills — and on an N63, a documented timing chain and valve seal history is worth more than any service stamp.

	SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: BMW 7 Series Generations — Find Your Vehicle
Sub-headline: Seven generations, nearly 50 years, and one flagship nameplate that defines BMW luxury. The right 7 Series depends entirely on which generation you're willing to maintain.
________________


BMW 7 Series E23
1977–1987 • M30 six-cylinder (2.5L–3.5L), M102 turbo
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The original flagship — the car that created the modern BMW luxury sedan. M30 six-cylinders are bulletproof; the M102 turbo is rare and complex. Bodywork and electronics are the real projects now.
Explore the E23 →
________________


BMW 7 Series E32
1986–1994 • M30, M60 V8, M70 V12
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The first 7 Series with a V8 and V12 — the M60's Nikasil liner issues are the caveat on early cars; post-1994 M60s are robust. The M70 V12 is a masterpiece but carries twin-bank maintenance costs.
Explore the E32 →
________________


BMW 7 Series E38 🔥 Most Enquired — classic era [BMW-VERIFIED]
1994–2001 • M52/M54 (728i/730i), M62 V8 (735i/740i), M57 diesel (730d), M73 V12 (750i)
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The 7 Series at its most beautiful — and the first to offer a diesel. The M57 730d is one of the most dependable BMW engines ever made; the M62 V8's timing chain guides are the defining risk. The 728i with M52 is the robust petrol choice.
Explore the E38 →
________________


BMW 7 Series E65/E66 🔥 Most Enquired — 300+ enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED]
2001–2008 • M54 (730i), N52 (730i LCI), N62 V8 (735i/745i/750i), M57 (730d), N73 V12 (760i)
★★★☆☆
Our Verdict: The controversial Bangle-era 7 Series — and the N62 V8's timing chain guides and valve stem seals define the risk. The 730d with M57 is the sensible pick; the V8s carry the caveat; the 760i with N73 V12 is a masterpiece with twin-bank costs.
Explore the E65 →
________________


BMW 7 Series F01/F02 🔥 Most Enquired — F01 750i drives N63 volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
2008–2015 • N52 (730i), N54 (740i), N63 V8 (750i), M57/N57 (730d/740d), N74 V12 (760i)
★★★☆☆
Our Verdict: The generation that brought twin-turbo V8s to the 7 Series — and the N63's timing chain guides, valve stem seals and turbo failures define the risk. The 730d with M57/N57 is the robust choice; the 750i's N63 is the one to avoid without a warranty.
Explore the F01 →
________________


BMW 7 Series G11/G12
2015–2022 • B47, B57 (730d/740d), B58 (740i), N63 (750i), N74 V12 (M760i)
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The CLAR-platform 7 Series — and the most dependable modern 7 Series ever made. The 740i with B58 is the sweet spot; the 730d with B57 is the diesel perfection. The N63 in the 750i still carries the V8 caveat; the M760i's V12 is the flagship experience.
Explore the G11 →
________________


BMW 7 Series G70
2022–present • B58 (735i/740i), B57 (740d), S68 V8 (760i), electric i7
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The seventh-generation 7 Series — available with petrol, diesel, plug-in hybrid, and fully electric i7 powertrains. The B58 in the 740i is as robust as ever; the S68 V8 in the 760i is BMW's latest twin-turbo V8; the i7 is the future. Too new for volume reliability data, but the B-family record is impeccable.
Explore the G70 →

The V8 Divide — The Line That Matters Most


	E38/E65 (M62/N62 V8)
	F01 (N63 V8)
	G11/G12/G70 (N63TU/S68 V8)
	Core issue
	Timing chain guides
	Timing chain guides + valve stem seals + turbo failure
	Improved but still complex
	Repair cost
	£3,000–£6,000
	£7,000–£10,000+
	£7,000–£12,000+
	Our position
	Buy only with documented guide replacement
	Avoid without comprehensive warranty
	Buy the straight-six instead
	Read the full comparison: BMW 7 Series F01 vs G11 →
The Badge Ambiguity — "740i" Means Different Things
Correction applied (internal): The "740i" badge covers different engines depending on generation. On the E38 (1994–2001), it's the M62B44 V8 (4.4L, 286 bhp). On the E65 (2001–2008), it's the N62B40 V8 (4.0L, 306 bhp). On the F01 (2008–2015), it's the N54B30 (3.0L twin-turbo straight-six, 326 bhp) — a completely different engine family. On the G11 (2015–2022), it's the B58B30 (3.0L turbo straight-six, 326 bhp). On the G70 (2022+), it's also the B58B30 (3.0L, 381 PS). All share a badge. None share a reliability profile.

	Confidence Score panel:
How confident are these ratings? N63B44, B58B30, B57D30, M57D30 ratings: 90% confidence — high-volume engines with deep specialist rebuild history and clear failure patterns from BMW TSB data and 100+ specialist interviews. N62/M62 ratings: 85% confidence — well-documented timing chain guide failure patterns. E23/E32/E38 engines: Expert-knowledge tier — decades of documented patterns and enthusiast records, not proprietary volume data. G70 engines: Provisional — the parc is too young for volume data; ratings reflect early patterns and shared-architecture evidence.
Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The N63B44 "hot-vee" V8 is the defining risk of the F01 7 Series — timing chain guides made of brittle plastic break apart, sending metal fragments through the engine. High oil consumption from worn valve stem seals leads to lubrication starvation. Turbochargers fail from excessive heat. BMW's later N63TU updates address some problems while creating new ones. Independent mechanics report repair bills consistently exceeding £7,000–£10,000.
Correction applied: The M62 V8 (E38 735i/740i) timing chain guides are the defining failure — plastic guides shatter, sending debris through the oil system. Symptoms typically begin between 80,000 and 100,000 miles.
Correction applied: The "740i" badge covers four different engines across generations — M62 V8 (E38), N62 V8 (E65), N54 straight-six (F01), B58 straight-six (G11/G70). The engine family — not the badge — determines reliability.
Correction applied: The G70 7 Series (2022+) offers petrol (B58, S68), diesel (B57), plug-in hybrid (750e, M760e) and fully electric (i7) powertrains. The B58 in the 740i produces 381 PS; the S68 V8 in the 760i produces 544 PS.
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW 7 Series Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the 7 Series range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	N63 Timing Chain Guide Failure
	The defining F01 7 Series issue — plastic timing chain guides break apart, sending metal fragments through the engine. Rattling on cold start is the warning; ignored, piston-to-valve contact destroys the engine. Affects F01 750i (2008–2015). £7,000–£10,000+ recon.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	2
	N63 Valve Stem Seal & Oil Consumption
	The N63's companion failure — worn valve stem seals cause blue smoke on startup and excessive oil consumption (1L per 1,000 miles). Affects F01 750i. Often requires engine-out repair.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	3
	M62/N62 Timing Chain Guides
	The E38 and E65 V8s' defining failure — plastic timing chain guides shatter, sending debris through the oil system. Rattling on cold start is the warning. Symptoms typically begin at 80,000–100,000 miles. £3,000–£6,000 repair.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	4
	N63 Turbocharger & HPFP Failure
	The N63's hot-vee design causes turbocharger failure from excessive heat and poor oil return. High-pressure fuel pumps dump raw fuel into cylinders, destroying pistons and rods. Affects F01 750i.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	5
	E38/E65 Air Suspension & Electronics
	Not an engine failure — but the most common non-engine 7 Series enquiry. The E38 and E65's air suspension and complex electronics (iDrive, ABS modules) are costly to repair. Check compressor and suspension on any E38/E65.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	6
	B58 Coolant System
	The modern B58's known weak point — coolant levels drop suddenly in the primary reservoir, leading to overheating. Valve cover gaskets can fail due to overheating. Affects G11/G70 740i (2015+). £400–£800 repair, not catastrophic if caught early.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The 7 Series generates fewer enquiries than the 5 Series — but the enquiries it does generate are significantly more expensive. The N63 V8 drives the majority of the volume, with owners facing £7,000–£10,000+ recon bills on cars worth £8,000–£20,000. The B58 owners, by contrast, barely feature in the data — the B58 is proving as robust in the flagship as it is in the 3 Series. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* F01 750i owners are the core of the 7 Series enquiry data — and the N63's timing chain guides, valve stem seals, turbo failures and HPFP issues are the primary drivers. These cars are now entering affordable territory (£8,000–£15,000 for a 750i), but the £7,000–£10,000+ recon bill means buyers need to budget accordingly. The 730d with M57/N57 is the sensible choice; the 750i is the emotional one — and the expensive one. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The G11/G12 740i with the B58 is the 7 Series to buy — the B58 is one of the most reliable BMW engines ever built, the G11 chassis is sharp, and depreciation has already done its work. The G70 is too new for volume reliability data, but the B-family record is impeccable. (Editorial synthesis.)

SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW 7 Series Buyer
"If you buy one 7 Series based on this page, buy a G11/G12 740i with the B58 straight-six — it's the most dependable engine ever fitted to the flagship, delivers 326 bhp and 40+ mpg, and the G11 chassis is the sharpest 7 Series ever made. If the V8 is calling you, budget £7,000–£10,000+ for an N63 recon and buy the best warranty you can find. And on an E38 or E65 V8, a documented timing chain guide replacement is worth more than any service stamp."
________________

N63B44, B58B30, B57D30, M57D30, N57D30 figures [BMW-QUOTE]; older engines [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet].
Labour estimate: Fitting a BMW 7 Series engine typically takes 10–24 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE].
The economics box (gold callout):
The 7 Series rule — flagship bills, bargain-bin values: A £7,000–£10,000+ N63 recon against an £8,000 F01 750i sits at 88–125% of the car's value — ❌ scrap threshold. Against a £70,000 G70 760i, a £9,000–£15,000 S68 recon is just 13–21% — ✅ safe. Against a £5,000 E38 740i, a £3,000–£6,000 M62 recon is 60–120% — ⚠️ watch. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Value the car first, then the engine. The B58 740i is the modern sweet spot — reliable, economical, and the flagship experience without the V8 bills.

SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW 7 SERIES ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW 7 Series Engine Range Evolved — 1977 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	Launch era — E23
	1977–1987
	M30 six-cylinder, M102 turbo
	BMW's first flagship sedan launched with the M30 six-cylinder — the same engine family that powered the 3 Series. The M102 turbo arrived in 1980 for the 745i.
	The M30 is one of BMW's most durable engines — 200k+ miles is routine. The M102 turbo is rare and complex.
	E32 — V8s & V12s arrive
	1986–1994
	M30, M60 V8, M70 V12
	BMW introduced the M60 V8 (first V8 in a BMW since the 1960s) and the M70 V12 — a landmark engineering achievement.
	The M60's Nikasil liners suffered from high-sulphur fuel issues on early cars — buy with a liner test or post-1994 build. The M70 V12 is a masterpiece but carries twin-bank maintenance costs.
	E38 — diesel arrives
	1994–2001
	M52/M54, M62 V8, M57 diesel, M73 V12
	The E38 introduced the M57 diesel — the first diesel 7 Series — and refined the V8 with the M62. The M73 V12 replaced the M70.
	The M57 is one of BMW's most dependable diesel engines — regularly exceeding 250,000 miles. The M62 V8's timing chain guides are the defining risk.
	E65 — N-series V8s
	2001–2008
	M54/N52, N62 V8, M57, N73 V12
	The Bangle-era 7 Series brought the N62 V8 — BMW's first V8 with variable valve lift and electric throttle. The N73 V12 replaced the M73.
	The N62's timing chain guides and valve stem seals are the defining failures. The M57 730d remains the sensible choice.
	F01 — twin-turbo era
	2008–2015
	N52, N54, N63 V8, N57, N74 V12
	The F01 introduced twin-turbocharging across the range — the N54 straight-six in the 740i, the N63 V8 in the 750i, the N57 diesel in the 730d/740d.
	The N63 V8 is the defining risk — timing chain guides, valve stem seals, turbo failures, HPFP issues. The N57 diesel is the robust choice.
	G11 — B-series & electrification
	2015–2022
	B47, B57, B58, N63TU, N74 V12
	The CLAR-platform G11 brought B-family engines — the B58 straight-six in the 740i, the B57 diesel in the 730d/740d. The N63 was updated to the N63TU but still carries V8 caveats.
	The B58 is the most dependable 7 Series powertrain ever made — no V8 chain issues, no diesel DPF nightmares. The 740i is the G11 to buy.
	G70 — the electric era
	2022–present
	B58, B57, S68 V8, i7 electric
	The seventh-generation 7 Series — available with petrol, diesel, plug-in hybrid, and fully electric i7 powertrains. The i7 is the future; the B58 is the combustion gold standard.
	The G70 is too new for volume reliability data, but the B-family record is impeccable. The S68 V8 in the 760i is BMW's latest twin-turbo V8 — too early to assess long-term reliability.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW 7 SERIES? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW 7 Series?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Flagship Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The 7 Series is built for this — the G11/G12 740i with the B58 delivers flagship luxury, 326 bhp, 40+ mpg, and the most dependable engine ever fitted to the 7 Series. The G70 740i is the modern version. Buy the B58; avoid the V8s unless you have a warranty.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	The 7 Series is a flagship, not a commuter car — it's long, wide, and thirsty (especially the V8s). The 730d with B57 is the most sensible choice, but the 5 Series does the commuting job better. Buy a 730d if you must, but the 5 Series is the sensible choice.
	Budget Owner
	⭐☆☆☆☆
	The most dangerous badge on this site for a tight budget. A £6,000 F01 750i is not a cheap 7 Series — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £15,000 G11 740i with B58 is the bargain; a £6,000 750i without a warranty is the trap.
	Chauffeur / Executive Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The 7 Series Long Wheelbase (E66/F02/G12/G70) is the definitive chauffeur car — acres of rear legroom, reclining seats, and the quietest cabin in the range. The B57 diesel is the sensible choice; the V8s are for clients who don't pay the fuel bills.
	Collector / Keeper
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	An E38 730d with the M57 is a rising classic — the most beautiful 7 Series and one of the most dependable diesel engines ever made. A G11 740i with the B58 is the modern keeper — reliable, beautiful, and appreciating as the last ICE 7 Series approaches.
	________________

SECTION 13 — BMW 7 SERIES FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW 7 Series Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW 7 Series engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW 7 Series engine typically costs £2,800–£10,000+ supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the B47 diesel at £2,800–£4,500 to the N63 V8 at £7,000–£10,000+. The N74 V12 (760i) costs £8,000–£14,000+; the S68 V8 (G70 760i) costs £9,000–£15,000. Fitting adds £650–£2,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW 7 Series engine is the most reliable?
The B58B30 straight-six in the G11/G12/G70 740i — the most dependable modern BMW engine in our dataset and the best engine ever fitted to a 7 Series. From the classic era, the M57D30 in the E38/E65 730d is one of the most durable diesel engines ever built.
3. Which BMW 7 Series engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but the N63B44 V8 (F01/G11 750i) without a documented timing chain guide replacement, valve stem seal history, and comprehensive warranty is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The N62 V8 (E65 750i) carries the same timing chain guide risk. A B58 740i has none of these caveats.
4. How do I know if my 7 Series engine needs replacing?
By engine: rattling on cold start (N63/M62/N62 timing chain guides), blue smoke on startup and excessive oil consumption (N63/N62 valve stem seals), oil in coolant (N63 turbo failure), long cranking and limp mode (N63 HPFP failure). On the M57, cooling system failure is the main cause of engine death — watch for coolant loss. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £2,800–£10,000+ engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW 7 Series engine?
Depends entirely on generation and engine. Against a £70,000 G70 760i, a £9,000–£15,000 S68 recon is 13–21% — ✅ safe. Against a £20,000 G11 750i, a £7,000–£10,000 N63 recon is 35–50% — ⚠️ watch. Against an £8,000 F01 750i, a £7,000–£10,000+ recon is 88–125% — ❌ walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW 7 Series should I buy?
G11/G12 740i with the B58 — the most dependable 7 Series ever made, 326 bhp, 40+ mpg, and depreciated into sensible territory. For diesel, G11/G12 730d with B57 — 50+ mpg and bulletproof. For the classic, E38 730d with M57 — the most beautiful 7 Series and one of the most dependable engines ever made. Avoid F01 750i without a comprehensive warranty; the B58 is the safe choice.
7. How do I find my BMW 7 Series engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation, model year and variant (730d, 740i, 750i, 760i) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW 7 Series vs 5 Series — which is more reliable?
Mechanically they share engines across the same eras — the N52/N62/N63/B57/B58 story is identical. The 7 Series is heavier and more complex (especially the long-wheelbase and V12 variants), so it works the powertrain harder. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The 5 Series offers more value; the 7 Series offers more presence and luxury. Read the full head-to-head →

BMW 8 SERIES
BMW 8 SERIES 
STEP 0 — MODEL CLASSIFICATION (internal working note, not published)
Classification: Widest Economics Swing
Justification: The 8 Series spans two distinct generations (E31 1990–1999, G14/G15/G16 2018–2026) across three body styles, with engines ranging from the M70 V12 to the N63 V8 and B58 straight-six. The modern 8 Series carries some of the widest value swings in the BMW range — from £30,000 used G15s to £100,000+ G16s — and the same N63 V8 recon at £7,000–£12,000 can be 12% of a new car's value or 35%+ of a used one's. The original E31 is now a collector's item where engine work is restoration, not repair. This is exactly the Range Rover precedent.
SECTION 1 — HERO — <ModelHero>
Tag Pill: BMW 8 Series • 1990–1999, 2018–2026 • 2 Generations (E31, G14/G15/G16) • M60, M62, M70, M73, S70, B58, N63, S63, B57
H1: 8 Series Engines — The Complete UK Guide
Sub-headline: Every BMW 8 Series engine across two generations and three body styles — from the M70 V12 of the original E31 to the B58 straight-six and N63 V8 of the modern G15. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's grand tourer flagship. Backed by 150+ real UK 8 Series engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].
Trust Strip: 📊 150+ Enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED] • 🔧 2 Generations, 3 Body Styles • 📖 10+ Engine Codes • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders
Primary CTA: Explore 8 Series Engines →
Data-integrity note (internal): The 150+ figure is derived from EM_Proprietary_Data shared-platform enquiries for the 8 Series family. The modern 8 Series (G14/G15/G16) shares engines with the 5 Series (G30) and 7 Series (G11/G70). The original E31 (1990–1999) volumes are not captured in the proprietary data and are labelled as pattern-data-tier throughout. Approximately 6,600 8 Series vehicles are currently on UK roads, with the 840i M Sport being the most popular trim at 3,421 units.
SECTION 2 — OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: BMW 8 Series — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★½ (3.5/5)
	Replacement Economics
	⚠️ The 8 Series rule — grand tourer, grand bills: an N63 recon at £7,000–£12,000 against a £30,000 used G15 850i is 23–40% of the car's value — ⚠️ watch. Against a £100,000 G16, the same repair is just 7–12% — ✅ safe. The E31 is now a classic where engine work is restoration, not repair.
	Reliability
	★★★☆☆ — the B58 straight-six is the modern gold standard; the N63 V8 carries the hot-vee caveats; the E31's V8s and V12s are ageing but fundamentally robust.
	Most Reliable Engine
	B58B30 (840i) — the most dependable modern BMW engine — and the M70/M73 V12 in the E31, which are surprisingly durable
	Highest-Risk Engine
	N63B44 (M850i) — timing chain guides, valve stem seals, turbo failures, excessive oil consumption
	Best Used Buy
	G15 840i with B58 — the straight-six is bulletproof, the chassis is magnificent, and the V8 caveats don't apply
	Best Performance Buy
	G15 M850i with N63 — 523 bhp of twin-turbo V8, but budget £7,000–£12,000 for a recon and buy the best warranty
	Average Engine Replacement
	£5,500 reconditioned, supply only [BMW-QUOTE]
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	N63 oil consumption / timing chain guides — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
	BMW Ranking
	👑 The Flagship GT — BMW's most expensive non-M model and the car with the highest parts bills in the range
	One-line verdict: The 8 Series is BMW's grand tourer flagship — beautiful, powerful, and expensive to keep. The B58 840i is the sensible choice; the N63 M850i is the emotional one. On the modern car, buy the straight-six unless you have the budget for V8 bills. On the E31, buy the V12 and treat it as a restoration project.

	SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: BMW 8 Series Generations — Find Your Vehicle
Sub-headline: Two generations, thirty years apart, and one grand touring mission. The original E31 is now a collector's classic; the modern G14/G15/G16 is a technological masterpiece. Choose your era — and your budget.
________________


First Generation — E31 (1990–1999)
BMW 8 Series E31
1990–1999 • M60B40 V8, M62B44 V8, M70B50 V12, M73B54 V12, S70B56 V12 (850CSi)
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The original 8 Series — a pillarless coupe with pop-up headlights and a V12 that defined 1990s BMW. The M70 and M73 V12s are surprisingly durable if maintained; the V8s (M60/M62) carry the same Nikasil liner caveats as the E34 5 Series. Now a rising classic where engine work is restoration, not repair. The 850CSi with the S70 V12 is a six-figure collector's item.
Explore the E31 →
________________


Second Generation — G14/G15/G16 (2018–2026)
BMW 8 Series Coupé G15 🔥 Most Enquired — 150+ enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED]
2018–2026 • B58B30 (840i), N63B44 (M850i), S63B44 (M8), B57D30 (840d)
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The return of the 8 Series after 19 years — and the B58 straight-six in the 840i is the most dependable engine ever fitted to the badge. The N63 V8 in the M850i carries the hot-vee caveats; the M8 with the S63 is the full-fat experience. The 840i is the sensible choice; the M850i is the emotional one. Buy the B58 unless you have V8 money.
Explore the G15 →
________________


BMW 8 Series Convertible G14
2018–2026 • B58B30 (840i), N63B44 (M850i), S63B44 (M8)
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The open-top 8 Series — same engines as the G15, but with a fabric roof that adds weight and complexity. The B58 in the 840i Convertible is the sensible pick; the M850i Convertible is the weekend toy. Check the roof mechanism on any used example.
Explore the G14 →
________________


BMW 8 Series Gran Coupé G16
2019–2026 • B58B30 (840i), N63B44 (M850i), S63B44 (M8)
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The four-door 8 Series — the most practical 8 Series and the one with the most presence. Same engines as the G15/G14, but the G16's added length and rear doors make it a genuine four-seater. The 840i Gran Coupé is the sweet spot; the M850i is the statement.
Explore the G16 →

The Badge Ambiguity — "840i" vs "840d"
Correction applied (internal): The "840i" badge uses the B58B30 straight-six petrol (3.0L, 333–340 bhp). The "840d" uses the B57D30 straight-six diesel (3.0L, 315–340 bhp). Both are excellent engines, but the B58 petrol is the more refined and reliable of the two. The "M850i" uses the N63B44 twin-turbo V8 (4.4L, 523 bhp) — a completely different engine family with different reliability characteristics. The "M8" uses the S63B44 — a high-performance version of the N63.

	Confidence Score panel:
How confident are these ratings? B58B30 and N63B44 ratings: 90% confidence — high-volume modern engines with deep specialist rebuild history and clear failure patterns from BMW TSB data and 100+ specialist interviews. B57D30 and S63B44 ratings: 85% confidence — well-documented patterns from the G30/G11 eras. E31 engines: Expert-knowledge tier — decades of documented patterns and enthusiast records, not proprietary volume data.
Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The source data lists "8 Series" as a single entry, but the 8 Series is two distinct generations. The modern G14/G15/G16 (2018–2026) shares engines with the 5 Series (G30) and 7 Series (G11/G70). The original E31 (1990–1999) is not captured in the proprietary data.
Correction applied: The N63B44 in the M850i carries the same hot-vee V8 caveats as the F01 750i and G30 M550i — timing chain guides made of brittle plastic, valve stem seal wear, excessive oil consumption, turbo failures from heat soak. BMW's later N63TU updates address some issues but the fundamental hot-vee design remains a weakness.
Correction applied: The M850i N63 produces 523 bhp — significantly more than the F01 750i's 407–450 bhp. The extra power means more heat, more stress, and potentially more failures.
Correction applied: The E31's M70 V12 (850i) and M73 V12 (850Ci) are surprisingly durable engines — the M70 was BMW's first production V12 and is known for high mileage if maintained. The S70 in the 850CSi is a rare and valuable engine — only a handful were produced.
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW 8 Series Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the 8 Series range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	N63 Timing Chain Guide Failure (M850i)
	The defining modern 8 Series issue — plastic timing chain guides break apart, sending metal fragments through the engine. Rattling on cold start is the warning; ignored, piston-to-valve contact destroys the engine. Affects G14/G15/G16 M850i. £7,000–£12,000+ recon.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	2
	N63 Valve Stem Seal & Oil Consumption
	The N63's companion failure — worn valve stem seals cause blue smoke on startup and excessive oil consumption (1L per 1,000 miles). Affects M850i. Often requires engine-out repair.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	3
	N63 Turbocharger & HPFP Failure
	The N63's hot-vee design causes turbocharger failure from excessive heat and poor oil return. High-pressure fuel pumps can fail, dumping raw fuel into cylinders. Affects M850i.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	4
	B58 Coolant System (840i)
	The B58's known weak point — coolant expansion tank leaks and water pump failures. Affects G14/G15/G16 840i (2018+). £400–£800 repair, not catastrophic if caught early.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	5
	B57 Diesel DPF & Injector Issues (840d)
	The B57 diesel's primary weak points — DPF clogging on urban-driven cars and injector issues. Affects G14/G15/G16 840d (2018+). Well-documented, repairable.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	6
	E31 Electrical & Cooling System
	The original 8 Series' common non-engine issues — complex electronics (EDC, ABS, airbag modules) and cooling system plastic components degrade. Check everything on any E31.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

Signal

	⬆ Rising [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The 8 Series generates fewer enquiries than any other BMW model in our dataset — and that's a good thing. The B58-powered 840i owners barely feature in the data; the N63-powered M850i owners are the core of the enquiry volume. The B58 is proving as reliable in the flagship GT as it is in the 3 Series. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* The M850i's N63 V8 drives the majority of 8 Series enquiries — timing chain guides, valve stem seals, turbo failures and HPFP issues. These cars are now entering affordable used territory (£30,000–£50,000 for a used M850i), but the £7,000–£12,000+ recon bill means buyers need to budget accordingly. The 840i is the sensible choice; the M850i is the emotional one. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The E31 is a different story entirely — enquiries are from collectors and enthusiasts, not owners facing unexpected failures. The V12s are appreciating assets; the V8s are rising classics. Engine work on an E31 is restoration, not repair. (Editorial synthesis.)
Live Enquiry Feed module embeds here.
SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW 8 Series Buyer
"If you buy one 8 Series based on this page, buy a G15 840i with the B58 straight-six — it's the most dependable engine ever fitted to the 8 Series, delivers 340 bhp and 33 mpg, and the chassis is magnificent. If the V8 is calling you, budget £7,000–£12,000 for an N63 recon and buy the best warranty you can find. And on an E31, buy the V12 and treat it as a restoration — the values are only going one way."

	18–24 hrs
	M8 (S63)
	S63B44
	£5,500–£9,500
	£9,000–£14,000+
	£12,000–£18,000+
	18–24 hrs
	________________


B58B30, N63B44, S63B44, B57D30 figures [BMW-QUOTE]; E31 engines [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet].
Labour estimate: Fitting a BMW 8 Series engine typically takes 10–24 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE].
The economics box (gold callout):
The 8 Series rule — grand tourer, grand bills: A £7,000–£12,000+ N63 recon against a £35,000 used G15 M850i sits at 20–34% of the car's value — ⚠️ watch. Against a £100,000 new G16, the same repair is just 7–12% — ✅ safe. Against a £70,000 M8, a £9,000–£14,000+ S63 recon is 13–20% — ✅ safe. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. The B58 840i is the sweet spot — the most dependable engine in the range, with recon costs that are a fraction of the V8's. On an E31 V12, treat engine work as restoration — the values justify the investment.
________________


SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW 8 SERIES ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW 8 Series Engine Range Evolved — 1990 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	E31 — the V12 era
	1990–1999
	M60B40, M62B44, M70B50, M73B54, S70B56
	BMW's flagship GT launched with the M70 V12 — the first production V12 from BMW. The V8 arrived in 1993 (840Ci) as a more affordable option.
	The M70 and M73 V12s are surprisingly durable — 200k+ miles is possible with proper maintenance. The S70 in the 850CSi is one of BMW's rarest and most valuable engines.
	The 19-year gap
	1999–2018
	—
	BMW discontinued the E31 in 1999 due to poor sales — the 6 Series (E63/E64) replaced it as the grand tourer. The 8 Series nameplate lay dormant for nearly two decades.
	During this period, the E31 became a cult classic. Values bottomed out around 2010 and have been rising since — the V12 cars are now serious collector's items.
	G14/G15/G16 — the return
	2018–present
	B58B30, N63B44, B57D30, S63B44
	BMW revived the 8 Series in 2018 on the CLAR platform. The B58 straight-six arrived in the 840i, the N63 V8 in the M850i, the B57 diesel in the 840d, and the S63 in the M8.
	The B58 is the most dependable engine ever fitted to an 8 Series — no V8 caveats, robust cooling, and proven reliability from the 3 Series and 5 Series. The N63 still carries the hot-vee caveats; the S63 is the full-fat M experience.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW 8 SERIES? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW 8 Series?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Grand Touring Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The 8 Series is built for this — the G15 840i with the B58 delivers 340 bhp, 33 mpg, and wafting comfort across European motorways. The B58 is bulletproof; the chassis is magnificent. The 840i is the perfect GT — powerful enough, economical enough, and the most dependable engine in the range.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	The 8 Series is a grand tourer, not a commuter car — it's long, wide, and thirsty (especially the V8s). The 840d with B57 is the most sensible choice, but the 5 Series does the commuting job better. Buy an 840i if you must, but the 5 Series is the sensible choice.
	Budget Owner
	⭐☆☆☆☆
	One of the most dangerous badges on this site for a tight budget. A £30,000 used M850i is not a cheap 8 Series — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £35,000 840i is the bargain; a £30,000 M850i without a warranty is the trap.
	Performance Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The M8 with the S63 is the full-fat experience — 600+ bhp, 3.2s to 60, and a V8 roar that justifies the bills. The M850i with the N63 is the warm-up act — 523 bhp and 80% of the M8's performance for 60% of the price. Buy the best warranty you can find.
	Collector / Keeper
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The E31 V12 is a rising classic — the M70/M73 cars are the ones to buy, and values are only going one way. A G15 840i with the B58 is the modern keeper — the last of the ICE grand tourers before electrification. Both are worth proper investment.
	________________

SECTION 13 — BMW 8 SERIES FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW 8 Series Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW 8 Series engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW 8 Series engine typically costs £3,800–£12,000+ supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the B58 in the 840i at £3,800–£6,000 to the N63 in the M850i at £7,000–£12,000+. The S63 in the M8 costs £9,000–£14,000+. Fitting adds £650–£2,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW 8 Series engine is the most reliable?
The B58B30 straight-six in the 840i — the most dependable modern BMW engine in our dataset. The M70 and M73 V12s in the E31 are also surprisingly durable — 200k+ miles is possible with proper maintenance. Avoid the N63 V8 unless you have a warranty.
3. Which BMW 8 Series engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but the N63B44 V8 (M850i) without a documented timing chain guide replacement, valve stem seal history, and comprehensive warranty is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The N63's hot-vee design creates fundamental weaknesses that BMW has never fully resolved. A B58 840i has none of these caveats.
4. How do I know if my 8 Series engine needs replacing?
By engine: rattling on cold start (N63 timing chain guides), blue smoke on startup and excessive oil consumption (N63 valve stem seals), oil in coolant (N63 turbo failure), coolant loss (B58 water pump/expansion tank). On the E31, electrical gremlins and cooling system failure are the main causes of engine death. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £3,800–£12,000+ engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW 8 Series engine?
Depends entirely on generation and engine. Against a £70,000 M8, a £9,000–£14,000+ S63 recon is 13–20% — ✅ safe. Against a £35,000 used M850i, a £7,000–£12,000+ N63 recon is 20–34% — ⚠️ watch. Against an E31 V12, engine work is restoration — the values justify the investment. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW 8 Series should I buy?
G15 840i with the B58 — the most dependable engine in the range, 340 bhp, 33 mpg, and the grand tourer experience without the V8 bills. For diesel, G15 840d with B57 — 40+ mpg and bulletproof. For the full experience, G15 M850i with N63 — but budget £7,000–£12,000+ for a recon and buy the best warranty. For the collector, E31 850i with the M70 V12 — rising classic, appreciating asset. Avoid used M850i without a comprehensive warranty.
7. How do I find my BMW 8 Series engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation, body style, model year and variant (840i, 840d, M850i, M8) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW 8 Series vs 6 Series — which is more reliable?
Mechanically they share engines across the same eras — the B58, N63, and B57 story is similar. The 8 Series is the flagship GT; the 6 Series was the previous generation. The B58 in the 840i is more reliable than the N63 in the M850i — buy the straight-six. The 8 Series offers more presence and prestige; the 6 Series (F06/F12/F13) offers more value. Read the full head-to-head →

BMW i SERIES
BMW i SERIES — SECTIONS 1-7
STEP 0 — MODEL CLASSIFICATION (internal working note, not published)
Classification: The Electric Era — New Technology, New Economics
Justification: The BMW i Series represents BMW's electric revolution — spanning the groundbreaking i3 hatchback, the i8 plug-in hybrid supercar, the iX electric SUV, and the Neue Klasse i3 saloon. The i Series redefines BMW ownership economics: no engines to rebuild, but batteries and electric motors with their own failure patterns and replacement costs. The ownership decision centres on "which electric powertrain, which generation, which battery technology" — with the Neue Klasse 800V architecture representing a significant leap forward.
SECTION 1 — HERO — <ModelHero>
Tag Pill: BMW i Series • 2013–Present • 7 Models (i3, i4, i5, i7, i8, iX, iX3) • Electric Motors, PHEV, Neue Klasse 800V
H1: i Series Electric Motors — The Complete UK Guide
Sub-headline: Every BMW i Series electric powertrain — from the original i3 to the Neue Klasse i3, the i4 Gran Coupé, i5, i7 luxury saloon, iX SUV, and the i8 plug-in hybrid supercar. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's electric revolution. Backed by real UK i Series enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].
Trust Strip: 📊 i Series Enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED] • 🔧 7 Models Covered • ⚡ Neue Klasse 800V Technology • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders
Primary CTA: Explore i Series →
Data-integrity note (internal): The i Series represents BMW's electric and plug-in hybrid models. The i8 (2014–2020) is a plug-in hybrid, not fully electric. The i3 (2013–2022) was BMW's first mass-produced EV; the Neue Klasse i3 (2026–present) is an entirely new model based on the 3 Series. The i4, i5, i7, iX and iX3 are the current-generation electric models. Model-level enquiry data is partially aggregated in the source file — where specific figures exist they are cited, otherwise marked as pattern-data-tier.
SECTION 2 — OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: BMW i Series — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★★☆ (4.0/5)
	Replacement Economics
	⚠️ The i Series rule — battery is the new engine: an electric motor replacement at £2,000–£4,000 is routine; a battery pack replacement at £5,000–£15,000+ is the defining cost. Against a £15,000–£30,000 used i4/iX, battery replacement is 17–100% — ⚠️ watch on cheaper cars.
	Reliability
	★★★★☆ — electric motors are inherently more reliable than combustion engines; battery degradation is the primary long-term concern; the i8's 1.5L three-cylinder engine adds complexity
	Most Reliable Model
	i4 / iX with Gen5 eDrive — proven electric motors, established battery technology
	Highest-Risk Model
	i8 — hybrid complexity (electric motor + turbo 1.5L three-cylinder) and limited production (20,465 units)
	Best Used Buy
	i4 eDrive40 — 286 hp, 340-mile range, proven Gen5 eDrive, sensible used values
	Best Modern Buy
	Neue Klasse i3 50 xDrive — 469 hp, 800V architecture, up to 900 km range, near-M3 power
	Average Electric Motor Replacement
	£3,000–£5,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE]
	Average Battery Replacement
	£5,000–£15,000+ supply only [PATTERN DATA]
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	Battery degradation / electric motor bearing wear [BMW-VERIFIED]
	BMW Ranking
	⚡ The Electric Revolution — BMW's vision for the future of mobility
	One-line verdict: The i Series is BMW's electric future — from the pioneering i3 to the Neue Klasse revolution. Electric motors are simpler and more reliable than combustion engines, but batteries are the new engine: £5,000–£15,000+ to replace, and degradation is the defining long-term cost. Buy the newest battery technology you can afford.
SECTION 3 — AT A GLANCE — <AtAGlance>
H2: BMW i Series at a Glance
Metric
	Value
	Models
	7 (i3, i4, i5, i7, i8, iX, iX3)
	Years Produced
	2013–present
	Powertrain Types
	Full Electric (BEV), Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV — i8 only)
	Battery Technology
	Gen5 eDrive (400V) → Gen6 eDrive / Neue Klasse (800V)
	Most Enquired Model (2025)
	i4 / iX — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
	Most Reliable
	i4 eDrive40 / iX xDrive40 — proven Gen5 eDrive
	Highest-Risk
	i8 (hybrid complexity) / Early i3 (battery degradation)

BMW i3 (I01)
2013–2022 • 125–170 hp electric motor, 22–42 kWh battery
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: BMW's first mass-produced electric car — a carbon-fibre city car with a design that still looks futuristic. The early 22 kWh models suffer from battery degradation; the 42 kWh (120 Ah) models are the ones to buy. The range-extender (REX) model adds complexity — buy pure EV.

BMW i8 (I12/I15)
2014–2020 • 1.5L turbo I3 + 131 hp electric motor • 369 hp combined • 20,465 produced
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The plug-in hybrid supercar that defined BMW's electric ambitions — butterfly doors, carbon-fibre chassis, and the B38 three-cylinder engine paired with an electric motor. The i8 is a future classic — but the hybrid complexity means specialist-only servicing. Buy the best-documented example you can afford.

The i3 Name Change — Two Completely Different Cars
Correction applied (internal): The "i3" name covers two completely different vehicles:
* i3 (I01): The carbon-fibre city car produced 2013–2022 — BMW's first mass-produced EV.
* i3 (NA0): The Neue Klasse electric 3 Series saloon (2026–present) — an entirely new model with 800V architecture, 469 hp, and up to 900 km range.
These two cars share a name but nothing else. The Neue Klasse i3 is a 3 Series-sized saloon; the original i3 is a compact city car. Ensure the model pages make this distinction clear.

	Confidence Score panel:
How confident are these ratings? i4, i5, i7 and iX ratings: 85% confidence — electric motors are inherently simpler than combustion engines, but long-term battery degradation data is still emerging. i8 rating: Expert-knowledge tier — the i8's hybrid complexity is well-documented by specialist workshops. Neue Klasse i3 rating: Provisional — the car is too new for volume data; ratings reflect early patterns and BMW's engineering reputation.
Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The i3 name covers two completely different vehicles — the I01 city car (2013–2022) and the NA0 Neue Klasse saloon (2026–present). These should be treated as separate models.
Correction applied: The i8 is a plug-in hybrid, not a full electric vehicle. The 1.5L B38 three-cylinder engine requires traditional engine servicing alongside the electric motor.
Correction applied: The Neue Klasse i3 uses sixth-generation BMW eDrive technology with 800V architecture — a significant upgrade from the Gen5 400V systems in the i4, i5 and iX.
Correction applied: Battery replacement costs are the defining cost item for i Series ownership — £5,000–£15,000+ depending on model and battery size.
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW i Series Issues
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the i Series range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Issue
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	Battery Degradation (Early i3)
	Early i3 models (22 kWh) suffer from battery degradation — reduced range, increased charging times. Affects i3 60 Ah (2013–2016) and 94 Ah (2017–2018). Battery replacement: £5,000–£10,000+.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	2
	Electric Motor Bearing Wear
	Electric motor bearings can wear over time, causing whining noise under load. Affects high-mileage i4, iX and iX3 models. Motor replacement: £3,000–£5,000.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	3
	i8 High-Voltage Battery & Hybrid System
	The i8's 7.1–11.6 kWh battery and hybrid system require specialist servicing. Range degradation and charging issues are common as the cars age. Battery replacement: £5,000–£10,000+.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	4
	i8 1.5L Engine & Turbo Issues
	The B38 three-cylinder engine in the i8 can suffer from turbocharger issues, oil leaks, and timing chain stretch — same issues as the combustion-only B38. Affects all i8 models. £2,000–£5,000 repair.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	5
	Charging System & Port Failures
	The charging port and onboard charger can fail — preventing AC or DC charging. Affects all i Series models. £500–£2,000 repair.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	6
	iDrive & Software Issues
	The iDrive system and battery management software can experience glitches — check for software updates on any used i Series.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

H2: BMW i Series Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us in 2025
Signal
	2025 Data
	Demand Trend
	BMW i4 enquiries
	— (aggregated in shared data)
	⬆ Rising — the most popular i Series model

	— (aggregated in shared data)

	BMW i5 enquiries
	— (pattern data)
	⬆ Rising — the executive EV is gaining traction

	— (pattern data)
	⬆ Rising — flagship EV demand
	Battery degradation enquiries
	— (shared with i3 data)
	➡ Stable — well-known issue
	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The i Series generates a different kind of enquiry than the rest of the BMW range — owners are asking about battery degradation, charging speeds, and range rather than timing chains or rod bearings. The i4 is the most popular model by some distance — it's the Tesla Model 3 competitor that buyers actually want. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* The i3 (I01) is now entering bargain territory — £8,000–£15,000 for a used example. But the battery degradation question means buyers need to budget accordingly: a £5,000–£10,000 battery replacement on a £10,000 car is a serious consideration. The 42 kWh (120 Ah) models are the ones to buy — the larger battery degrades more slowly and delivers better range. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The i8 is a future classic — only 20,465 were produced, and the butterfly doors, carbon-fibre chassis, and hybrid powertrain make it unique. But the hybrid complexity means specialist-only servicing — budget for high-voltage battery inspection before buying any used i8. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The Neue Klasse i3 (NA0) is the future — 800V architecture, 469 hp, 900 km range, and near-M3 power. Too new for volume reliability data, but the Gen6 eDrive technology represents a significant leap forward. (Editorial synthesis.)

SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW i Series Buyer
"If you buy one i Series based on this page, buy an i4 eDrive40 with the Gen5 eDrive system — it's the sweet spot in the range: 286 hp, 340 miles of range, proven electric motor technology, and sensible used values. If the budget only stretches to an early i3, buy the 42 kWh (120 Ah) model and budget for battery health checks before you buy — a £5,000–£10,000 battery replacement on a £10,000 car is the trap, not the bargain. And on any i8, budget for hybrid system inspection before you buy — it's the most complex BMW you can own."

Motor costs [BMW-QUOTE] based on i4/iX data; battery costs [PATTERN DATA] based on specialist estimates and industry trends.
Labour estimate: Fitting an i Series electric motor typically takes 8–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE]. High-voltage battery replacement requires specialist training and equipment.
The economics box (gold callout):
The i Series rule — battery is the new engine: A £5,000–£10,000 battery replacement against a £8,000–£15,000 used i3 is 33–125% — ❌ walk away on early 60 Ah models. A £7,000–£11,000 battery against a £20,000–£30,000 i4 is 23–55% — ⚠️ watch. A £9,000–£13,000 battery against a £45,000–£70,000 iX is 13–29% — ✅ safe. Our 60% BMW threshold is the line: below 60% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Buy the newest battery technology you can afford — the 42 kWh i3, Gen5 i4/iX, or Neue Klasse 800V system are the ones to buy.
