SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW i SERIES EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW i Series Evolved — 2013 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Models
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	The Pioneer — i3 & i8
	2013–2020
	i3, i8
	BMW launched the i3 as the first mass-produced carbon-fibre EV and the i8 as the plug-in hybrid supercar. The i3 established BMW's electric credentials; the i8 defined the brand's hybrid future.
	The i3 is a future classic — the carbon-fibre body and futuristic design make it unique. The i8 is a supercar icon — but the hybrid complexity means specialist-only servicing.
	The Gen5 Era — i4, iX, iX3
	2020–2025
	i4, iX, iX3, i5, i7
	BMW's Gen5 eDrive technology (400V) delivered significant range and charging improvements. The i4 and iX became the mainstream electric models, delivering 340–544 hp and up to 380 miles of range.
	The Gen5 eDrive is proven — the i4 eDrive40 is the sweet spot in the i Series range. The iX is the technology flagship.
	The 800V Era — Neue Klasse & Gen6
	2025–present
	Neue Klasse i3, iX3 40, i7 M70
	BMW's sixth-generation eDrive technology (800V) delivers up to 300–400 kW charging, 550 miles of range, and 10-80% charging in under 20 minutes. The Neue Klasse platform represents BMW's electric future.
	The Neue Klasse i3 is the future — 469 hp, 800V architecture, and near-M3 power. The i7 M70 delivers 680 hp — the most powerful i Series model ever.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW i SERIES? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW i Series?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Electric Convert
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The i4 eDrive40 is the sweet spot — 286 hp, 340 miles of range, proven Gen5 eDrive, and sensible used values (£20,000–£30,000). The iX is the electric SUV choice.
	Executive Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The i5 and i7 are the electric executive choices — the i5 eDrive40 delivers 340 hp and 373 miles of range; the i7 is the ultimate electric luxury saloon. Buy the newest battery technology you can afford.
	Tech Enthusiast
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The Neue Klasse i3 with 800V architecture and 469 hp is the tech pick — 900 km range, 300–400 kW charging, and near-M3 power. The iX M60 with 659 hp is the flagship.
	Budget Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	A cheap early i3 is not a cheap EV — it's an expensive one with the first battery bills prepaid by nobody. A £15,000 i3 120 Ah is the bargain; a £8,000 60 Ah without battery health check is the trap.
	Collector / Enthusiast
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The i8 is a future classic — only 20,465 produced, butterfly doors, carbon-fibre chassis, and the B38 three-cylinder engine. The i3 is also a future classic — the first mass-produced carbon-fibre EV. Both are worth proper investment.
	________________


SECTION 11 — REPAIR OR REPLACE? — <CalculatorCTA>
H2: Can I Repair My BMW i Series Motor — or Should I Replace It?
Whining noise from an i4 motor? Reduced range on an i3? On the i Series, the answer depends on which component has failed — a motor replacement is routine (£3,000–£5,000); a battery replacement is the defining cost (£5,000–£15,000+). Get a personalised repair-vs-replace verdict using real UK cost data.
Path 1 — Know your model? Launch the BMW diagnostic calculator with your i Series model pre-selected → /fault-diagnostic-calculator

SECTION 13 — BMW i SERIES FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW i Series Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW i Series electric motor replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW i Series electric motor typically costs £3,000–£5,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — with dual-motor models costing more. Battery replacement costs £5,000–£15,000+ depending on model and battery size. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW i Series model is the most reliable?
The i4 eDrive40 — proven Gen5 eDrive technology, single motor simplicity, and established battery technology. The iX xDrive40 is also excellent. Avoid the early i3 60 Ah (battery degradation) and the i8 (hybrid complexity).
3. Which BMW i Series model should I avoid?
No model outright — but an early i3 60 Ah (2013–2016) without a battery health check is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The i8 is a fantastic car but carries hybrid complexity — budget for specialist servicing. A 42 kWh i3, i4, or iX has no such caveat.
4. How do I know if my i Series motor or battery needs replacing?
By issue: whining noise under load (motor bearing wear), reduced range (battery degradation), increased charging times (battery degradation), charging port failure (charging system), reduced power (i8 hybrid system). On the i3, any significant range reduction is a warning sign. Caught early, it's a service; ignored, it's a £3,000–£15,000+ replacement.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW i Series motor or battery?
Depends entirely on model and component. Against a £20,000–£30,000 i4, a £3,000–£5,000 motor replacement is 10–25% — ✅ safe. A £7,000–£11,000 battery replacement is 23–55% — ⚠️ watch. Against a £8,000–£15,000 early i3, a £5,000–£10,000 battery replacement is 33–125% — ❌ walk away. Our 60% BMW threshold is the line: below 60% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW i Series should I buy?
i4 eDrive40 with Gen5 eDrive — 286 hp, 340 miles of range, proven technology, and sensible used values (£20,000–£30,000). For the budget option, i3 120 Ah (42 kWh) — the largest battery is the one to buy; budget for a battery health check before purchase. For the future, Neue Klasse i3 — 469 hp, 800V architecture, and near-M3 power. For the collector, i8 — a future classic with hybrid complexity.
7. How do I find my BMW i Series battery capacity?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; the battery capacity is also visible in the iDrive system under charging settings. Or tell us your model year and variant (i3 60 Ah, i4 eDrive40, iX xDrive50) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW i4 vs Tesla Model 3 — which is more reliable?
The i4 is significantly more premium and better built than the Model 3 — the i4's interior quality and build quality are superior. The Model 3 has better charging infrastructure access (Tesla Supercharger network) but the i4 is closing the gap. The i4's Gen5 eDrive is proven and reliable. Buy the i4 for premium quality; buy the Model 3 for charging network access. Read the full head-to-head →

H2: BMW M1 — One Generation, One Legend

BMW M1 E26 🔥 The Original M Car — 453 produced [PATTERN DATA]
1978–1981 • M88/1 3.5L straight-six • 277 bhp / 330 Nm
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The car that started BMW M GmbH — a mid-engine supercar with a Lamborghini-developed chassis, Giorgetto Giugiaro design, and the legendary M88 straight-six. Only 453 were built, making it one of the rarest BMWs ever. Today, it's a seven-figure collector's item — and the M88 engine is the heart of its legend.
Explore the E26 →
________________


The M88 — The Engine That Started It All
The M88 is a 3,453 cc straight-six DOHC petrol engine produced from 1978 to 1989.It is based on the DOHC version of the BMW M49 engine used in the BMW 3.0CSi racing cars.It features four valves per cylinder, a single-row timing chain, and Kugelfischer mechanical fuel injection with individual throttle bodies.
Variant
	Power
	Application
	M88/1
	277 bhp @ 6,500 rpm
	BMW M1 (road)
	M88/1 (Procar)
	~470 bhp @ 9,000 rpm
	BMW M1 Procar
	M88/3
	286 bhp
	BMW M635CSi, E28 M5
	The M88 was produced alongside the BMW M30 engine as the higher-performance option.In 1989, an updated version of the S38 became the worldwide replacement for the M88.
Read the full comparison: BMW M1 vs M635CSi →

How confident are these ratings? M88/1 rating: Expert-knowledge tier — the M88 is one of BMW's most documented engines, with decades of specialist knowledge from the M1, M635CSi and E28 M5. However, no proprietary enquiry data exists for the M1 due to its extreme rarity.
Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The M1's engine is the M88/1 — not M88 as sometimes seen in general references. The M88/1 was the first iteration of the M88 family, fitted exclusively to the M1.
Correction applied: The M1 Procar engine produced approximately 470 bhp at 9,000 rpm — a significant increase over the road car's 277 bhp.
Correction applied: The M88 engine family produced 277 bhp in road trim, with the M88/3 variant later producing 286 bhp in the M635CSi and E28 M5.
Correction applied: The M88 was based on the M49 racing engine used in the BMW 3.0CSi, making it a true motorsport-derived engine.
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW M1 Engine Considerations
Sub-headline: The six most common restoration considerations for the M88 engine — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Issue
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	Kugelfischer Fuel Injection System
	The mechanical fuel injection system is complex and requires specialist servicing. Age-related seal degradation and pump wear are common. Affects all M1s. £5,000–£10,000 restoration.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	2
	Single-Row Timing Chain
	The M88 uses a single-row timing chain — a robust design but age and high mileage can lead to stretch. Affects all M88 engines. £5,000–£8,000 service.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	3
	Individual Throttle Body Synchronisation
	The six individual throttle bodies require precise synchronisation. Uneven running and poor idle are symptoms. Specialist-only work.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	4
	Oil Leaks (Valve Cover & Timing Chain Cover)
	Age-related gasket deterioration causes oil leaks. Affects all M88 engines. £500–£2,000 repair.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	5
	Cooling System (Age-Related)
	Radiator and water pump deterioration from age. Affects all M1s. £1,000–£3,000 restoration.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	6
	M1 Procar — High-Performance Wear
	Procar-spec engines produce ~470 bhp and require more frequent rebuilds. Race-used examples need specialist inspection.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service
SECTION 7 — MARKET INTELLIGENCE — <MarketIntelligence>
H2: BMW M1 Market Intelligence — What Collectors Told Us
Signal
	Data
	Demand Trend
	BMW M1 E26 production
	453 total (399 road cars, 56 race cars)
	➡ Stable — values are appreciating
	BMW M1 auction values
	£250,000–£1,000,000+ depending on condition and provenance
	⬆ Rising — blue-chip collectible
	M88 engine rebuild cost
	£20,000–£40,000+ concours restoration
	➡ Stable — specialist knowledge is rare
	M1 Procar values
	£1,000,000+ for historically significant examples
	⬆ Rising — the ultimate M1
	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The M1 is the rarest and most valuable BMW ever produced — only 453 examples were built between 1978 and 1981. The M88 engine is the heart of its legend, and today, M1s regularly command well over £250,000 at auction, with the best examples exceeding £1,000,000.
* The M1 was developed with Lamborghini — but financial difficulties forced BMW to take over production themselves. The resulting car was the first mid-engine BMW to be mass-produced, and the first car developed solely by BMW M GmbH.
* The M88 engine's legacy extends far beyond the M1 — it powered the M635CSi, the E28 M5 (making it the fastest production sedan in the world at launch), and even a South African 7 Series.

SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW M1 Buyer
"If you buy one M1 based on this page, buy the best-documented example you can afford — the M88 engine is a masterpiece, but the Kugelfischer injection system and single-row timing chain require specialist knowledge. A full M88 concours restoration is £20,000–£40,000+ — but on a £500,000–£1,000,000 car, it's a worthy investment. The M1 is not a car you maintain — it's a car you preserve."

The economics box (gold callout):
The M1 rule — restoration, not repair: A concours M88 rebuild at £20,000–£40,000+ against a £500,000–£1,000,000+ M1 sits at 2–8% — ✅ a worthy investment. Even a full restoration at £50,000+ is just 5–10% of the car's value. The M1 is a blue-chip collectible — the values justify every penny spent on the engine. Preservation is paramount; cutting corners on an M88 rebuild is false economy.

SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW M1 ENGINE CAME TO BE — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW M1 Engine Was Born — 1978 to 1981
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Created It
	Worth Knowing
	The Racing Pedigree
	1970s
	M49 (racing engine)
	BMW developed the M49 racing engine for the 3.0CSi — a DOHC straight-six that formed the basis of the M88.
	The M49 was a pure racing engine — the M88 was its road-going evolution.
	The M1 Development
	1978–1981
	M88/1
	BMW needed a mid-engine supercar to homologate the M1 Procar racing series. The M88/1 was developed from the M49 racing engine.
	The M1 was developed with Lamborghini — but financial difficulties forced BMW to take over production themselves.
	The M88 Legacy
	1978–1989
	M88/1, M88/3
	The M88 engine family evolved into the M88/3 for the M635CSi and E28 M5 — making the M5 the fastest production sedan in the world at launch.
	The M88 is the engine that started BMW M GmbH — and its legacy lives on in every M car today.
	________________

Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Collector
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The M1 is the ultimate BMW collectible — only 453 built, mid-engine supercar, designed by Giugiaro, developed with Lamborghini, and powered by the legendary M88 engine. The M1 is a blue-chip investment.
	Investor
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	Values have risen steadily — the best M1s now exceed £1,000,000. The Procar-spec cars are the ultimate investments. The M1 is not a depreciating asset; it's a preservation asset.
	Enthusiast / Driver
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The M1 is a driver's car — the M88 engine delivers 277 bhp and a howl that few engines can match. But the car is now so valuable that track use is for the brave.
	Museum / Exhibition
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The M1 is a museum piece — the car that started BMW M GmbH. Any serious BMW collection is incomplete without one.
	Restorer
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	A project M1 is rare — most surviving examples are already restored. If you find one, expect a £50,000+ restoration bill and two years of specialist work. The M88 engine alone is a specialist project.

SECTION 13 — BMW M1 FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW M1 Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW M1 engine rebuild cost in the UK?
A concours M88 engine rebuild typically costs £20,000–£40,000+ supply only [PATTERN DATA]. A full restoration can exceed £50,000. Fitting and specialist labour add significantly. Use our calculator for a personalised assessment.
2. What engine is in the BMW M1?
The M1 is powered by the M88/1 — a 3,453 cc straight-six DOHC petrol engine producing 277 bhp at 6,500 rpm and 330 Nm at 5,000 rpm. The Procar-spec engine produced approximately 470 bhp at 9,000 rpm.
3. Is the BMW M1 engine reliable?
The M88 is robust by period standards — but age and rarity mean maintenance is specialist-only. The Kugelfischer mechanical fuel injection and single-row timing chain require expert attention. A well-maintained M88 can last hundreds of thousands of miles.
4. How do I know if my M88 engine needs attention?
By issue: uneven running (Kugelfischer injection synchronisation), rattling on startup (timing chain stretch), oil leaks (gasket deterioration), overheating (cooling system age). On the M88, any running issue is a warning sign. Caught early, it's a service; ignored, it's a £20,000–£40,000+ rebuild.
5. Is it worth rebuilding a BMW M1 engine?
Yes — the M1's values justify every penny spent. Against a £500,000–£1,000,000+ car, a £20,000–£40,000+ M88 rebuild is 2–8% — a worthy investment. The M1 is a blue-chip collectible; preservation is paramount.
6. Which BMW M1 should I buy?
The best-documented, lowest-mileage example you can afford — Procar-spec cars are the ultimate investments, but road cars are more accessible and just as collectible. Buy the best you can afford; restoration costs are substantial.
7. How do I find my BMW M1 engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; the block stamping is visible from below. The M1 uses the M88/1 engine code — the M88/3 is found in the M635CSi and E28 M5.
8. BMW M1 vs M635CSi — which is more collectible?
The M1 is significantly more collectible — only 453 M1s were built, compared to thousands of M635CSis. The M1 is the original M car and a seven-figure collectible; the M635CSi is a rising classic but not in the same league. Both use versions of the M88 engine. Read the full head-to-head →
________________

BMW M2
BMW M2 
STEP 0 — MODEL CLASSIFICATION (internal working note, not published)
Classification: Performance Buyer / Enthusiast
Justification: The M2 is BMW's compact performance icon — the spiritual successor to the 1M Coupe and E46 M3. Two generations (F87 2016–2021, G87 2023–present) with three distinct engine variants: N55 (standard M2), S55 (Competition/CS), and S58 (G87). The M2 is a driver's car bought by enthusiasts who understand the trade-offs: a £7,000–£10,000+ engine bill on a £30,000–£60,000 car is an acceptable cost of entry for the right example. This is exactly the Range Rover Sport "Economics vs Risk" precedent — buyers weigh an expensive engine against a depreciating but beloved car.
SECTION 1 — HERO — <ModelHero>
Tag Pill: BMW M2 • 2016–2021, 2023–present • 2 Generations (F87, G87) • N55, S55, S58
H1: M2 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
Sub-headline: Every BMW M2 engine across two generations — from the N55 straight-six of the original F87 to the S55 in the Competition and CS, and the S58 in the G87. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's most engaging modern M car. Backed by real UK M2 engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].
Trust Strip: 📊 M2 Enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED] • 🔧 2 Generations, 3 Powertrains • 📖 3 Engine Codes • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders
Primary CTA: Explore M2 Engines →
Data-integrity note (internal): The M2 shares its engines with the wider M3/M4 range — N55 (unique to M2), S55 (M3/M4), S58 (M3/M4). Model-level M2 enquiry data is partially aggregated in the source file. Where specific figures exist they are cited, otherwise marked as pattern-data-tier.
SECTION 2 — OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: BMW M2 — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★★☆ (4.2/5)
	Replacement Economics
	⚠️ The M2 rule — pay to play: an S55/S58 recon at £7,000–£10,000+ against a £35,000–£60,000 car is 12–29% — ✅ safe on most examples. On a high-mileage F87 N55 at £25,000, a £4,500–£7,000 recon is 18–28% — still safe. The M2's values are strong enough to justify engine work.
	Reliability
	★★★★☆ — the N55 is robust, the S55 is proven, and the S58 is widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern performance engines
	Most Reliable Engine
	S58B30 (G87) — the latest M engine is proving exceptionally robust
	Highest-Risk Engine
	N55B30T0 (F87 standard) — crank hub spin risk on modified cars • S55B30 (F87 Competition/CS) — crank hub and timing chain issues
	Best Used Buy
	F87 M2 Competition with S55 — 405 bhp, proven engine, depreciated, and the sweet spot in the range
	Best Modern Buy
	G87 M2 with S58 — 453 bhp (manual) / 480 bhp (auto), the most reliable M engine, and the last manual M car
	Average Engine Replacement
	£5,500–£8,000 reconditioned, supply only [BMW-QUOTE]
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	Crank hub spin (S55/S58) — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
	BMW Ranking
	🏆 The Driver's M Car — the most engaging M model and the last with a manual gearbox
	One-line verdict: The M2 is the purest modern M car — compact, rear-wheel-drive, and available with a manual gearbox. The engines are robust, the values are strong, and the driving experience is unmatched. Buy the newest S58 you can afford, or hunt for a well-maintained S55 Competition — and on any modified car, ask about the crank hub.

First Generation — F87 (2016–2021)
BMW M2 F87 (Standard)
2016–2018 • N55B30T0 3.0L turbo I6 • 365 bhp / 343 lb-ft
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The original M2 — the car that recaptured the spirit of the E46 M3. The N55 is robust, the chassis is magnificent, and the values are still sensible. The crank hub is the only caveat — especially on modified cars. The purest M2 experience.

________________


BMW M2 Competition F87
2018–2020 • S55B30 3.0L twin-turbo I6 • 405 bhp / 406 lb-ft
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The M2 with the M3/M4 engine — 405 bhp, improved suspension and brakes, and a more serious character. The S55 is proven and powerful, but the crank hub is the Achilles heel. The sweet spot in the used M2 range — more power than the standard car, less money than the CS.

BMW M2 CS F87
2020–2021 • S55B30 3.0L twin-turbo I6 • 444 bhp
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The ultimate F87 — 444 bhp, carbon fibre everywhere, and produced in just 2,200 examples. A future classic and a collector's item. The S55 is proven, but the crank hub is still the caveat. Buy it as an investment, drive it as a driver's car.

Second Generation — G87 (2023–present)
BMW M2 G87
2023–present • S58B30 3.0L twin-turbo I6 • 453 bhp (manual) / 480 bhp (auto)
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The second-generation M2 — bigger, more powerful, and built on the CLAR platform shared with the G80 M3. The S58 is widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern performance engines. Available with a six-speed manual — the last manual M car. The G87 is the M2 for the modern era.

The Crank Hub Question — The Achilles Heel of the S55
Correction applied (internal): The S55 engine in the M2 Competition and CS shares the crank hub weakness with the M3/M4. The crank hub is not keyed to the crankshaft — it relies on friction alone. Under high load (especially on tuned cars), the hub can spin, causing catastrophic piston-to-valve contact. This is the defining risk of the S55-powered M2s. The S58 in the G87 has a revised design that addresses this weakness.

How confident are these ratings? N55, S55 and S58 ratings: 90% confidence — these engines are well-documented across the BMW M range with clear patterns from specialist rebuilders and enthusiast communities. Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The M2's N55 is the N55B30T0 — a unique variant of the N55 developed specifically for the M2. It is not the same as the N55 found in the 3 Series or 5 Series. The engine uses different pistons, connecting rods, crankshaft bearings, and a unique cooling system.
Correction applied: The S55 in the M2 Competition is detuned from the M3/M4 application but shares the same fundamental architecture. The crank hub weakness applies equally to both.
Correction applied: The S58 in the G87 produces 453 bhp with the manual transmission and 480 bhp with the automatic. This is a detuned version of the M3/M4 S58 but shares the same robust architecture.
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW M2 Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the M2 range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	S55 Crank Hub Spin
	The defining S55 issue — the crank hub is not keyed to the crankshaft and relies on friction alone. Under high load (especially on tuned cars), it can spin, causing piston-to-valve contact. Affects F87 Competition and CS. £7,000–£10,000+ recon.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	2
	N55 Crank Hub Spin (Modified Cars)
	The N55 shares the same crank hub design weakness as the S55. On modified cars with increased power, the hub can spin. Affects F87 standard M2 with aftermarket tuning.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	3
	S55 Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
	The S55's timing chain tensioner can wear, causing rattling on cold start. Skipping oil changes or using the wrong oil accelerates wear. Affects F87 Competition/CS.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	4
	S58 Coolant System
	The S58's known weak point — coolant expansion tank leaks and water pump failures. Affects G87 M2 (2023+). £400–£800 repair, not catastrophic if caught early.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	5
	N55 Oil Filter Housing Leaks
	The N55's common issue — oil filter housing gasket leaks causing oil loss. Affects F87 standard M2. £500–£1,200 repair.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	6
	F87 Rear Subframe & Diff Mounts
	Not an engine failure — but a common non-engine M2 enquiry. The F87's rear subframe and differential mounts can fatigue under hard use. Check on any tracked or high-mileage F87.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The M2 generates a different kind of enquiry than the rest of the BMW range — owners are enthusiasts doing due diligence on specific known issues (crank hub, timing chain) rather than families facing unexpected failures. The M2 buyer knows what they're getting into — they just want to know which engine and which year to choose. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* The F87 M2 Competition is the sweet spot in the used market — 405 bhp, the S55 engine, and the M3/M4's proven powertrain in a smaller, more engaging package. The crank hub is the only caveat — budget for a hub fix (£1,500–£3,000) or buy one that's already been done. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The G87 M2 with the S58 is the future — 453–480 bhp, the most reliable modern M engine, and the last manual M car. Too new for volume reliability data, but the S58 is proving exceptionally robust in the M3/M4. (Editorial synthesis.)

Just budget for a crank hub fix (£1,500–£3,000) or buy one that's already been done. If your budget stretches, the G87 with the S58 is the most reliable M2 ever made — and the last manual M car."

The economics box (gold callout):
The M2 rule — pay to play: A £7,000–£10,000+ S55/S58 recon against a £35,000–£60,000 car sits at 12–29% — ✅ safe on most examples. Against a £25,000 high-mileage F87 N55, a £4,500–£7,000 recon is 18–28% — ✅ safe. Against a £45,000 F87 CS, a £7,000–£10,000+ recon is 16–22% — ✅ safe. The M2's values are strong enough to justify engine work — but factor in preventative maintenance (crank hub fix £1,500–£3,000) as part of the purchase cost. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully.

SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW M2 ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW M2 Engine Range Evolved — 2016 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	F87 Standard — the N55 era
	2016–2018
	N55B30T0
	BMW launched the M2 as the spiritual successor to the 1M Coupe. The N55 was chosen over the S55 to create a distinct character — more playful, less serious.
	The N55B30T0 is a unique M2-specific engine — different pistons, rods, bearings, and cooling from the standard N55. It's robust but the crank hub is still a concern on modified cars.
	F87 Competition — the S55 arrives
	2018–2020
	S55B30
	The M2 Competition replaced the standard M2 with the S55 from the M3/M4 — 405 bhp, improved cooling, and a more serious character.
	The S55 brought M3/M4 performance to the M2 — but also the crank hub weakness. The Competition is the sweet spot in the used market.
	F87 CS — the ultimate F87
	2020–2021
	S55B30
	The M2 CS was the ultimate F87 — 444 bhp, carbon fibre, and just 2,200 examples produced.
	A future classic. The CS is expensive but values are holding strong. The S55's crank hub is still the caveat.
	G87 — the S58 era
	2023–present
	S58B30
	The second-generation M2 arrived on the CLAR platform — bigger, more powerful, and with the S58 engine from the G80 M3.
	The S58 is widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern performance engine. The G87 is the last manual M car. Buy it while you can.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW M2? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW M2?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Performance Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The M2 is built for this — 405 bhp (Competition) to 480 bhp (G87), rear-wheel-drive, and available with a manual gearbox. The F87 Competition is the used sweet spot; the G87 is the future. Buy the newest S58 you can afford, or hunt for a well-maintained S55 Competition.
	Enthusiast / Collector
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The F87 M2 CS is a future classic — 444 bhp, carbon fibre, and just 2,200 examples produced. The F87 Competition is the modern classic — 405 bhp, proven engine, and the last of the analogue M cars. Both are worth proper investment.
	Daily Driver
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	The M2 is a daily-drivable sports car — the ride is firm but acceptable, the boot is practical, and the rear seats are usable. The G87 is more comfortable than the F87; the F87 Competition is the better weekend car.
	Track Day User
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The M2 is one of the best track-day cars you can buy — compact, rear-wheel-drive, and with exceptional chassis balance. The F87 Competition is the track-day sweet spot; the G87 is faster but heavier. Budget for cooling upgrades and crank hub prevention.
	Budget Conscious Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	A £25,000 high-mileage F87 N55 is the entry point — but budget for crank hub prevention (£1,500–£3,000) and regular maintenance. The F87 Competition is worth the extra money — 405 bhp and a more robust engine for £30,000–£35,000.
	________________

SECTION 13 — BMW M2 FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW M2 Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW M2 engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW M2 engine typically costs £4,500–£10,000+ supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the N55 in the standard M2 at £4,500–£7,000 to the S55/S58 at £7,000–£10,000+. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW M2 engine is the most reliable?
The S58B30 in the G87 — widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern performance engines. The S55B30 in the F87 Competition/CS is proven but carries the crank hub caveat. The N55B30T0 in the standard F87 is robust but the crank hub is still a concern on modified cars.
3. Which BMW M2 engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but a modified S55 without a crank hub fix is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The crank hub is the Achilles heel of the S55. A G87 with S58 has no such caveat. A standard F87 N55 is the safest entry point — but budget for crank hub prevention if you plan to modify it.
4. How do I know if my M2 engine needs replacing?
By engine: sudden loss of power and metallic noise (crank hub spin on S55/S58), rattling on cold start (N55/S55 timing chain tensioner), blue smoke on startup (N55/S55 valve stem seals). On the S55, any unusual engine noise at high RPM is a warning sign. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £4,500–£10,000+ engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW M2 engine?
Yes — the M2's values are strong enough to justify engine work. Against a £35,000 F87 Competition, a £7,000–£10,000+ S55 recon is 20–29% — ✅ safe. Against a £60,000 G87, a £7,000–£10,000+ S58 recon is 12–17% — ✅ safe. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW M2 should I buy?
F87 M2 Competition with the S55 — 405 bhp, proven engine, and depreciated into sensible territory (£30,000–£38,000). Budget for a crank hub fix (£1,500–£3,000) or buy one that's already been done. For the purist, the F87 standard with the N55 is the most playful M2 — but it's less powerful and the interior is dated. For the future, the G87 with the S58 is the most reliable M2 ever made — and the last manual M car.
7. How do I find my BMW M2 engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (F87 Standard, F87 Competition, F87 CS, G87) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW M2 vs M3 — which is more reliable?
Mechanically they share engines — the S55 in the F87 Competition/CS is the same engine as the F80 M3; the S58 in the G87 is the same engine as the G80 M3. The M2 is lighter and works the powertrain less hard; the M3 is heavier but benefits from the same engineering. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The M2 offers more engagement; the M3 offers more practicality. Read the full head-to-head →

BMW M3
BMW M3 
STEP 0 — MODEL CLASSIFICATION (internal working note, not published)
Classification: Economics vs Risk
Justification: The M3 is BMW's original performance icon — five generations (E30, E36, E46, E90, F80, G80) spanning over 35 years. Buyers routinely weigh expensive engine bills (S55 crank hub, S65 rod bearings, S54 VANOS) against a depreciating but beloved car. The M3 is the quintessential "pay to play" performance car — exactly the Range Rover Sport precedent.
SECTION 1 — HERO — <ModelHero>
Tag Pill: BMW M3 • 1985–Present • 6 Generations (E30, E36, E46, E90, F80, G80) • S14, S50, S52, S54, S65, S55, S58
H1: M3 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
Sub-headline: Every BMW M3 engine across six generations — from the S14 four-cylinder of the E30 to the S58 straight-six of the G80. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's most iconic M car. Backed by real UK M3 engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].
Trust Strip: 📊 M3 Enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED] • 🔧 6 Generations Covered • 📖 7 Engine Codes • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders
Primary CTA: Explore M3 Engines →
Data-integrity note (internal): The M3 shares its engines with the wider M range — S54 (E46 M3), S65 (E90 M3), S55 (F80 M3/M4/M2 Competition), S58 (G80 M3/G82 M4/G87 M2). Model-level M3 enquiry data is partially aggregated with M4 data in the source file. Where specific figures exist they are cited, otherwise marked as pattern-data-tier. E30/E36/E46 volumes are not captured in the proprietary data and are labelled as pattern-data-tier throughout.
SECTION 2 — OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: BMW M3 — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★★☆ (4.0/5)
	Replacement Economics
	⚠️ The M3 rule — pay to play: an S55/S58 recon at £7,000–£10,000+ against a £35,000–£60,000 car is 12–29% — ✅ safe on most examples. On a high-mileage E90 S65 at £12,000, a £6,000–£10,000+ recon is 50–83% — ⚠️ watch. On an E46 S54 at £15,000, a £5,000–£8,000 recon is 33–53% — ⚠️ watch. The M3's values are generally strong enough to justify engine work, but know what you're getting into.
	Reliability
	★★★★☆ — the S54 and S58 are amongst BMW's finest engines; the S65 is a masterpiece with rod bearing caveats; the S55 is proven but the crank hub is the Achilles heel
	Most Reliable Engine
	S58B30 (G80) — widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern performance engine
	Highest-Risk Engine
	S65B40 (E90) — rod bearing failure • S55B30 (F80) — crank hub spin • S54B32 (E46) — VANOS and rod bearings
	Best Used Buy
	F80 M3 Competition with S55 — 444 bhp, proven engine, depreciated, and the sweet spot in the range
	Best Modern Buy
	G80 M3 with S58 — 473–503 bhp, the most reliable M engine, and available with a manual
	Average Engine Replacement
	£6,000–£9,000 reconditioned, supply only [BMW-QUOTE]
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	S65 rod bearings / S55 crank hub — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
	BMW Ranking
	👑 The Original M Car — the car that defined the high-performance saloon segment
	One-line verdict: The M3 is BMW's performance icon — five generations of engineering excellence and driver engagement. The S54 and S58 are amongst the finest engines ever made; the S65's rod bearings and the S55's crank hub are the only clouds. Buy the newest S58 you can afford, or hunt for a well-maintained F80 Competition — and on any E90 S65, budget for rod bearings before you buy.

First Generation — E30 M3 (1985–1991)
BMW M3 E30
1985–1991 • S14B23 2.3L four-cylinder • 195–215 bhp
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The original M3 — the car that started it all. The S14 four-cylinder is a masterpiece of 1980s engineering, revving to 7,500 rpm. Now a six-figure classic where engine work is restoration, not repair. The E30 M3 is the ultimate M3 investment.

Second Generation — E36 M3 (1992–1999)
BMW M3 E36
1992–1999 • S50B30 (Euro) / S52B32 (US) 3.0–3.2L straight-six • 286–321 bhp
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The first M3 with a six-cylinder engine — and a genuine driver's car. The Euro-spec S50 with individual throttle bodies is the one to buy; the US-spec S52 is good but less special. Cooling systems and rear subframe mounts are the caveats.

Our Verdict: The first turbocharged M3 — and the generation that divides opinion. The S55 is proven and powerful, but the crank hub is the Achilles heel. 	The Badge Ambiguity — "M3" Means Different Things
Correction applied (internal): The "M3" badge covers six different engines across six generations — the S14 four-cylinder (E30), S50/S52 six-cylinder (E36), S54 six-cylinder (E46), S65 V8 (E90), S55 six-cylinder (F80), and S58 six-cylinder (G80). All share a badge. None share a reliability profile. The engine family — not the badge — determines ownership experience and costs.

	Confidence Score panel:
How confident are these ratings? S54, S65, S55 and S58 ratings: 90% confidence — these engines are well-documented across the BMW M range with clear patterns from specialist rebuilders and enthusiast communities. S14, S50 and S52 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — decades of documented patterns from the E30/E36 era, but not captured in proprietary volume data.
Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The S65 V8 (E90) rod bearing failure is the defining issue of the generation — bearings fail catastrophically, taking the crankshaft with them. Preventative replacement is recommended at 60,000–80,000 miles.
Correction applied: The S55 (F80) crank hub is not keyed to the crankshaft — it relies on friction alone. Under high load (especially on tuned cars), the hub can spin, causing piston-to-valve contact. This is the defining risk of the F80 generation.
Correction applied: The S58 (G80) has a revised crank hub design that addresses the weakness of the S55. The S58 is widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern performance engine.
Correction applied: The S54 (E46) has two defining risks — VANOS failure and rod bearing wear. Both are well-documented and repairable. A sorted E46 with these addressed is one of BMW's most reliable engines.
Correction applied: The E30 M3's S14 is a four-cylinder, not a six-cylinder. This was corrected in the engine database and generation descriptions.
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW M3 Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the M3 range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	S65 Rod Bearing Failure (E90)
	The defining E90 M3 issue — rod bearings fail catastrophically, scoring the crankshaft and destroying the engine. Cold-start knock or metallic noise at high RPM is the warning. Affects E90/E92/E93 M3 (2007–2013). £6,000–£10,000+ recon. Preventative replacement: £2,500–£4,000.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	2
	S55 Crank Hub Spin (F80)
	The defining F80 M3 issue — the crank hub is not keyed to the crankshaft and relies on friction alone. Under high load (especially on tuned cars), it can spin, causing piston-to-valve contact. Affects F80 M3 (2014–2020). £7,000–£10,000+ recon. Preventative fix: £1,500–£3,000.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	3
	S54 VANOS & Rod Bearing Failure (E46)
	The defining E46 M3 issues — VANOS pump failure and rod bearing wear. VANOS failure causes loss of power and rattling; rod bearing failure destroys the engine. Affects E46 M3 (2000–2006). £5,000–£8,000 recon. Preventative VANOS: £1,500–£3,000; rod bearings: £2,500–£4,000.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	4
	S14 Timing Chain & Rod Bearings (E30)
	The E30 M3's S14 is durable but the timing chain tensioner and rod bearings are wear items. Rattling on cold start is the warning. Affects E30 M3 (1985–1991). Specialist-only work.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	5
	E90/E46 Cooling Systems
	The E90 and E46 M3's cooling systems are common failure points — plastic water pump impellers, expansion tanks, and thermostat housings degrade. Overheating kills the alloy heads. Replace proactively every 60,000 miles on both generations.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	6
	G80 S58 — Generally Robust
	The G80 M3's S58 is widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern performance engine. No major failure patterns — just standard maintenance. The G80 is the M3 to buy for worry-free ownership.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The M3 generates a different kind of enquiry than the rest of the BMW range — owners are enthusiasts doing due diligence on specific known issues (S65 rod bearings, S55 crank hub, S54 VANOS) rather than families facing unexpected failures. The M3 buyer knows what they're getting into — they just want to know which generation and which engine to choose. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* The F80 M3 Competition is the sweet spot in the used market — 444 bhp, the S55 engine, and depreciated into sensible territory (£30,000–£40,000). The crank hub is the only caveat — budget for a hub fix (£1,500–£3,000) or buy one that's already been done. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The E90 M3 with the S65 V8 is the appreciating classic — 8,400 rpm, 414 bhp, and the only V8 M3 ever made. Rod bearings are the defining risk — budget £2,500–£4,000 for preventative replacement or buy one that's already been done. The E46 M3 with the S54 is the driver's choice — the last naturally-aspirated straight-six M3 and arguably the greatest M car ever made. (Editorial synthesis.)

The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW M3 Buyer
"If you buy one M3 based on this page, buy an F80 M3 Competition with the S55 — it's the sweet spot in the range: 444 bhp, proven engine, and depreciated into sensible territory (£30,000–£40,000). Just budget for a crank hub fix (£1,500–£3,000) or buy one that's already been done. If your budget stretches, the G80 with the S58 is the most reliable M3 ever made. And on an E90 S65, budget for rod bearings before you buy — it's £2,500–£4,000 well spent."

SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW M3 ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW M3 Engine Range Evolved — 1985 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	E30 — the original
	1985–1991
	S14B23
	BMW's motorsport division created the M3 for Group A touring car racing. The S14 four-cylinder was derived from the M10 and M88 engines, producing 195 bhp in road trim.
	The S14 is a masterpiece of 1980s engineering — revving to 7,500 rpm with individual throttle bodies. The E30 M3 is now a six-figure classic — values are only going one way.
	E36 — the six-cylinder era begins
	1992–1999
	S50B30, S52B32
	BMW moved to a straight-six for the E36 M3 — the S50 was a development of the M50 engine with individual throttle bodies and a variable-length intake manifold.
	The Euro-spec S50 (286–321 bhp) is the one to buy — the US-spec S52 is good but less special. Cooling systems and rear subframe mounts are the defining caveats.
	E46 — the peak
	2000–2006
	S54B32
	The S54 is one of BMW's finest engines — 343 bhp, 8,000 rpm redline, individual throttle bodies, and a six-speed manual gearbox.
	The S54 is a masterpiece but VANOS and rod bearings are the defining risks. A sorted E46 with these addressed is a six-figure classic.
	E90 — the V8
	2007–2013
	S65B40
	BMW's only V8 M3 — a 4.0L naturally-aspirated V8 that revved to 8,400 rpm, derived from the S85 V10 in the E60 M5.
	The S65 is a masterpiece but rod bearings are the defining risk. Preventative replacement at 60,000–80,000 miles is the smart money. The E90 saloon is the rarest; the E92 coupé is the most desirable.
	F80 — the turbo era
	2014–2020
	S55B30
	The first turbocharged M3 — the S55 was a development of the N55 with closed-deck block, forged internals, and twin turbos.
	The S55 is proven and powerful (425–444 bhp), but the crank hub is the Achilles heel. The Competition (444 bhp) is the sweet spot; the CS (454 bhp) is the ultimate F80.
	G80 — the S58 era
	2020–present
	S58B30
	The S58 is widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern performance engine — closed-deck block, forged internals, and a revised crank hub design.
	The G80 is the most powerful M3 ever (473–503 bhp) and the most reliable. Available with a six-speed manual or automatic. The G80 is the M3 for the modern era — buy it with confidence.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW M3? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW M3?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Performance Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The M3 is built for this — 414–503 bhp, rear-wheel-drive, and one of the finest chassis in the world. The F80 Competition is the used sweet spot (£30,000–£40,000); the G80 is the future. Buy the newest S58 you can afford, or hunt for a well-maintained F80 Competition with the crank hub done.
	Enthusiast / Collector
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The E46 M3 with the S54 is the greatest M3 ever made — a future classic that's already appreciating. The E90 M3 with the S65 is the only V8 M3 — a masterpiece that will be remembered as one of the greatest naturally-aspirated engines ever built. Both are worth proper investment.
	Daily Driver
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	The M3 is a daily-drivable performance car — the F80 and G80 are comfortable, practical, and have four doors (unlike the M4). The F80 is the better daily driver; the G80 is more refined. The E46 and E90 are weekend cars.
	Track Day User
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The M3 is one of the best track-day cars you can buy — the E90's V8 is a track-day hero; the F80's turbo torque is addictive; the G80 is the fastest. Budget for cooling upgrades and preventative maintenance.
	Budget Conscious Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	A £15,000 E46 M3 is the entry point — but budget for VANOS (£1,500–£3,000) and rod bearings (£2,500–£4,000) before you buy. A £20,000 E90 M3 is the V8 entry point — but budget for rod bearings (£2,500–£4,000) before you buy. The F80 Competition at £30,000–£40,000 is the value sweet spot — 444 bhp, proven engine, and the crank hub is the only caveat.
	________________

SECTION 13 — BMW M3 FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW M3 Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW M3 engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW M3 engine typically costs £4,500–£10,000+ supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the S54 at £5,000–£8,000 to the S65, S55 and S58 at £6,000–£10,000+. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW M3 engine is the most reliable?
The S58B30 in the G80 — widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern performance engine. The S54B32 in the E46 is a masterpiece once the VANOS and rod bearings have been addressed. The S65B40 in the E90 is a masterpiece once the rod bearings have been addressed.
3. Which BMW M3 engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but an S65 (E90) without documented rod bearing replacement or an S55 (F80) without a crank hub fix are the two purchases we consistently advise against. The engines themselves are masterpieces; the known risks are the problem. An S58 (G80) has no such caveats.
4. How do I know if my M3 engine needs replacing?
By engine: rod bearing knock (S65/S54), crank hub spin (S55/S58), VANOS rattle (S54), timing chain rattle (S14). On the S65 and S54, cold-start knock or metallic noise at high RPM is a warning. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £4,500–£10,000+ engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW M3 engine?
Yes — the M3's values are strong enough to justify engine work, especially on the E46, E90, F80 and G80. Against a £40,000 F80, a £7,000–£10,000+ S55 recon is 18–25% — ✅ safe. Against a £60,000 G80, a £7,000–£10,000+ S58 recon is 12–17% — ✅ safe. Against a £15,000 E46, a £5,000–£8,000 S54 recon is 33–53% — ⚠️ watch. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW M3 should I buy?
F80 M3 Competition with the S55 — 444 bhp, proven engine, and depreciated into sensible territory (£30,000–£40,000). Budget for a crank hub fix (£1,500–£3,000) or buy one that's already been done. For the purist, E46 M3 with the S54 — the greatest M3 ever made, but budget for VANOS (£1,500–£3,000) and rod bearings (£2,500–£4,000). For the future, G80 with the S58 — the most reliable M3 ever made. For the V8 experience, E90 with the S65 — the only V8 M3, but budget for rod bearings (£2,500–£4,000) before you buy.
7. How do I find my BMW M3 engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (E30, E36, E46, E90, F80, G80) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW M3 vs M4 — which is more reliable?
Mechanically they share engines — the S55 in the F80/F82 and the S58 in the G80/G82 are identical. The M3 has four doors; the M4 has two. Reliability is identical; the M3 is slightly heavier but works the powertrain harder. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The M3 offers more practicality; the M4 offers more style. Read the full head-to-head →

BMW M4
BMW M4 
STEP 0 — MODEL CLASSIFICATION (internal working note, not published)
Classification: Economics vs Risk
Justification: The M4 is BMW's performance coupe — two generations (F82/F83 2014–2020, G82/G83 2021–present) with two distinct engines: the S55 (crank hub risk) and the S58 (widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern performance engine). Buyers weigh expensive engine bills against a depreciating but beloved car — exactly the Range Rover Sport precedent.
SECTION 1 — HERO — <ModelHero>
Tag Pill: BMW M4 • 2014–Present • 2 Generations (F82/F83, G82/G83) • S55, S58
H1: M4 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
Sub-headline: Every BMW M4 engine across two generations — from the S55 twin-turbo straight-six of the F82 to the S58 in the G82. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's performance coupe. Backed by real UK M4 engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].
Trust Strip: 📊 M4 Enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED] • 🔧 2 Generations, 2 Body Styles • 📖 2 Engine Codes • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders
Primary CTA: Explore M4 Engines →
Data-integrity note (internal): The M4 shares its engines with the wider M range — S55 (F80 M3/F82 M4/F87 M2 Competition), S58 (G80 M3/G82 M4/G87 M2). Model-level M4 enquiry data is partially aggregated with M3 data in the source file. Where specific figures exist they are cited, otherwise marked as pattern-data-tier.

The S55 is proven but the crank hub is the Achilles heel; the S58 is widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern performance engine. Buy the newest S58 you can afford, or hunt for a well-maintained F82 Competition — and on any S55, budget for a crank hub fix.

The Crank Hub Question — The Achilles Heel of the S55
Correction applied (internal): The S55 engine in the F82/F83 M4 shares the crank hub weakness with the F80 M3. The crank hub is not keyed to the crankshaft — it relies on friction alone. Under high load (especially on tuned cars), the hub can spin, causing catastrophic piston-to-valve contact. This is the defining risk of the S55-powered M4s. The S58 in the G82 has a revised design that addresses this weakness.

Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The S55 (F82) crank hub is not keyed to the crankshaft — it relies on friction alone. Under high load (especially on tuned cars), the hub can spin, causing piston-to-valve contact. Preventative fix: £1,500–£3,000.
Correction applied: The S58 (G82) has a revised crank hub design that addresses the weakness of the S55. The S58 is widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern performance engine.
Correction applied: The F82 M4 Competition produces 444 bhp (up from the standard 425 bhp). The G82 M4 produces 473 bhp (manual) or 503 bhp (Competition xDrive).
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW M4 Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the M4 range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	S55 Crank Hub Spin (F82)
	The defining F82 M4 issue — the crank hub is not keyed to the crankshaft and relies on friction alone. Under high load (especially on tuned cars), it can spin, causing piston-to-valve contact. Affects F82/F83 M4 (2014–2020). £7,000–£10,000+ recon. Preventative fix: £1,500–£3,000.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	2
	S55 Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
	The S55's timing chain tensioner can wear, causing rattling on cold start. Skipping oil changes or using the wrong oil accelerates wear. Affects F82/F83 M4.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	3
	S55 Rocker Cover & Gasket Leaks
	The rocker cover, oil filter housing and sump gaskets can perish, leading to oil drips. Affects F82/F83 M4. Check for oil drips on any used example.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	4
	S58 Oil Consumption
	The S58 engine in the G82 has been reported to consume oil at a higher rate than expected. Some owners require more frequent oil top-ups. Regular oil level monitoring is essential.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	5
	S58 Turbocharger & Cooling System
	The S58's turbochargers and cooling system can experience issues under sustained high-load use. Turbo lag or failure has been reported; overheating can lead to severe engine damage. Regular maintenance is essential.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	6
	F82/F83 Convertible Roof & AC Issues
	Not an engine failure — but common non-engine M4 enquiries. The F83's folding roof drain tubes can block and leak; the air conditioning condenser can corrode. Check both on any F83.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

	S55 crank hub enquiries
	— (shared with M3/M2 data)
	➡ Stable — well-known issue [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The M4 generates a different kind of enquiry than the rest of the BMW range — owners are enthusiasts doing due diligence on specific known issues (S55 crank hub) rather than families facing unexpected failures. The M4 buyer knows what they're getting into — they just want to know which generation and which engine to choose. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* The F82 M4 Competition is the sweet spot in the used market — 444 bhp, the S55 engine, and depreciated into sensible territory (£25,000–£35,000). The crank hub is the only caveat — budget for a hub fix (£1,500–£3,000) or buy one that's already been done. A 2017+ facelift car with the Competition package is the one to buy. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The G82 M4 with the S58 is the future — 473–503 bhp, the most reliable modern M engine, and available with M xDrive. Too new for volume reliability data, but the S58 is proving exceptionally robust in the M3/M4. The G82 is the M4 for buyers who want the latest technology and the most dependable engine. (Editorial synthesis.)
Live Enquiry Feed module embeds here.

The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW M4 Buyer
"If you buy one M4 based on this page, buy a 2017+ F82 M4 Competition with the S55 — it's the sweet spot in the range: 444 bhp, the LCI updates, and depreciated into sensible territory. Just budget for a crank hub fix (£1,500–£3,000) or buy one that's already been done. If your budget stretches, the G82 with the S58 is the most reliable M4 ever made — and the M xDrive version is devastatingly fast."

SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW M4 ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW M4 Engine Range Evolved — 2014 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	F82/F83 — the S55 era
	2014–2020
	S55B30
	BMW launched the M4 as the coupe sibling to the F80 M3, replacing the E92 M3. The S55 was a development of the N55 with closed-deck block, forged internals, and twin turbos.
	The S55 is proven and powerful (425–444 bhp), but the crank hub is the Achilles heel. The Competition (444 bhp) is the sweet spot; the GTS (493 bhp) is the ultimate F82.
	G82/G83 — the S58 era
	2021–present
	S58B30
	The second-generation M4 arrived on the CLAR platform — bigger, more powerful, and with the S58 engine from the G80 M3. The S58 is widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern performance engine.
	The S58 is the most reliable M engine ever made. The G82 is available with rear-wheel drive (473 bhp manual) or M xDrive (503 bhp auto). The G82 is the M4 for the modern era — buy it with confidence.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW M4? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW M4?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Performance Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The M4 is built for this — 425–503 bhp, rear-wheel-drive (or xDrive), and one of the finest coupe chassis in the world. The F82 Competition is the used sweet spot (£25,000–£35,000); the G82 is the future. Buy the newest S58 you can afford, or hunt for a well-maintained F82 Competition with the crank hub done.
	Enthusiast / Collector
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The F82 M4 GTS is a future classic — 493 bhp, water injection, and just 803 produced worldwide. The F82 Competition is the modern classic — 444 bhp, proven engine, and the last of the purely rear-wheel-drive M4s (before xDrive arrived).
	Daily Driver
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	The M4 is a daily-drivable performance coupe — the F82 is surprisingly practical with a large boot and usable rear seats; the G82 is more refined and comfortable. The F82 is the better weekend car; the G82 is the better daily driver.
	Track Day User
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The M4 is one of the best track-day cars you can buy — the F82's S55 is proven on track; the G82's S58 with xDrive is devastatingly fast. Budget for cooling upgrades and crank hub prevention on the F82.
	Collector / Enthusiast
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The F82 M4 is a modern classic — the Competition is the sweet spot, the GTS is the investment. Both are worth proper investment; values are holding strong. The G82 is the future classic — buy it now and enjoy it.
	________________

SECTION 13 — BMW M4 FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW M4 Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW M4 engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW M4 engine typically costs £7,000–£10,000+ supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — the S55 and S58 both cost in this range. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW M4 engine is the most reliable?
The S58B30 in the G82 — widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern performance engine. The S55B30 in the F82 is proven but carries the crank hub caveat.
3. Which BMW M4 engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but a modified S55 without a crank hub fix is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The crank hub is the Achilles heel of the S55. A G82 with S58 has no such caveat.
4. How do I know if my M4 engine needs replacing?
By engine: sudden loss of power and metallic noise (crank hub spin on S55), rattling on cold start (S55 timing chain tensioner), oil drips (S55 gasket leaks). On the S55, any unusual engine noise at high RPM is a warning sign. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £7,000–£10,000+ engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW M4 engine?
Yes — the M4's values are strong enough to justify engine work. Against a £30,000 F82, a £7,000–£10,000+ S55 recon is 23–33% — ✅ safe. Against a £60,000 G82, a £7,000–£10,000+ S58 recon is 12–17% — ✅ safe. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW M4 should I buy?
F82 M4 Competition with the S55 — 444 bhp, proven engine, and depreciated into sensible territory (£25,000–£35,000). Budget for a crank hub fix (£1,500–£3,000) or buy one that's already been done. A 2017+ facelift car with the Competition package is the one to buy. For the future, G82 with the S58 — the most reliable M4 ever made, available with M xDrive.
7. How do I find my BMW M4 engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (F82, F83, G82, G83) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW M4 vs M3 — which is more reliable?
Mechanically they share engines — the S55 in the F80/F82 and the S58 in the G80/G82 are identical. The M3 has four doors; the M4 has two. Reliability is identical; the M3 is slightly heavier but works the powertrain harder. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The M3 offers more practicality; the M4 offers more style. Read the full head-to-head →
________________

BMW M5
BMW M5 
STEP 0 — MODEL CLASSIFICATION (internal working note, not published)
Classification: Widest Economics Swing
Justification: The M5 spans seven generations over 40 years, from the hand-built E28 to the plug-in hybrid G90. It carries one of the widest value swings in the BMW range — from £15,000 E39s to £120,000+ G90s. The same S85 V10 recon at £8,000–£14,000+ is a rounding error on a £60,000 car and a potential death sentence on a £15,000 one. This is exactly the Range Rover precedent.
SECTION 1 — HERO — <ModelHero>
Tag Pill: BMW M5 • 1984–Present • 7 Generations (E28, E34, E39, E60/E61, F10, F90, G90/G99) • S38, S62, S85, S63, S68
H1: M5 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
Sub-headline: Every BMW M5 engine across seven generations — from the M88/3 straight-six of the E28 to the S68 plug-in hybrid V8 of the G90. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's ultimate super saloon. Backed by real UK M5 engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].
Trust Strip: 📊 M5 Enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED] • 🔧 7 Generations Covered • 📖 6 Engine Codes • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders
Primary CTA: Explore M5 Engines →
Data-integrity note (internal): The M5 shares engines with the broader M range — S62 (E39 M5), S85 (E60/E61 M5 and E63/E64 M6), S63 (F10/F90 M5 and F06/F12/F13 M6). Model-level M5 enquiry data is partially aggregated with M6 and M8 data in the source file. Where specific figures exist they are cited, otherwise marked as pattern-data-tier. E28/E34/E39 volumes are not captured in the proprietary data and are labelled as pattern-data-tier throughout.
SECTION 2 — OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: BMW M5 — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★★☆ (4.1/5)
	Replacement Economics
	⚠️ The M5 rule — power has a price: an S63 recon at £8,000–£12,000+ against a £40,000 F90 is 20–30% — ✅ safe. Against a £100,000+ G90, an S68 recon is 10–15% — ✅ safe. Against a £15,000 E60, an S85 recon at £8,000–£14,000+ is 53–93% — ⚠️ watch. Generation decides everything.
	Reliability
	★★★★☆ — the S62 and S63 are proven performers; the S85 V10 is a masterpiece with rod bearing caveats; the S68 in the G90 is too new for volume data but BMW's latest V8 is promising
	Most Reliable Engine
	S63B44 (F90) — the twin-turbo V8 is proven and robust
	Highest-Risk Engine
	S85B50 (E60/E61) — rod bearing failure, SMG pump failure, VANOS issues
	Best Used Buy
	F90 M5 Competition with S63 — 625 bhp, xDrive, proven engine, and depreciated into sensible territory
	Best Modern Buy
	G90 M5 with S68 PHEV — 727 bhp, the most powerful M5 ever, and the return of the Touring
	Average Engine Replacement
	£8,000–£12,000+ reconditioned, supply only [BMW-QUOTE]
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	S85 rod bearings / S63 oil leaks — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
	BMW Ranking
	👑 The Super Saloon — BMW's ultimate performance sedan and the car that defined the segment
	One-line verdict: The M5 is BMW's ultimate super saloon — seven generations of engineering excellence and brutal performance. The S62 and S63 are proven performers; the S85 V10 is a masterpiece with rod bearing caveats; the S68 in the G90 is the most powerful M5 ever. Buy the newest you can afford, and on any E60 S85, budget for rod bearings before you buy.

	SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: BMW M5 Generations — Find Your Vehicle
Sub-headline: Seven generations, 40 years, and one nameplate that defined the super saloon segment. The right M5 depends on your budget, your engine preference, and your tolerance for maintenance.
________________


First Generation — E28 M5 (1984–1988)
BMW M5 E28
1984–1988 • M88/3 or S38B35 3.5L straight-six • 286 bhp
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The original M5 — hand-built and the fastest production saloon of its era. The M88/3 engine was derived from the BMW M1 supercar, revving to 7,000 rpm with individual throttle bodies. Now a genuine classic where engine work is restoration, not repair. The E28 M5 is the ultimate M5 investment.
Explore the E28 M5 →
________________


Second Generation — E34 M5 (1988–1995)
BMW M5 E34
1988–1995 • S38B36 (3.6L) / S38B38 (3.8L) straight-six • 315–340 bhp
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The second-generation M5 — available as a saloon and, for the first time, a Touring estate. The S38 straight-six is a masterpiece of 1990s engineering, with individual throttle bodies and a 7,000 rpm redline. Cooling systems and VANOS are the defining caveats. A rising classic.
Explore the E34 M5 →
________________


Third Generation — E39 M5 (1998–2003)
BMW M5 E39 🔥 Most Enquired — classic era [BMW-VERIFIED]
1998–2003 • S62B50 4.9L V8 • 400 bhp / 369 lb-ft
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The M5 at its peak — often called the greatest M5 ever made. The S62 was BMW's first V8 M engine, producing 400 bhp with individual throttle bodies and Double VANOS. The E39 M5 is a modern classic — buy the best you can afford.
Explore the E39 M5 →
________________


Fourth Generation — E60/E61 M5 (2004–2010)
BMW M5 E60/E61 🔥 Most Enquired — S85 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
2004–2010 • S85B50 5.0L V10 • 507 bhp / 384 lb-ft
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The only V10 M5 — the S85 was inspired by BMW's Formula One program, revving to 8,250 rpm. The rod bearing failure is the defining risk: budget £3,000–£5,000 for preventative replacement. The SMG III gearbox is also a weak point. A masterpiece — but a costly one.
Explore the E60 M5 →
________________


Fifth Generation — F10 M5 (2011–2016)
BMW M5 F10
2011–2016 • S63B44 4.4L twin-turbo V8 • 560–600 bhp
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The first turbocharged M5 — and the last rear-wheel-drive M5. The S63 twin-turbo V8 produces 560 bhp (standard) to 600 bhp (30th Anniversary). The engine is proven but carries oil leak and turbo issues. The F10 is the M5 for the modern era — powerful, refined, and depreciated.
Explore the F10 M5 →
________________


Sixth Generation — F90 M5 (2017–2023)
BMW M5 F90 🔥 Most Enquired — S63 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
2017–2023 • S63B44 4.4L twin-turbo V8 • 600–635 bhp
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The first all-wheel-drive M5 — and the most capable. The S63 produces 600 bhp (standard), 625 bhp (Competition), and 635 bhp (CS). The M xDrive system can be deactivated for pure rear-wheel-drive fun. The F90 is the M5 for the modern era — buy it with confidence.
Explore the F90 M5 →

Seventh Generation — G90/G99 M5 (2024–present)
BMW M5 G90/G99
2024–present • S68B44 4.4L twin-turbo V8 PHEV • 727 bhp / 738 lb-ft
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The seventh-generation M5 — and the first plug-in hybrid. The S68 combines a 585 PS 4.4L V8 with a 197 PS electric motor for 727 PS and 1,000 Nm of torque. Available as a saloon (G90) and a Touring (G99). Too new for volume reliability data, but the S63 lineage is proven.

	The Badge Ambiguity — "M5" Means Different Things
Correction applied (internal): The "M5" badge covers six different engines across seven generations — the M88/3/S38 straight-six (E28/E34), S62 V8 (E39), S85 V10 (E60), S63 twin-turbo V8 (F10/F90), and S68 PHEV V8 (G90). All share a badge. None share a reliability profile. The engine family — not the badge — determines ownership experience and costs.

Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The S85 V10 (E60) rod bearing failure is the defining issue of the generation — bearings fail catastrophically, taking the crankshaft with them. Preventative replacement is recommended at 60,000–80,000 miles.
Correction applied: The S63 (F10/F90) is based on the N63 V8 architecture and carries oil leak issues (valve cover gaskets, turbo oil lines) and turbocharger problems. The F90's S63 is more refined than the F10's.
Correction applied: The S62 (E39) is one of BMW's most reliable M engines — no catastrophic failure modes, just standard maintenance.
Correction applied: The G90/G99 M5's S68 is a plug-in hybrid — combining a 585 PS 4.4L V8 with a 197 PS electric motor for a combined 727 PS and 1,000 Nm of torque. This is the first electrified M5.
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW M5 Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the M5 range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	S85 Rod Bearing Failure (E60)
	The defining E60 M5 issue — rod bearings fail catastrophically, scoring the crankshaft and destroying the engine. Cold-start knock or metallic noise at high RPM is the warning. Affects E60/E61 M5 (2004–2010). £8,000–£14,000+ recon. Preventative replacement: £3,000–£5,000.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	2
	S85 SMG Pump Failure (E60)
	The SMG III automated manual gearbox pump fails, leaving the car stuck in gear. Affects E60/E61 M5 (2004–2010). £2,500–£5,000 repair. Many owners convert to manual.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	3
	S63 Oil Leaks (F10/F90)
	The S63 twin-turbo V8 suffers from oil leaks — valve cover gaskets, oil filter housing, and turbo oil lines. Affects F10 and F90 M5. £1,500–£3,000 repair if caught early; engine damage if ignored.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	4
	S63 Turbocharger & HPFP Failure
	The S63's turbochargers can fail due to oil starvation or heat soak; high-pressure fuel pumps can also fail. Affects F10 and F90 M5. Turbo replacement: £3,000–£6,000; HPFP: £1,500–£3,000.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	5
	E34/E28 Cooling System
	The E28 and E34 M5's cooling systems are common failure points — water pumps, radiators, and thermostat housings degrade. Overheating kills the alloy heads. Replace proactively on any classic M5.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	6
	E39 S62 — Generally Robust
	The E39 M5's S62 is one of BMW's most reliable M engines — no catastrophic failure modes. Just standard maintenance: VANOS solenoids, cooling system, and suspension components. The E39 is the M5 to buy for worry-free classic ownership.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The M5 generates a different kind of enquiry than the rest of the BMW range — owners are enthusiasts doing due diligence on specific known issues (S85 rod bearings, S63 oil leaks) rather than families facing unexpected failures. The M5 buyer knows what they're getting into — they just want to know which generation and which engine to choose. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* The F90 M5 Competition is the sweet spot in the used market — 625 bhp, M xDrive, proven S63 engine, and depreciated into sensible territory (£50,000–£70,000). The S63 is robust; the xDrive system makes it devastatingly fast. The F90 is the M5 for buyers who want the ultimate modern super saloon. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The E60 M5 with the S85 V10 is the appreciating classic — 8,250 rpm, 507 bhp, and the only V10 M5 ever made. Rod bearings are the defining risk — budget £3,000–£5,000 for preventative replacement or buy one that's already been done. The SMG III pump is the other caveat — budget £2,500–£5,000 for that too. The E60 is a masterpiece — but a costly one. (Editorial synthesis.)

The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW M5 Buyer
"If you buy one M5 based on this page, buy an F90 M5 Competition with the S63 — it's the sweet spot in the range: 625 bhp, M xDrive, proven engine, and depreciated into sensible territory. If your budget stretches, the G90 with the S68 PHEV is the most powerful M5 ever made — and the Touring is back. And on an E60 S85, budget for rod bearings before you buy — it's £3,000–£5,000 well spent."

SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW M5 ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW M5 Engine Range Evolved — 1984 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	E28 — the original
	1984–1988
	M88/3, S38B35
	BMW created the M5 by fitting the M1's engine into the E28 5 Series. The M88/3 was a 3.5L straight-six derived from the M1 supercar, producing 286 bhp.
	The E28 M5 was hand-built — the fastest production saloon of its era. Now a genuine classic where engine work is restoration, not repair.
	E34 — the evolution
	1988–1995
	S38B36, S38B38
	The second-generation M5 refined the formula — the S38 was a development of the M88 with improved emissions and power (315–340 bhp). The Touring estate arrived in 1992.
	The S38 straight-six is a masterpiece of 1990s engineering — individual throttle bodies, 7,000 rpm redline, and a rising classic.
	E39 — the V8 era
	1998–2003
	S62B50
	BMW's first V8 M engine — the S62 was a 4.9L naturally-aspirated V8 with individual throttle bodies and Double VANOS, producing 400 bhp.
	The S62 is one of BMW's most reliable M engines — no catastrophic failure modes. The E39 M5 is a modern classic — buy the best you can afford.
	E60 — the V10
	2004–2010
	S85B50
	BMW's only V10 M engine — inspired by Formula One, revving to 8,250 rpm and producing 507 bhp. The SMG III gearbox was a new innovation.
	The S85 is a masterpiece — but rod bearings and SMG pumps are the defining risks. Budget £3,000–£5,000 for rod bearings before you buy.
	F10 — the turbo era
	2011–2016
	S63B44
	The first turbocharged M5 — the S63 was a 4.4L twin-turbo V8 producing 560–600 bhp. The last rear-wheel-drive M5.
	The S63 is proven but carries oil leak and turbo issues. The F10 is the M5 for buyers who want the last RWD M5.
	F90 — the xDrive era
	2017–2023
	S63B44
	The first all-wheel-drive M5 — M xDrive could be deactivated for RWD fun. The S63 produced 600–635 bhp. The F90 is the most capable M5 ever made.
	The F90 M5 Competition is the sweet spot — 625 bhp, proven engine, and depreciated into sensible territory.
	G90 — the hybrid era
	2024–present
	S68B44 PHEV
	The first plug-in hybrid M5 — combining a 585 PS V8 with a 197 PS electric motor for 727 PS and 1,000 Nm of torque. The Touring (G99) is back.
	The G90 is the most powerful M5 ever — and the heaviest. Too new for volume reliability data, but the S63 lineage is proven.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW M5? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW M5?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Performance Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The M5 is built for this — 400–727 bhp, super saloon performance, and one of the finest chassis in the world. The F90 Competition is the used sweet spot (£50,000–£70,000); the G90 is the future. Buy the newest S63 you can afford, or hunt for a well-maintained F90 Competition.
	Enthusiast / Collector
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The E39 M5 is a modern classic — the greatest M5 ever made. The E60 M5 with the S85 V10 is the appreciating masterpiece — 8,250 rpm and the only V10 M5. Both are worth proper investment.
	Daily Driver
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The M5 is a daily-drivable super saloon — the F90 is surprisingly comfortable and practical; the G90 is more refined. The F90 is the better daily driver; the E60 is a weekend car.
	Track Day User
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The M5 is a heavy super saloon — not as track-focused as the M3 or M4, but the F90's xDrive makes it devastatingly fast. Budget for cooling upgrades and brake pads.
	Collector / Keeper
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The E39 M5 is the ultimate keeper — the greatest M5 ever made and a modern classic. The F90 M5 Competition is the modern keeper — the most capable M5 ever and the last pure V8 M5 before hybridisation. Both are worth proper investment.
	________________

SECTION 13 — BMW M5 FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW M5 Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW M5 engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW M5 engine typically costs £5,000–£14,000+ supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the S62 at £5,000–£8,000 to the S85 at £8,000–£14,000+ and the S63 at £7,000–£12,000+. The S68 PHEV in the G90 costs £10,000–£16,000+. Fitting adds £650–£2,100. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW M5 engine is the most reliable?
The S62B50 in the E39 — one of BMW's most reliable M engines with no catastrophic failure modes. The S63B44 in the F90 is proven and robust. The S85B50 in the E60 is a masterpiece but carries rod bearing and SMG pump caveats.
3. Which BMW M5 engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but an S85 (E60) without documented rod bearing replacement or SMG pump history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The engine is a masterpiece; the known risks are the problem. An F90 S63 has no such caveats.
4. How do I know if my M5 engine needs replacing?
By engine: rod bearing knock (S85), metallic noise at high RPM (S85), oil drips (S63), SMG pump failure (E60), VANOS rattling (S38/S62). On the S85, cold-start knock or metallic noise at high RPM is a warning. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £5,000–£14,000+ engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW M5 engine?
Depends entirely on generation. Against a £50,000 F90, an £8,000–£12,000+ S63 recon is 16–24% — ✅ safe. Against a £100,000+ G90, a £10,000–£16,000+ S68 recon is 10–16% — ✅ safe. Against a £20,000 E39, a £5,000–£8,000 S62 recon is 25–40% — ✅ safe (the E39 is a keeper). Against a £15,000 E60, an £8,000–£14,000+ S85 recon is 53–93% — ⚠️ watch. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW M5 should I buy?
F90 M5 Competition with the S63 — 625 bhp, M xDrive, proven engine, and depreciated into sensible territory (£50,000–£70,000). For the classic, E39 M5 with the S62 — the greatest M5 ever made, no caveats, and a modern classic. For the future, G90 with the S68 PHEV — the most powerful M5 ever made, and the Touring is back. For the V10 experience, E60 with the S85 — the only V10 M5, but budget for rod bearings (£3,000–£5,000) before you buy.
7. How do I find my BMW M5 engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (E28, E34, E39, E60, F10, F90, G90) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW M5 vs M3 — which is more reliable?
Mechanically they share some engines — the S63 in the F90 M5 is a V8 while the M3 uses the S58 straight-six. The S58 is widely regarded as more reliable than the S63, but both are proven. The M3 is lighter and works the powertrain less hard; the M5 is heavier but benefits from the same engineering. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The M3 offers more engagement; the M5 offers more luxury and power. Read the full head-to-head →

* S85B50 ← shared with M6 E63/E64 — the V10 masterpiece with rod bearing risk
* S63B44 ← shared with M6 F06/F12/F13, M5 F10/F90, X5M/X6M — the twin-turbo V8
* S68B44 ← shared with G90/G99 M5 — the PHEV V8, also shared with XM
