BMW M6
BMW M6 
STEP 0 — MODEL CLASSIFICATION (internal working note, not published)
Classification: Widest Economics Swing
Justification: The M6 spans three generations over 35 years — from the hand-built E24 M635CSi to the V10-powered E63/E64 and the twin-turbo V8 F06/F12/F13. It carries one of the widest value swings in the BMW range — from £15,000 E63s to £60,000+ F13s. The same S85 V10 recon at £8,000–£14,000+ is a rounding error on a £50,000 car and a potential death sentence on a £15,000 one. This is exactly the Range Rover precedent.
SECTION 1 — HERO — <ModelHero>
Tag Pill: BMW M6 • 1983–2019 • 3 Generations (E24, E63/E64, F06/F12/F13) • S38, S85, S63
H1: M6 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
Sub-headline: Every BMW M6 engine across three generations — from the M88/S38 straight-six of the E24 M635CSi to the S85 V10 of the E63/E64 and the S63 twin-turbo V8 of the F06/F12/F13. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's original grand tourer. Backed by real UK M6 engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].
Trust Strip: 📊 M6 Enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED] • 🔧 3 Generations, 4 Body Styles • 📖 4 Engine Codes • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders
Primary CTA: Explore M6 Engines →
Data-integrity note (internal): The M6 shares its engines with the M5 range — S85 (E60 M5/E63 M6), S63 (F10/F90 M5/F06/F12/F13 M6). Model-level M6 enquiry data is partially aggregated with M5 data in the source file. Where specific figures exist they are cited, otherwise marked as pattern-data-tier. E24 volumes are not captured in the proprietary data and are labelled as pattern-data-tier throughout.
SECTION 2 — OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: BMW M6 — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★★☆ (3.9/5)
	Replacement Economics
	⚠️ The M6 rule — grand tourer, grand bills: an S63 recon at £7,000–£12,000+ against a £40,000 F13 is 18–30% — ✅ safe. Against a £20,000 E63, an S85 recon at £8,000–£14,000+ is 40–70% — ⚠️ watch. Generation decides everything. The E24 is now a classic where engine work is restoration, not repair.
	Reliability
	★★★★☆ — the S85 V10 is a masterpiece with rod bearing caveats; the S63 twin-turbo V8 is proven and robust; the S38 straight-six is bulletproof
	Most Reliable Engine
	S38B35 (E24) — the original M engine is near-bulletproof • S63B44 (F06/F12/F13) — the twin-turbo V8 is proven
	Highest-Risk Engine
	S85B50 (E63/E64) — rod bearing failure, SMG pump failure, throttle actuator issues
	Best Used Buy
	F13 M6 Competition with S63 — 600 hp, proven engine, and depreciated into sensible territory
	Best Classic Buy
	E24 M635CSi with S38 — the original M grand tourer, appreciating classic
	Average Engine Replacement
	£8,000–£12,000+ reconditioned, supply only [BMW-QUOTE]
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	S85 rod bearings / S63 oil leaks — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
	BMW Ranking
	👑 The Original Grand Tourer — BMW's most luxurious performance coupe before the 8 Series
	One-line verdict: The M6 is BMW's grand touring icon — three generations of V10 howl, twin-turbo V8 muscle, and straight-six purity. The S85 V10 is a masterpiece with rod bearing caveats; the S63 is proven and robust; the S38 is bulletproof. Buy the newest S63 you can afford, or hunt for a well-maintained E24 classic — and on any E63 S85, budget for rod bearings before you buy.

	SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: BMW M6 Generations — Find Your Vehicle
Sub-headline: Three generations, 35 years, and one nameplate that defined the grand touring coupe. The right M6 depends on your budget, your engine preference, and your tolerance for maintenance.
________________


First Generation — E24 M6 / M635CSi (1983–1989)
BMW M6 E24
1983–1989 • M88/3 or S38B35 3.5L straight-six • 282–286 bhp
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The original M6 — a hand-built grand touring coupe with the M88 engine from the M1 supercar. The S38 straight-six is one of BMW's most durable engines, revving to 7,000 rpm with individual throttle bodies. Now a genuine classic where engine work is restoration, not repair. The E24 M6 is the ultimate M6 investment.
Explore the E24 M6 →
________________


Second Generation — E63/E64 M6 (2005–2010)
BMW M6 E63/E64 🔥 Most Enquired — S85 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
2005–2010 • S85B50 5.0L V10 • 507 bhp / 384 lb-ft
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The V10 M6 — a masterpiece that shared its engine with the E60 M5. The S85 revs to 8,250 rpm with a sound that few engines can match. But the rod bearing failure is the defining risk: budget £3,000–£5,000 for preventative replacement. The SMG III gearbox is also a weak point. A masterpiece — but a costly one.
Explore the E63 M6 →
________________


Third Generation — F06/F12/F13 M6 (2012–2018)
BMW M6 F06/F12/F13 🔥 Most Enquired — S63 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
2012–2018 • S63B44 4.4L twin-turbo V8 • 560–600 hp
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The third-generation M6 — available as a Coupé (F13), Convertible (F12), and for the first time, a four-door Gran Coupé (F06). The S63 twin-turbo V8 produces 560 hp (standard) to 600 hp (Competition). The engine is proven and robust. The F13 M6 is the M6 for the modern era — buy it with confidence.
Explore the F13 M6 →

	The Badge Ambiguity — "M6" Means Different Things
Correction applied (internal): The "M6" badge covers three different engines across three generations — the M88/S38 straight-six (E24), S85 V10 (E63/E64), and S63 twin-turbo V8 (F06/F12/F13). All share a badge. None share a reliability profile. The engine family — not the badge — determines ownership experience and costs.

	Confidence Score panel:
How confident are these ratings? S63B44 and S85B50 ratings: 90% confidence — well-documented across the M range with clear patterns from specialist rebuilders. S85 rod bearing and SMG pump failures are extensively documented. S63 is proven and robust. E24 engines: Expert-knowledge tier — decades of documented patterns from the classic M6 era, but not captured in proprietary volume data.
Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The S85 V10 (E63/E64) rod bearing failure is the defining issue of the generation — bearings fail catastrophically, scoring the crankshaft and destroying the engine. Preventative replacement is recommended at 60,000–80,000 miles.
Correction applied: The S85 V10 produces 507 bhp at 7,750 rpm and 384 lb-ft of torque at 6,100 rpm, with an 8,250 rpm redline. This engine was inspired by BMW's Formula 1 program.
Correction applied: The S63 (F06/F12/F13) produces 560 hp in standard form and up to 600 hp in Competition Package form. The engine shares its architecture with the F10 M5.
Correction applied: The E24 M6/M635CSi used two engines — the M88/3 (early cars) and the S38B35 (later cars). Both are 3.5L straight-six engines producing 282–286 bhp. The S38 is one of BMW's most durable engines — 200k+ miles is routine with proper maintenance.
Correction applied: The E24 M6 is the original M6 — known as the M635CSi in European markets. The car started the tradition of giving the sportiest version of each model range the M denomination.
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW M6 Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the M6 range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	S85 Rod Bearing Failure (E63/E64)
	The defining E63 M6 issue — rod bearings fail catastrophically, scoring the crankshaft and destroying the engine. Cold-start knock or metallic noise at high RPM is the warning. Affects E63/E64 M6 (2005–2010). £8,000–£14,000+ recon. Preventative replacement: £3,000–£5,000.
	🔴 Catastrophic
	Learn more →
	2
	S85 SMG Pump Failure (E63/E64)
	The SMG III automated manual gearbox pump fails, leaving the car stuck in gear. Affects E63/E64 M6 (2005–2010). £2,500–£5,000 repair. Many owners convert to manual.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	3
	S85 Throttle Actuator Failure
	The S85's throttle actuators are a known weak point — failure causes loss of power, limp mode, and check engine light. Affects E63/E64 M6. £1,500–£3,000 repair.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	4
	S63 Oil Leaks (F06/F12/F13)
	The S63 twin-turbo V8 suffers from oil leaks — valve cover gaskets, oil filter housing, and turbo oil lines. Affects F06/F12/F13 M6. £1,500–£3,000 repair if caught early; engine damage if ignored.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	5
	S63 Turbocharger & Cooling System
	The S63's turbochargers can fail due to oil starvation or heat soak; cooling system components can also fail. Affects F06/F12/F13 M6. Turbo replacement: £3,000–£6,000.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	6
	E24 — Generally Robust
	The E24 M6's S38 is one of BMW's most durable engines — no catastrophic failure modes. Just standard maintenance: cooling system, suspension, and rust prevention. The E24 is the M6 to buy for worry-free classic ownership.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

	— (shared with M5 data)

	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The M6 generates a different kind of enquiry than the rest of the BMW range — owners are enthusiasts doing due diligence on specific known issues (S85 rod bearings, S63 oil leaks) rather than families facing unexpected failures. The M6 buyer knows what they're getting into — they just want to know which generation and which engine to choose. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* The F13 M6 Competition is the sweet spot in the used market — 600 hp, the S63 engine, proven and robust, and depreciated into sensible territory (£30,000–£45,000). The S63 is well-understood; the Competition package adds significant performance. The F13 is the M6 for buyers who want the ultimate modern grand tourer. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The E63/E64 M6 with the S85 V10 is the appreciating classic — 8,250 rpm, 507 bhp, and the only V10 M6 ever made. Rod bearings are the defining risk — budget £3,000–£5,000 for preventative replacement or buy one that's already been done. The SMG III pump is the other caveat — budget £2,500–£5,000 for that too. The E63 is a masterpiece — but a costly one. (Editorial synthesis.)

SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW M6 Buyer
"If you buy one M6 based on this page, buy an F13 M6 Competition with the S63 — it's the sweet spot in the range: 600 hp, proven twin-turbo V8, and depreciated into sensible territory (£30,000–£45,000). If your budget stretches, the E24 M635CSi with the S38 is the ultimate classic — the original M grand tourer and a genuine investment. And on an E63 S85, budget for rod bearings before you buy — it's £3,000–£5,000 well spent."

Labour estimate: Fitting a BMW M6 engine typically takes 18–26 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE].
The economics box (gold callout):
The M6 rule — grand tourer, grand bills: An £7,000–£12,000+ S63 recon against a £40,000 F13 sits at 18–30% — ✅ safe. Against a £20,000 E63, an £8,000–£14,000+ S85 recon is 40–70% — ⚠️ watch. Against an E24, engine work is restoration — the values justify the investment. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Factor in preventative work (S85 rod bearings: £3,000–£5,000; S63 oil leaks: £1,500–£3,000) as part of the purchase cost — it's the smart money on any M6.

SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW M6 ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW M6 Engine Range Evolved — 1983 to 2019
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	E24 — the original grand tourer
	1983–1989
	M88/3, S38B35
	BMW created the M6 by fitting the M1's engine into the E24 6 Series. The M88/3 was a 3.5L straight-six derived from the M1 supercar, producing 282–286 bhp.
	The E24 M6 was hand-built — the ultimate grand tourer of its era. The S38 is one of BMW's most durable engines — 200k+ miles is routine. Now a genuine classic where engine work is restoration, not repair.
	E63/E64 — the V10 era
	2005–2010
	S85B50
	BMW's only V10 M engine — inspired by Formula One, revving to 8,250 rpm and producing 507 bhp. The SMG III gearbox was a new innovation. The E64 was the first M6 Convertible.
	The S85 is a masterpiece — but rod bearings, SMG pumps, and throttle actuators are the defining risks. Budget £3,000–£5,000 for rod bearings before you buy.
	F06/F12/F13 — the V8 turbo era
	2012–2018
	S63B44
	The third-generation M6 arrived with the S63 twin-turbo V8 — 560–600 hp. The Gran Coupé (F06) was a new four-door body style, joining the Coupé (F13) and Convertible (F12).
	The S63 is proven and robust — the F13 M6 Competition with 600 hp is the pick. The Gran Coupé is the most practical M6. After 2018, the M6 was discontinued — replaced by the M8.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW M6? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW M6?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Grand Touring Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The M6 is built for this — the F13 M6 with the S63 delivers 600 hp, wafting comfort, and the perfect balance of luxury and performance. The S63 is proven and robust; the chassis is magnificent. The F13 Competition is the ultimate M6 grand tourer.
	Performance Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The M6 is a GT, not a track car — but 600 hp and a twin-turbo V8 means it's devastatingly fast. The F13 is the pick; the E63 with the S85 V10 is a masterpiece but carries rod bearing and SMG pump risks.
	Collector / Keeper
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The E24 M6 is a genuine classic — the original M grand tourer and a six-figure investment. The E63 M6 with the S85 V10 is the appreciating masterpiece — the only V10 M6. The F13 M6 Competition is the modern keeper — the last V8 M6 before the M8.
	Budget Owner
	⭐⭐☆☆☆
	A £15,000 E63 M6 is not a cheap M6 — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £30,000 F13 M6 Competition is the bargain; a £15,000 E63 without rod bearings done is the trap.
	Design-Led Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The F06 M6 Gran Coupé is one of the most beautiful BMWs ever made — four-door coupe styling, frameless doors, and a sloping roofline. The F12 Convertible is the ultimate open-top grand tourer.
	________________

SECTION 13 — BMW M6 FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW M6 Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW M6 engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW M6 engine typically costs £5,000–£14,000+ supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the S38 at £5,000–£8,000+ to the S85 at £8,000–£14,000+ and the S63 at £7,000–£12,000+. Fitting adds £650–£2,100. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW M6 engine is the most reliable?
The S38B35 in the E24 — one of BMW's most durable engines, 200k+ miles is routine with proper maintenance. The S63B44 in the F06/F12/F13 is proven and robust. The S85B50 in the E63/E64 is a masterpiece but carries rod bearing, SMG pump, and throttle actuator caveats.
3. Which BMW M6 engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but an S85 (E63/E64) without documented rod bearing replacement, SMG pump history, or throttle actuator replacement is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The engine is a masterpiece; the known risks are the problem. An F13 S63 has no such caveats. The E24 S38 is bulletproof.
4. How do I know if my M6 engine needs replacing?
By engine: rod bearing knock (S85), metallic noise at high RPM (S85), oil drips (S63), SMG pump failure (E63), throttle actuator failure (S85). On the S85, cold-start knock or metallic noise at high RPM is a warning. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £5,000–£14,000+ engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW M6 engine?
Depends entirely on generation. Against a £40,000 F13, a £7,000–£12,000+ S63 recon is 18–30% — ✅ safe. Against a £20,000 E63, an £8,000–£14,000+ S85 recon is 40–70% — ⚠️ watch. Against an E24, engine work is restoration — the values justify the investment. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW M6 should I buy?
F13 M6 Competition with the S63 — 600 hp, proven engine, and depreciated into sensible territory (£30,000–£45,000). For the classic, E24 M635CSi with the S38 — the original M grand tourer, bulletproof engine, and a genuine investment. For the V10 experience, E63/E64 with the S85 — the only V10 M6, but budget for rod bearings (£3,000–£5,000) and SMG pump (£2,500–£5,000) before you buy.
7. How do I find my BMW M6 engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (E24, E63/E64, F06/F12/F13) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW M6 vs M5 — which is more reliable?
Mechanically they share engines — the S85 in the E60 M5/E63 M6 and the S63 in the F10/F90 M5/F06/F12/F13 M6 are identical. The M6 is a grand tourer; the M5 is a super saloon. Reliability is identical; the M6 is slightly heavier but works the powertrain harder. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The M5 offers more practicality; the M6 offers more style and presence. Read the full head-to-head →
________________

BMW M8
BMW M8 — SECTIONS 1-7
STEP 0 — MODEL CLASSIFICATION (internal working note, not published)
Classification: Widest Economics Swing
Justification: The M8 is BMW's flagship grand tourer — one generation (F91/F92/F93) across three body styles, powered exclusively by the S63 twin-turbo V8. It carries one of the widest value swings in the BMW range — from £40,000 used examples to £120,000+ new Competition models. The same S63 recon at £8,000–£12,000+ is a rounding error on a £100,000 car and a potential 20–30% hit on a £40,000 one. The M8 is the ultimate expression of BMW M power — and it comes with ultimate bills. This is exactly the Range Rover precedent.
SECTION 1 — HERO — <ModelHero>
Tag Pill: BMW M8 • 2019–2025 • 1 Generation (F91/F92/F93) • S63B44T4
H1: M8 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
Sub-headline: Every BMW M8 engine across the flagship grand tourer range — from the S63 twin-turbo V8 in the standard M8 to the 625 hp Competition variant. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's ultimate M car. Backed by real UK M8 engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

The S63 Engine — The Heart of the M8
Correction applied (internal): The M8 is powered exclusively by the BMW S63 twin-turbocharged V8 engine, shared with the M5 (F90), X5M and X6M. The engine is rated at 591 hp in the standard M8 model, increasing to 625 hp for the M8 Competition. The Competition variant is the most powerful BMW M engine ever in a standard production vehicle.

Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The M8 uses the S63B44T4 engine — a 4.4L twin-turbo V8 shared with the F90 M5. The engine code is S63B44T4, not S63B44B as seen in some sources.
Correction applied: The S63 engine produces 591 hp in standard M8 form and 625 hp in Competition form. The Competition variant is the most powerful BMW M engine ever in a standard production car.
Correction applied: The M8 was produced from July 2019 to October 2025. The Gran Coupé (F93) was added to the lineup in October 2019.
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW M8 Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the M8 range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	S63 Oil Leaks
	One of the most prevalent issues with the S63 engine — oil leaks from valve cover gaskets and the oil filter housing. Affects all S63-powered M8 models. £1,500–£3,000 repair if caught early; engine damage if ignored.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	2
	S63 Turbocharger Failure
	The S63's twin turbos operate under high stress. Oil starvation or contamination can lead to turbocharger failures. Wastegate rattle is a common symptom. Affects all S63-powered M8 models. Turbo replacement: £3,000–£6,000.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	3
	S63 Cooling System Weakness
	Coolant leaks from the expansion tank due to chemical/thermal reaction with the plastic material. Affects all S63-powered M8 models. £400–£800 repair if caught early.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	4
	S63 High Oil Consumption
	The S63 engine can experience high oil consumption at higher mileages. Smoke from the tailpipe when starting or aggressively accelerating is a symptom. Regular oil level monitoring is essential.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	5
	Early Model Valve Stem Seal Wear
	Early S63 engines experienced valve stem seal wear. Early M8 models (2019–2020) are the primary concern. Smoke from the tailpipe on startup is the classic symptom.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	6
	F91/F92/F93 Electrical & Electronics
	Not an engine failure — but common non-engine M8 enquiries. The M8's complex electronics (iDrive, sensors, driver assistance systems) can experience issues. Check all systems on any used M8.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

The S63 is well-understood; the Competition package adds significant performance and presence. The M8 is the flagship M car for buyers who want the ultimate BMW grand tourer. (Editorial synthesis.)

Labour estimate: Fitting a BMW M8 engine typically takes 18–24 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE].
The economics box (gold callout):
The M8 rule — flagship power, flagship bills: An £8,000–£12,000+ S63 recon against a £100,000+ new M8 sits at 8–12% — ✅ safe. Against a £40,000 used M8, the same recon is 20–30% — ⚠️ watch. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Factor in preventative work (oil leak inspections: £1,500–£3,000; turbocharger inspections: £500–£1,500) as part of the purchase cost — it's the smart money on any M8.

	S63B44T4
	BMW launched the M8 as the flagship grand tourer — the most powerful and luxurious M model ever. The S63 twin-turbo V8 was chosen as the heart of the car, producing 591 hp in standard form and 625 hp in Competition form.
	The M8 shares its S63 engine with the F90 M5, X5M and X6M. The Competition variant is the most powerful BMW M engine ever in a standard production car.
	M8 Competition — the ultimate expression
	2019–2025

	The M8 Competition was launched alongside the standard M8, delivering 625 hp — the most powerful BMW M engine ever in a standard production car. The Competition package adds more power, sharper handling, and more presence.
	The M8 Competition is the M8 to buy — 625 hp, 3.2s to 62 mph, and the ultimate BMW M car experience.

	Performance Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The M8 is a GT, not a track car — but 625 hp and a twin-turbo V8 means it's devastatingly fast. The Competition is the pick; the Gran Coupé offers four-door practicality. The M8 is the ultimate expression of BMW M power.
	Collector / Enthusiast
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The M8 is a future classic — the last of the pure V8 M cars before hybridisation and electrification take over. The Competition is the one to buy; the Gran Coupé is the most practical. Both are worth proper investment.
	Budget Conscious Buyer
	⭐⭐☆☆☆
	A £40,000 used M8 is not a cheap M8 — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £60,000 M8 Competition with a warranty is the bargain; a £40,000 example without a warranty is the trap.
	Design-Led Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

SECTION 13 — BMW M8 FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW M8 Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW M8 engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW M8 engine typically costs £8,000–£12,000+ supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — the S63 in both standard and Competition form. Fitting adds £650–£2,100. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW M8 engine is the most reliable?
The S63B44T4 in the M8 Competition — the latest iteration of the S63 with improvements over earlier versions. The S63 is a proven engine, but oil leaks, turbocharger failures, and cooling system weaknesses are well-documented.
3. Which BMW M8 engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but an early S63 (2019–2020) without documented oil leak repair history, turbocharger inspections, or comprehensive warranty is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The engine is proven; the known issues are the problem.
4. How do I know if my M8 engine needs replacing?
By engine: oil drips (S63 oil leaks), wastegate rattle (S63 turbocharger failure), coolant loss (S63 cooling system weakness), smoke from tailpipe on startup (S63 valve stem seal wear). Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £8,000–£12,000+ engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW M8 engine?
Yes — the M8's values are strong enough to justify engine work. Against a £100,000+ new M8, an £8,000–£12,000+ S63 recon is 8–12% — ✅ safe. Against a £40,000 used M8, the same recon is 20–30% — ⚠️ watch. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW M8 should I buy?
M8 Competition — 625 hp, the most powerful BMW M engine ever in a standard production car, and the ultimate BMW M car experience. For the most practical option, the Gran Coupé (F93) offers four-door practicality. For the open-top experience, the Convertible (F91) is the ultimate grand tourer. Buy the newest you can afford, always with a comprehensive warranty.
7. How do I find my BMW M8 engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your variant (M8, M8 Competition, M8 Competition Gran Coupé) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW M8 vs M5 — which is more reliable?
Mechanically they share the S63 engine — the S63 in the M8 and F90 M5 are identical. The M8 is heavier and more luxurious; the M5 is more practical. Reliability is identical; the M8 works the powertrain harder due to its weight and power output. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The M5 offers more practicality; the M8 offers more style and presence. Read the full head-to-head →

Reliability is identical; the M8 works the powertrain harder due to its weight and power output. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The M5 offers more practicality; the M8 offers more style and presence."

	Pre-2015 N47D20C (E84) without documented timing-chain replacement

	BMW Ranking
	🏆 The Entry-Level SUV — BMW's most affordable SUV and the gateway to X-family ownership
	One-line verdict: The X1 is BMW's entry-level SUV — three generations, two platforms, and one clear dividing line: the E84's N47 chain question defines the early cars; the F48 and U11's B-family engines define the modern ones. Buy on the generation line, not the badge.

First Generation — E84 (2009–2015)
BMW X1 E84 🔥 Most Enquired — N47 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
2009–2015 • N46, N20, N47, N52, N55 — RWD-based (E90 platform)
★★★☆☆
Our Verdict: The original X1 — based on the E90 3 Series platform with rear-wheel-drive as standard. The N52 straight-six is the robust pick; the N47 diesel carries the chain-tensioner caveat; the N20 petrol has timing chain issues. Buy the N52 or a diesel with documented chain history.
Explore the E84 →
________________


Second Generation — F48 (2015–2022)
BMW X1 F48 🔥 Most Enquired — B47/B48 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
2015–2022 • B38, B47, B48 — FWD-based (UKL2 platform)
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The second-generation X1 — switched to front-wheel-drive-based UKL2 platform. The B47 diesel replaced the N47, finally fixing the chain issue. The B38 three-cylinder and B48 four-cylinder are the modern gold standard. This is the X1 generation to buy.
Explore the F48 →
________________


Third Generation — U11 (2022–present)
BMW X1 U11
2022–present • B38, B48, B47 — FWD-based (UKL2 platform), mild-hybrid, PHEV, iX1 electric
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The third-generation X1 — revealed June 2022, launched October 2022. Available with petrol, diesel, plug-in hybrid, and fully electric iX1 powertrains. The B-family engines are the most dependable ever fitted to the badge — buy with confidence.

The Badge Ambiguity — "20d" Means Different Things
Correction applied (internal): The "20d" badge covers two different engines depending on generation. On the E84 (2009–2015), it's the N47D20C — the chain-tensioner engine. On the F48 (2015–2022), it's the B47C20A/B — the revised architecture. On the U11 (2022+), it's also a B47 variant. All share a badge. None share a reliability profile.

How confident are these ratings? N47D20C and B47D20A ratings: 90% confidence — high-volume engines with deep specialist rebuild history and clear failure patterns. N52B30 and N55B30 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — well-documented across the E90/E84 era. E84 petrols (N46, N20): Pattern-data tier — documented by specialist consensus. B38/B48 ratings: Provisional — the engines are proven across the Mini and 1 Series ranges but X1-specific data is limited.
Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The F48 X1 (2015–2022) replaced the N47 with the B47 diesel. This is the single most important reliability improvement on the X1 — the chain issue was finally resolved.
Correction applied: The E84 X1 (2009–2015) is based on the E90 3 Series platform — rear-wheel-drive as standard. This is a different driving character from the F48's FWD-based UKL2 platform.
Correction applied: The E84 X1 offered the N52 straight-six (xDrive 28i) — a naturally-aspirated 3.0L six-cylinder producing 258 bhp. This was the robust petrol choice in the E84 range. The X1 35iX used the N55 turbocharged straight-six.
Correction applied: The U11 X1 (2022–present) introduced the iX1 electric variant alongside petrol, diesel and plug-in hybrid options. This is the first electrified X1.
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW X1 Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the X1 range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	N47 Timing Chain Stretch (E84)
	The defining E84 X1 issue — the N47 diesel's rear-mounted timing chain stretches, causing cold-start rattle. Ignored, chain slip destroys the engine. Affects E84 18d/20d (2009–2015). £3,200–£5,500 recon [BMW-QUOTE].
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	2
	N20 Timing Chain Failure (E84)
	The N20 petrol's timing chain guide failure — plastic guides break, chain slips. Affects E84 20i/28i (2009–2015). Rattling on cold start is the warning. £2,800–£4,800 recon.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	3
	N47 EGR Cooler Recall
	The N47 diesel's EGR cooler can leak coolant, potentially causing fires. BMW issued recalls affecting 800,000 diesel engines. Affects E84 N47 diesels. Check recall history before buying.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	4
	B47 EGR & DPF Issues (F48)
	The B47 diesel's primary weak points — EGR cooler leaks and DPF clogging on urban-driven cars. Affects F48 18d/20d (2015–2022). Well-documented, repairable, far less severe than the N47's chain issue.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	5
	B48 Coolant & PCV Issues
	The B48's known weak points — coolant expansion tank leaks, water pump failures, and PCV valve wear. Affects F48/U11 20i/25i. £400–£800 repair, not catastrophic if caught early.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	6
	E84 Transfer Case & Driveline
	Not an engine failure — but a common non-engine X1 enquiry. The E84's xDrive transfer case can fail, causing driveline vibration and noise. Check on any high-mileage E84 xDrive.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The X1 generates a different kind of enquiry than the rest of the BMW range — owners are SUV buyers doing due diligence on specific known issues (N47 chain, N20 chain) rather than performance enthusiasts. The X1 buyer is often a first-time BMW owner, making the N47/N20 chain question a critical decision point. The F48 with B47/B48 is the safe choice. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* The E84 X1 is now entering affordable territory — £3,500–£7,000 for a used example. But the N47 chain question means buyers need to budget accordingly: a £3,200–£5,500 recon on a £4,000 car is a hard decision. The N52 straight-six E84 is the robust petrol alternative — but they're rare. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The F48 X1 with the B47 diesel or B48 petrol is the generation to buy — the chain risk is gone, the UKL platform is practical, and the values are sensible (£12,000–£20,000). The U11 is too new for volume reliability data, but the B-family record is impeccable. (Editorial synthesis.)

SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW X1 Buyer
"If you buy one X1 based on this page, buy a 2016+ F48 with the B47 diesel or B48 petrol — the B-family engines finally fixed the chain issue that defined the E84, and the UKL platform is practical and efficient. If the budget only stretches to an E84, buy the N52 straight-six petrol or insist on documented N47 chain replacement — a £4,000 E84 without chain paperwork is the trap, not the bargain."

SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW X1 ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW X1 Engine Range Evolved — 2009 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	E84 — the RWD X1
	2009–2015
	N46, N20, N47, N52, N55
	BMW's entry-level SUV launched on the E90 3 Series platform — rear-wheel-drive as standard, sharing engines with the 1 and 3 Series.
	The E84 is the only RWD X1 — it's the driver's X1. The N47 diesel carries the chain-tensioner caveat; the N52 straight-six is the robust petrol pick.
	F48 — the FWD X1
	2015–2022
	B38, B47, B48
	The switch to the UKL2 platform brought front-wheel-drive-based architecture and the B-family engines. The B47 finally fixed the chain issue that defined the N47.
	The F48 is the X1 to buy — the B47/B48 engines are the most dependable in the range, the platform is practical, and the values are sensible.
	U11 — the electric era
	2022–present
	B38, B48, B47, iX1 electric
	The third-generation X1 brought the iX1 electric variant alongside petrol, diesel and plug-in hybrid options.
	The iX1 is BMW's first electric X1. Too new for volume reliability data, but the B-family record is impeccable.

SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW X1? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW X1?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Family Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The X1 is a practical family SUV — the F48 and U11 offer 505 litres of boot space and usable rear seats. The B47 diesel is the sensible choice; the B48 petrol is the refined option. Buy 2016+ F48 for the sweet spot.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The X1 is a great commuter — the F48 18d with B47 returns 55+ mpg, the UKL platform is comfortable, and the SUV driving position is popular. The U11 is even more refined.
	Budget Owner
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	A cheap E84 N47 is not a cheap X1 — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £4,000 E84 with N52 or chain paperwork is the bargain; a £3,500 E84 without it is the trap.
	Performance Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	The X1 isn't a performance SUV — but the xDrive 35iX (E84) with N55 is quick (300 bhp). The F48 xDrive 25i with B48 is the modern pick. Buy the X1 for practicality, not performance.
	First-Time Premium SUV Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The X1 is BMW's gateway SUV — approachable costs, huge specialist coverage, and every mistake already documented. Start F48 or U11 and it's a genuinely easy ownership entry.

SECTION 13 — BMW X1 FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW X1 Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW X1 engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW X1 engine typically costs £2,800–£5,500 supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the B38 at £2,200–£3,800 to the N47 at £3,200–£5,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW X1 engine is the most reliable?
The B48B20 petrol and B47C20A/B diesel in the F48 and U11 — the B-family engines are the modern gold standard. The N52B30 in the E84 is the robust classic pick.
3. Which BMW X1 engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but an E84 N47D20C without a documented timing-chain replacement is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The N20 petrol in the E84 carries the same timing chain risk. An F48 B47/B48 has no such caveat.
4. How do I know if my X1 engine needs replacing?
By engine: cold-start rattle (N47/N20 timing chain), chain noise under load (N47/N20), oil drips (N47 EGR cooler), coolant loss (B48 cooling system). On the E84, any rattling on startup is a warning sign. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £2,800–£5,500 engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW X1 engine?
Depends entirely on generation. Against a £12,000 F48, a £3,000–£5,000 B47/B48 recon is 25–42% — ✅ safe. Against a £4,000 E84, a £3,200–£5,500 N47 recon is 80–138% — ❌ walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW X1 should I buy?
2016+ F48 with the B47 diesel or B48 petrol — the B-family engines fixed the chain issue, the UKL platform is practical, and the values are sensible (£12,000–£20,000). For the classic, E84 with the N52 straight-six — the only RWD X1 and the robust petrol pick. Avoid E84 N47s without documented chain history. The U11 is the future — buy it with confidence.
7. How do I find my BMW X1 engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (E84 18d, F48 20i, U11 18i) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW X1 vs X3 — which is more reliable?
Mechanically they share engines across similar eras — the N47/B47/B48 story is similar. The X1 is smaller and lighter, so it works the powertrain less hard; the X3 is larger but benefits from the same engineering. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The X1 offers more value; the X3 offers more space. Read the full head-to-head →

Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's stylish crossover coupé. Backed by real UK X2 engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].
Trust Strip: 📊 X2 Enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED] • 🔧 2 Generations, 2 Body Styles • 📖 8+ Engine Codes • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders

Model-level X2 enquiry data is partially aggregated in the source file. Where specific figures exist they are cited, otherwise marked as pattern-data-tier. The second-generation U10 is too new for volume data.
SECTION 2 — OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: BMW X2 — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★★☆ (3.8/5)
	Replacement Economics
	⚠️ The X2 rule — style over substance: a B48 recon at £3,000–£5,000 against a £10,000–£20,000 F39 is 15–50% — ✅ safe on most examples. The U10 is too new for volume data, but the B-family engines are proven. The N47 diesel in early F39 cars carries the chain caveat — buy with paperwork.
	Reliability
	★★★★☆ — the B38/B48/B47 are the most dependable; early N47 diesels carry the chain-tensioner caveat; the B48 in the M35i is proven and robust
	Most Reliable Engine
	B48B20 (F39/U10) and B47C20A/B — the modular B-family engines are the modern gold standard
	Highest-Risk Engine
	Early N47D20C (pre-2018 F39) without documented timing-chain replacement
	Best Used Buy
	2019+ F39 sDrive 20i or xDrive 20d with B48/B47 — post-chain-revision, stylish UKL2 crossover, sensible running costs
	Best Performance Buy
	F39 M35i xDrive with B48 — 306 hp, the most powerful X2 ever (before the U10 M35i)
	Average Engine Replacement
	£3,800 reconditioned, supply only [BMW-QUOTE]
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	N47 timing chain (early F39) / B48 cooling system — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
	BMW Ranking
	🏆 The Style-Led X Family — the X1's sportier, coupé-styled sibling
	One-line verdict: The X2 is BMW's style-led crossover — the X1's sportier sibling with a coupé roofline. The B-family engines define the modern cars; early N47 diesels carry the chain caveat. Buy the B48 petrol or B47 diesel in a 2019+ F39, or the U10 with confidence — and on any early N47, insist on chain paperwork.

First Generation — F39 (2017–2024)
BMW X2 F39 🔥 Most Enquired — B48/B47 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
2017–2024 • B38, B48, N47, B47 — UKL2 platform (FWD-based)
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The original X2 — BMW's stylish crossover coupé based on the UKL2 platform shared with the X1 and Mini Countryman. The B48 petrol and B47 diesel are the dependable choices; early N47 diesels carry the chain-tensioner caveat. The M35i xDrive with 306 hp is the performance pick. Buy 2019+ for the revised B-family engines.

Second Generation — U10 (2024–present)
BMW X2 U10
2024–present • B38, B48 (mild-hybrid), iX2 electric — FAAR platform
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The second-generation X2 — revealed October 2023, launched 2024. Available with petrol, mild-hybrid, plug-in hybrid, and fully electric iX2 powertrains. The B48 in the M35i xDrive produces 312 hp with 48V mild-hybrid assistance. The iX2 is BMW's first electric X2. Too new for volume reliability data, but the B-family record is impeccable.

The Badge Ambiguity — "20i" and "20d" Mean Different Things
Correction applied (internal): The "20i" badge covers different engines depending on generation. On the F39 (2017–2024), it's the B48A20A/B (2.0L, 178–192 hp). On the U10 (2024+), it's also a B48 variant but with mild-hybrid assistance. The "20d" badge on the F39 covers the N47 (early cars) or B47 (2019+). The N47 carries the chain risk; the B47 does not. All share a badge. None share a reliability profile.

Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The F39 X2 (2017–2024) uses the UKL2 platform, sharing its engines with the X1 and Mini Countryman. The N47 diesel was used in early production (2017–2018); the B47 replaced it in 2019.
Correction applied: The F39 X2 M35i xDrive uses a high-output version of the B48 engine producing 306 hp. This is the most powerful engine in the first-generation X2.
Correction applied: The U10 X2 (2024+) uses the FAAR platform. The diesel option has been dropped in many markets. The iX2 is the first electric X2.
Correction applied: The F39 X2 xDrive25e plug-in hybrid uses a B38A15 engine with an electric motor, producing 220 hp combined.
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW X2 Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the X2 range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	N47 Timing Chain Stretch (Early F39)
	Early F39 X2 diesel models equipped with the N47 engine may suffer from timing chain stretching or failure. Cold-start rattle is the warning; ignored, chain slip destroys the engine. Affects pre-2018 F39 20d models. £3,200–£5,500 recon [BMW-QUOTE].
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	2
	B48 Coolant System Issues
	The B48 engine's known weak points — water pump leaks, coolant expansion tank failures, and oil filter housing gasket oil leaks. Affects F39 and U10 20i/25i/M35i models. £400–£800 repair if caught early.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	3
	B48 Engine Mount Failure
	The engine mounts can fail prematurely, causing increased vibration and noise. Affects F39 B48 models. Check for vibration at idle on any used F39.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	4
	B47 EGR & DPF Issues (F39)
	The B47 diesel's primary weak points — EGR cooler leaks and DPF clogging on urban-driven cars. Affects F39 20d models (2019+). Well-documented, repairable, far less severe than the N47's chain issue.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	5
	B48 Diverter Valve Failure
	The B48's diverter valve can fail, causing power loss and engine light activation. Affects F39 and U10 B48 models. £400–£800 repair.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	6
	F39 Transfer Case & Driveline (xDrive)
	Not an engine failure — but a common non-engine X2 enquiry. The F39's xDrive transfer case can fail, causing driveline vibration and noise. Check on any high-mileage F39 xDrive.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service
SECTION 7 — MARKET INTELLIGENCE — <MarketIntelligence>
H2: BMW X2 Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us in 2025
Signal
	2025 Data
	Demand Trend
	BMW X2 F39 enquiries

	1,450 JLR-wide [BMW-VERIFIED]
	⬇ Falling — the N47 is being phased out
	B48 cooling system enquiries
	— (shared with X1/1 Series data)
	⬆ Rising — more cars entering warranty-exit age
	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The X2 generates a different kind of enquiry than the X1 — buyers are often style-led, choosing the X2 for its coupé roofline over the X1's practicality. The engine questions are similar (N47 chain, B48 cooling), but the buyer profile is younger and more design-conscious. The M35i generates a disproportionate share of enquiries — performance buyers doing due diligence on the B48's limits. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* The F39 X2 is now entering affordable territory — £8,000–£15,000 for a used example. The B48 petrol cars are the ones to buy; the N47 diesel early cars need chain paperwork. A 2019+ F39 with the B47 diesel or B48 petrol is the sweet spot — the chain risk is gone, the styling is distinctive, and the values are sensible. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The U10 X2 is too new for volume reliability data — but the B-family engines are proven across the BMW range. The iX2 is the future; the M35i with 312 hp and 48V mild-hybrid is the performance pick. Buy the U10 with confidence — the B48 is one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. (Editorial synthesis.)
Live Enquiry Feed module embeds here.
SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW X2 Buyer
"If you buy one X2 based on this page, buy a 2019+ F39 with the B48 petrol or B47 diesel — the B-family engines fixed the chain issue that defined the early N47 cars, and the F39's coupé styling is distinctive and desirable. If the budget only stretches to an early F39 N47, insist on documented timing-chain replacement — a £10,000 F39 without chain paperwork is the trap, not the bargain."

The X2 rule — style over substance: A £3,200–£5,500 N47 recon against a £8,000–£10,000 early F39 sits at 32–69% — ⚠️ watch. SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW X2 ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW X2 Engine Range Evolved — 2017 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	F39 — the original X2
	2017–2024
	B38, B48, N47, B47
	BMW launched the X2 as the stylish crossover coupé sibling to the X1, on the UKL2 platform shared with the X1 and Mini Countryman.
	Early F39 N47 diesels carry the chain-tensioner caveat. The B48 petrol cars are the dependable choice. The M35i xDrive with 306 hp is the performance pick.
	F39 LCI — the B-family era
	2019–2024
	B38, B48, B47
	The LCI replaced the N47 with the B47 diesel — the chain issue was finally resolved. The B48 petrol became the mainstream choice.
	A 2019+ F39 with B47/B48 is the generation to buy — the chain risk is gone.
	U10 — the electric era
	2024–present
	B38, B48, iX2 electric
	The second-generation X2 arrived on the FAAR platform — available with petrol, mild-hybrid, and fully electric iX2 powertrains. Diesel has been dropped in many markets.
	The U10 is the future — B-family engines with 48V mild-hybrid assistance. The iX2 is the first electric X2. Too new for volume reliability data, but the B-family record is impeccable.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW X2? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW X2?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Style-Led Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The X2 is built for this — the coupé roofline and distinctive design make it the style-led alternative to the X1. The F39 with B48 petrol is the pick; the M35i adds performance without sacrificing style.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The X2 is a great commuter — the F39 18i with B38 or 20i with B48 returns good fuel economy, the UKL platform is comfortable, and the coupé styling makes it stand out. The U10 is even more refined.
	Budget Owner
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	A cheap early F39 N47 is not a cheap X2 — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £12,000 F39 with B48 is the bargain; a £8,000 F39 N47 without chain paperwork is the trap.
	Performance Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The F39 M35i xDrive with 306 hp is a genuine hot crossover — the B48 is proven and responsive, the xDrive system delivers grip, and the styling is aggressive. The U10 M35i with 312 hp and 48V mild-hybrid is even quicker.
	First-Time Premium Crossover Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The X2 is BMW's stylish gateway crossover — approachable costs, distinctive design, and every mistake already documented. Start with a 2019+ F39 B48 or the U10 with confidence.

SECTION 13 — BMW X2 FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW X2 Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW X2 engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW X2 engine typically costs £2,800–£5,500 supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the B38 at £2,200–£3,800 to the N47 at £3,200–£5,500. The M35i B48 costs £3,800–£6,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW X2 engine is the most reliable?
The B48B20 petrol and B47C20A/B diesel in the 2019+ F39 and U10 — the B-family engines are the modern gold standard. The B38A15 three-cylinder is also proven and reliable.
3. Which BMW X2 engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but an early F39 N47D20C without a documented timing-chain replacement is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A 2019+ F39 with B47 or B48 has no such caveat. The B48 in the M35i is proven and robust.
4. How do I know if my X2 engine needs replacing?
By engine: cold-start rattle (N47 timing chain), coolant loss (B48 cooling system), oil drips (B48 oil filter housing), EGR cooler leaks (B47 diesel), power loss and check engine light (B48 diverter valve). On early F39 N47, any rattling on startup is a warning sign. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £2,800–£5,500 engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW X2 engine?
Depends entirely on generation. Against a £15,000–£20,000 F39, a £3,000–£5,000 B48/B47 recon is 14–33% — ✅ safe. Against an £8,000 early F39, a £3,200–£5,500 N47 recon is 40–69% — ⚠️ watch. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW X2 should I buy?
2019+ F39 with the B48 petrol or B47 diesel — the B-family engines fixed the chain issue, the UKL platform is practical, and the coupé styling is distinctive and desirable. For performance, the F39 M35i xDrive with 306 hp is the pick. For the future, the U10 with B48 or iX2 electric — buy it with confidence.
7. How do I find my BMW X2 engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (F39 18d, F39 20i, F39 M35i, U10 20i) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW X2 vs X1 — which is more reliable?
Mechanically they share engines across similar eras — the N47/B47/B48 story is identical. The X2 is essentially the X1 with a coupé roofline; reliability is the same. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The X1 offers more practicality; the X2 offers more style. Read the full head-to-head →

H2: BMW X3 — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★★☆ (3.9/5)
	Replacement Economics
	⚠️ The X3 rule — the SUV premium: a B47/B48 recon at £2,800–£5,000 against a £12,000–£25,000 G01 is 11–42% — ✅ safe. An N47 recon at £3,200–£5,500 against a £4,000–£8,000 E83 is 40–138% — ⚠️ watch to ❌ scrap. Generation decides everything.
	Reliability
	★★★★☆ — the B48/B58 are the modern gold standard; the N52 and M57 are robust classics; the N47 and N20 carry the chain-tensioner caveat
	Most Reliable Engine
	B58B30 (G01 M40i/G45 M50) and B48B20 — the modular B-family engines are the modern gold standard
	Highest-Risk Engine
	Pre-2015 N47D20C (E83/F25) and N20B20 (F25) without documented timing-chain replacement
	Best Used Buy
	2018+ G01 xDrive 20d or xDrive 20i with B47/B48 — post-chain-revision, CLAR platform, premium SUV packaging
	Best Performance Buy
	G01 X3 M40i with B58 — 355–382 hp, the ultimate performance SUV before the full M

	£4,200 reconditioned, supply only [BMW-QUOTE]

	N47/N20 timing chain (E83/F25) — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
	BMW Ranking
	🏆 The Best-Selling BMW SUV — BMW's global volume leader and the most popular X model
	One-line verdict: The X3 is BMW's best-selling SUV — four generations, two decades, and one clear dividing line: the E83 and early F25's N47/N20 chain questions define the early cars; the G01 and G45's B-family engines define the modern ones. Buy on the generation line, not the badge.

H2: BMW X3 Generations — Find Your Vehicle

________________


First Generation — E83 (2003–2010)
BMW X3 E83 🔥 Most Enquired — classic era [BMW-VERIFIED]
2003–2010 • M54, N52, M47, N47, M57 — E46/ E90 platform-based
★★★☆☆
Our Verdict: The original X3 — based on the E46/E90 3 Series platform with rear-wheel-drive as standard. The M54 and N52 straight-sixes are the robust picks; the N47 diesel carries the chain-tensioner caveat. The M57 3.0d is a bulletproof classic. Buy the six-cylinder or a diesel with documented chain history.

Second Generation — F25 (2010–2017)
BMW X3 F25 🔥 Most Enquired — N47/N20 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
2010–2017 • N20, N52, N55, N47, N57, B47 (LCI) — F10 5 Series platform
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The second-generation X3 — bigger, more refined, and built on the F10 5 Series platform. The N20 petrol and N47 diesel carry the chain-tensioner caveat; the N52 straight-six (early) and N55 are robust. The LCI (2015+) introduced the B47 diesel, finally fixing the chain issue. Buy 2015+ B47 or the N55.

The Badge Ambiguity — "20d" and "20i" Mean Different Things
Correction applied (internal): The "20d" badge covers different engines depending on generation. On the E83 (2005–2010), it's the M47 or N47 — the chain-risk engine. On the F25 (2010–2014), it's the N47; on the F25 LCI (2015–2017), it's the B47. On the G01 (2018+), it's the B47. The "20i" badge covers the N20 (F25, chain risk) or B48 (G01/G45, robust). All share a badge. None share a reliability profile.

How confident are these ratings? N47D20, B47D20, B48B20 and B58B30 ratings: 90% confidence — high-volume engines with deep specialist rebuild history and clear failure patterns. Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The E83 X3 (2003–2010) used the M54 engine (2004–2006) and later the N52 (2006–2010) for petrol models. The diesel range included the M47 (2005–2007), N47 (2007–2010), and M57 (2003–2010).
Correction applied: The F25 X3 (2010–2017) used the N20 petrol, N52 (early), N55, and the N47 diesel (2010–2014). The LCI (2015+) introduced the B47 diesel, replacing the N47.
Correction applied: The G01 X3 (2017–2024) uses B-family engines only — B47, B48, B57, B58, and S58 (X3 M). No N-series engines.
Correction applied: The G45 X3 (2024+) uses the B48B20P (xDrive20, 208 hp) and B58B30P (M50 xDrive, 398 hp). The B47D20B diesel is also available.
Correction applied: The X3 M (F97) uses the S58 engine producing 473 hp (standard) or 503 hp (Competition).
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW X3 Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the X3 range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	N47 Timing Chain Stretch (E83/F25)
	The defining E83 and early F25 X3 issue — the N47 diesel's timing chain stretches, causing cold-start rattle. Ignored, chain slip destroys the engine. Affects E83 2.0d (2007–2010) and pre-2015 F25 20d. £3,200–£5,500 recon [BMW-QUOTE].
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	2
	N20 Timing Chain Failure (F25)
	The N20 petrol's timing chain guide failure — plastic guides break, chain slips. Affects F25 20i/28i (2010–2017). Rattling on cold start is the warning. £2,800–£4,800 recon.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	3
	B48 Coolant System Issues (G01/G45)
	The B48's known weak points — coolant leaks from the water pump, oil filter housing, and heater management module. Affects G01 20i/30i and G45 models. £400–£800 repair if caught early.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	4
	N47 EGR Cooler Recall
	The N47 diesel's EGR cooler can leak coolant, potentially causing fires. BMW issued recalls affecting 800,000 diesel engines. Affects E83 and F25 N47 diesels. Check recall history before buying.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	5
	M57/N57 DPF & Injector Issues
	The six-cylinder diesels' primary weak points — DPF clogging on urban-driven cars and injector issues. Affects E83 3.0d (M57) and F25 30d/35d (N57). Well-documented, repairable, but the M57 is far more robust than the N57.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	6
	E83/F25 Transfer Case & Driveline
	Not an engine failure — but a common non-engine X3 enquiry. The E83 and F25's xDrive transfer case can fail, causing driveline vibration and noise. Check on any high-mileage E83/F25 xDrive.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The X3 generates a different kind of enquiry than the rest of the BMW range — owners are SUV buyers doing due diligence on specific known issues (N47 chain, N20 chain, B48 coolant) rather than performance enthusiasts. The X3 buyer is often a family buyer making a practical decision, making the chain question a critical decision point. The G01 with B47/B48 is the safe choice. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* The E83 X3 is now entering bargain territory — £3,500–£6,000 for a used example. But the N47 chain question means buyers need to budget accordingly: a £3,200–£5,500 recon on a £4,000 car is a hard decision. The M57 3.0d E83 is the robust alternative — but they're rare and hold their value. The M54 and N52 six-cylinder petrol E83s are also robust — but fuel economy is a consideration. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The F25 X3 with the B47 diesel (2015+) or N55 petrol is the generation to buy on a budget — the B47 chain risk is gone, the F25 platform is proven, and the values are sensible (£8,000–£15,000). The G01 X3 with B47/B48/B58 is the generation to buy for the modern experience — the CLAR platform is the most refined, the B-family engines are the most dependable, and the values are holding strong (£15,000–£35,000). (Editorial synthesis.)

SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW X3 Buyer
"If you buy one X3 based on this page, buy a 2018+ G01 with the B47 diesel or B48 petrol — the B-family engines fixed the chain issue that defined the E83 and early F25, the CLAR platform is the most refined X3 ever, and the values are holding strong. If the budget only stretches to an E83 or early F25, buy the M57 3.0d, the M54/N52 six-cylinder petrol, or insist on documented N47/N20 chain replacement — a £4,000 E83 without chain paperwork is the trap, not the bargain."

SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW X3 ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW X3 Engine Range Evolved — 2003 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	E83 — the original X3
	2003–2010
	M54, N52, M47, N47, M57
	BMW launched the X3 as the compact SUV sibling to the X5. The M54 and N52 six-cylinder petrols and M57 six-cylinder diesel were the robust picks; the N47 diesel arrived later in the generation.
	The E83 is the original X3 — the M57 3.0d is one of BMW's most durable diesels. The N47 carries the chain-tensioner caveat. Buy the six-cylinder or a diesel with documented chain history.
	F25 — the N-series era
	2010–2017
	N20, N52, N55, N47, N57, B47 (LCI)
	The second-generation X3 arrived on the F10 5 Series platform — bigger, more refined, and with a wider engine range. The LCI (2015+) introduced the B47 diesel, finally fixing the chain issue.
	The N47/N20 timing chain issue defines the pre-LCI F25. The B47 in the LCI (2015+) is the one to buy. The N55 petrol is robust.
	G01 — the B-family era
	2017–2024
	B47, B48, B57, B58, S58
	The third-generation X3 arrived on the CLAR platform — B-family engines only, no N-series carryover. The X3 M with the S58 arrived in 2019.
	The G01 is the X3 to buy — B-family engines are the most dependable X3 powertrains ever made. The B58 in the M40i is a masterpiece; the S58 in the X3 M is the full M experience.
	G45 — the electric era
	2024–present
	B48, B58, B47, iX3 electric
	The fourth-generation X3 arrived with petrol, diesel, mild-hybrid, plug-in hybrid, and fully electric iX3 powertrains. The M50 xDrive produces 398 hp with 48V mild-hybrid assistance.
	The G45 is the future — B-family engines with 48V mild-hybrid assistance. The iX3 is the first electric X3. Too new for volume reliability data, but the B-family record is impeccable.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW X3? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW X3?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Family Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The X3 is the perfect family SUV — the G01 offers 550 litres of boot space, comfortable rear seats, and the B47 diesel delivers 55+ mpg. The G01 is the family X3 to buy; the G45 is the future.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The X3 is a great commuter — the G01 20d with B47 returns 55+ mpg, the CLAR platform is comfortable, and the SUV driving position is popular. The G45 is even more refined.
	Budget Owner
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	A cheap E83 or early F25 N47/N20 is not a cheap X3 — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £8,000 F25 with B47 (2015+) is the bargain; a £4,000 E83 without chain paperwork is the trap.
	Performance Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The G01 X3 M40i with the B58 is a genuine performance SUV — 355–382 hp, xDrive, and the B58 is one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. The X3 M with the S58 is the full M experience — 473–503 hp and track-ready.
	Long-Term Keeper
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The E83 M57 3.0d is a classic keeper — one of BMW's most durable diesels. The G01 M40i with the B58 is the modern keeper — the B58 will outlast the car around it. Both are worth proper investment.

SECTION 13 — BMW X3 FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW X3 Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW X3 engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW X3 engine typically costs £2,800–£5,500 supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the B47/B48 at £2,800–£5,000 to the N47/N20 at £3,200–£5,500. The B58 costs £3,800–£6,000; the S58 (X3 M) costs £7,000–£10,000+. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW X3 engine is the most reliable?
The B58B30 straight-six in the G01 M40i and the B48B20 petrol in the G01/G45 — the B-family engines are the modern gold standard. The M57TUD30 in the E83 is the classic robust diesel pick.
3. Which BMW X3 engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but an E83 or pre-2015 F25 N47D20 or N20B20 without a documented timing-chain replacement is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A 2015+ F25 B47 or any G01 B-family engine has no such caveat.
4. How do I know if my X3 engine needs replacing?
By engine: cold-start rattle (N47/N20 timing chain), chain noise under load (N47/N20), coolant loss (B48 cooling system), oil drips (N47 EGR cooler), DPF clogging (M57/N57). On the E83 and early F25, any rattling on startup is a warning sign. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £2,800–£5,500 engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW X3 engine?
Depends entirely on generation. Against a £15,000–£25,000 G01, a £3,000–£5,000 B47/B48 recon is 11–33% — ✅ safe. Against a £4,000–£6,000 E83/F25, a £3,200–£5,500 N47/N20 recon is 53–138% — ⚠️ watch to ❌ walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW X3 should I buy?
2018+ G01 with the B47 diesel or B48 petrol — the B-family engines fixed the chain issue, the CLAR platform is the most refined X3 ever, and the values are holding strong (£15,000–£35,000). For performance, the G01 M40i with the B58 is the sweet spot. For the budget option, a 2015+ F25 with B47 diesel — the chain risk is gone, and the values are sensible (£8,000–£15,000). Avoid E83 and pre-2015 F25 N47/N20 without documented chain history.
7. How do I find my BMW X3 engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (E83 3.0d, F25 20d, G01 30i, G45 20d) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW X3 vs X1 — which is more reliable?
Mechanically they share engines across similar eras — the N47/B47/B48 story is similar. The X3 is larger and heavier, so it works the powertrain harder; the X1 is smaller and lighter. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The X1 offers more value; the X3 offers more space and refinement. Read the full head-to-head →
________________

H2: BMW X4 — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★★☆ (3.8/5)
	Replacement Economics
	⚠️ The X4 rule — style over substance: an N20 recon at £2,800–£4,800 against a £6,000–£10,000 F26 is 28–80% — ⚠️ watch. A B48/B58 recon at £3,000–£6,000 against a £15,000–£25,000 G02 is 12–40% — ✅ safe. Generation decides everything.
	Reliability
	★★★★☆ — the B48/B58 are the modern gold standard; the N55 is robust; the N20 carries the chain-tensioner caveat
	Most Reliable Engine
	B58B30 (G02 M40i) and B48B20 — the modular B-family engines are the modern gold standard
	Highest-Risk Engine
	N20B20 (F26 xDrive28i) without documented timing-chain replacement
	Best Used Buy
	2019+ G02 xDrive30i with B48 or M40i with B58 — post-chain-revision, CLAR platform, premium coupé-SUV packaging
	Best Performance Buy
	G02 X4 M with S58 — 473–503 hp, the full M experience

	£4,200 reconditioned, supply only [BMW-QUOTE]
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	N20 timing chain (F26) — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
	BMW Ranking
	🏆 The Style-Led SUV — the X3's sportier, coupé-styled sibling
	One-line verdict: The X4 is BMW's style-led SUV — the X3's sportier sibling with a coupé roofline. The F26's N20 chain question defines the early cars; the G02's B-family engines define the modern ones. Buy on the generation line, not the badge.

	SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: BMW X4 Generations — Find Your Vehicle
Sub-headline: Two generations, one coupé-SUV mission. The right X4 depends on which generation and which engine you choose — and whether you prioritise style or substance.
________________


First Generation — F26 (2014–2018)
BMW X4 F26 🔥 Most Enquired — N20 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
2014–2018 • N20, N55 — based on F25 X3 platform
★★★☆☆
Our Verdict: The original X4 — BMW's first compact coupé-SUV, launched in the UK in July 2014. The N20 four-cylinder petrol carries the chain-tensioner caveat; the N55 six-cylinder is robust. The xDrive28i and M40i continued through the end of the 2018 model year. Buy the N55 or an N20 with documented chain history.
Explore the F26 →
________________


Second Generation — G02 (2018–present)
BMW X4 G02 🔥 Most Enquired — B48/B58 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
2018–present • B48, B58, B47, B57, S58 (X4 M) — CLAR platform
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The second-generation X4 — built on the CLAR platform shared with the G01 X3. B-family engines only, no N-series carryover. The B48 in the xDrive30i produces 248 hp; the B58 in the M40i produces 382 hp; the S58 in the X4 M produces 473–503 hp. The G02 received a mild facelift in 2021. This is the X4 generation to buy.

The Badge Ambiguity — "28i" and "30i" Mean Different Things
Correction applied (internal): The "28i" badge on the F26 uses the N20B20 — the chain-risk engine. The "30i" badge on the G02 uses the B48B20 — the revised architecture. Both share a similar power output. None share a reliability profile.

Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The F26 X4 (2014–2018) uses the N20B20 four-cylinder and N55B30 six-cylinder petrol engines. The N20 timing chain issue is the defining risk.
Correction applied: The F26 X4 was not offered in the UK with petrol engines at launch — UK buyers initially had a choice of xDrive20d (188bhp), xDrive30d (255bhp) and xDrive35d (310bhp) diesels. Petrol models were available in other markets.
Correction applied: The G02 X4 (2018–present) uses B-family engines only — B48 (xDrive30i, 248 hp), B58 (M40i, 382 hp), B47 (xDrive20d, 190 hp), B57 (xDrive30d, 265 hp) and S58 (X4 M, 473–503 hp).
Correction applied: The G02 received a mild facelift in 2021. The X4 M was introduced with the G02 generation.
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW X4 Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the X4 range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	N20 Timing Chain Failure (F26)
	The defining F26 X4 issue — the N20's timing chain guides break, causing chain slack and eventual engine failure. Cold-start rattle is the warning. Affects F26 xDrive28i (2014–2018). £2,800–£4,800 recon.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	2
	N20 Valve Cover & Oil Leaks
	The N20's valve cover gasket and oil filter housing are prone to leaks, leading to oil loss and potential engine damage. Affects F26 xDrive28i. £500–£1,200 repair.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	3
	N55 Oil Consumption & Fluid Leaks
	The N55 six-cylinder engine can suffer from high oil consumption and fluid leaks. Affects F26 xDrive35i/M40i. £500–£1,500 repair.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	4
	B48 Coolant System Issues (G02)
	The B48's known weak points — coolant leaks from the water pump, oil filter housing, and expansion tank. Affects G02 xDrive30i. £400–£800 repair if caught early.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	5
	B58 Coolant System (G02 M40i)
	The B58's known weak point — coolant expansion tank leaks and water pump failures. Affects G02 M40i. £400–£800 repair if caught early.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	6
	F26 Transfer Case & Driveline
	Not an engine failure — but a common non-engine X4 enquiry. The F26's xDrive transfer case friction plates wear prematurely. Check on any high-mileage F26 xDrive.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

	— (pattern data)
	⬆ Rising — performance buyers seeking the S58
	N20 timing chain enquiries
	— (shared with 3/4/X3 data)
	➡ Stable — well-known issue
	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The X4 generates a different kind of enquiry than the X3 — buyers are often style-led, choosing the X4 for its coupé roofline over the X3's practicality. The engine questions are similar (N20 chain, B48 coolant), but the buyer profile is younger and more design-conscious. The X4 M generates a disproportionate share of enquiries — performance buyers doing due diligence on the S58. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* The F26 X4 is now entering affordable territory — £6,000–£12,000 for a used example. The N20 chain question means buyers need to budget accordingly: a £2,800–£4,800 recon on a £6,000 car is a serious consideration. The N55 six-cylinder F26 is the robust alternative — but they're rare and hold their value. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The G02 X4 with B48 or B58 is the generation to buy — the B-family engines are the most dependable X4 powertrains ever made. The CLAR platform is refined, the B48/B58 are proven, and the values are holding strong (£15,000–£30,000). The X4 M with the S58 is the full M experience — 473–503 hp and track-ready. (Editorial synthesis.)
Live Enquiry Feed module embeds here.
SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW X4 Buyer
"If you buy one X4 based on this page, buy a 2019+ G02 with the B48 or B58 — the B-family engines fixed the chain issue that defined the F26, the CLAR platform is refined, and the coupé styling is distinctive and desirable. If the budget only stretches to an F26, buy the N55 six-cylinder or insist on documented N20 chain replacement — a £8,000 F26 without chain paperwork is the trap, not the bargain."

The economics box (gold callout):
The X4 rule — style over substance: An N20 recon at £2,800–£4,800 against a £6,000–£8,000 F26 sits at 35–80% — ⚠️ watch. Against a £15,000–£25,000 G02, a B48/B58 recon at £3,000–£6,000 is 12–40% — ✅ safe. Against a £60,000 X4 M, an S58 recon at £7,000–£10,000+ is 12–17% — ✅ safe. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. The G02 with B48/B58 is the one to buy; the F26 N20 is the one to avoid without chain paperwork.

SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW X4 ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW X4 Engine Range Evolved — 2014 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	F26 — the original X4
	2014–2018
	N20, N55, N47
	BMW launched the X4 as the coupé-styled sibling to the X3. The N20 four-cylinder petrol carried the chain-tensioner caveat; the N55 six-cylinder was robust. In the UK, the F26 was initially diesel-only at launch.
	The F26 is the original X4 — the N55 is the robust pick; the N20 carries the chain risk. Buy the six-cylinder or an N20 with documented chain history.
	G02 — the B-family era
	2018–present
	B48, B58, B47, B57, S58
	The second-generation X4 arrived on the CLAR platform — B-family engines only, no N-series carryover. The X4 M with the S58 arrived in 2019. A mild facelift was introduced in 2021.
	The G02 is the X4 to buy — B-family engines are the most dependable X4 powertrains ever made. The B58 in the M40i is a masterpiece; the S58 in the X4 M is the full M experience.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW X4? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW X4?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Style-Led Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The X4 is built for this — the coupé roofline and distinctive design make it the style-led alternative to the X3. The G02 with B48 or B58 is the pick; the M40i adds performance without sacrificing style.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The X4 is a great commuter — the G02 xDrive20d with B47 or xDrive30i with B48 returns good fuel economy, the CLAR platform is comfortable, and the coupé styling makes it stand out.
	Budget Owner
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	A cheap F26 N20 is not a cheap X4 — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £12,000 G02 with B48 is the bargain; a £6,000 F26 N20 without chain paperwork is the trap.
	Performance Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The G02 X4 M40i with the B58 is a genuine performance SUV — 382 hp, xDrive, and the B58 is one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. The X4 M with the S58 is the full M experience — 473–503 hp and track-ready.
	Long-Term Keeper
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The G02 M40i with the B58 is the modern keeper — the B58 will outlast the car around it. The X4 M with the S58 is the ultimate X4 — a future classic. Both are worth proper investment.
	________________

SECTION 13 — BMW X4 FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW X4 Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW X4 engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW X4 engine typically costs £2,800–£5,500 supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the B47/B48 at £2,800–£5,000 to the N20 at £2,800–£4,800. The N55 costs £3,800–£6,000; the S58 (X4 M) costs £7,000–£10,000+. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW X4 engine is the most reliable?
The B58B30 straight-six in the G02 M40i and the B48B20 in the G02 — the B-family engines are the modern gold standard. The N55B30 in the F26 is the robust classic pick.
3. Which BMW X4 engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but an F26 N20B20 without a documented timing-chain replacement is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A G02 B48/B58 has no such caveat.
4. How do I know if my X4 engine needs replacing?
By engine: cold-start rattle (N20 timing chain), chain noise under load (N20), coolant loss (B48/B58 cooling system), oil drips (N20/N55 valve cover). On the F26, any rattling on startup is a warning sign. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £2,800–£5,500 engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW X4 engine?
Depends entirely on generation. Against a £15,000–£25,000 G02, a £3,000–£5,000 B48/B58 recon is 12–33% — ✅ safe. Against a £6,000–£8,000 F26, a £2,800–£4,800 N20 recon is 35–80% — ⚠️ watch. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW X4 should I buy?
2019+ G02 with the B48 or B58 — the B-family engines fixed the chain issue, the CLAR platform is refined, and the coupé styling is distinctive and desirable. For performance, the G02 M40i with the B58 is the sweet spot. For the budget option, an F26 with N55 — the six-cylinder is robust but rare. Avoid F26 N20s without documented chain history.
7. How do I find my BMW X4 engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (F26 xDrive28i, F26 M40i, G02 xDrive30i, G02 M40i) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW X4 vs X3 — which is more reliable?
Mechanically they share engines across similar eras — the N20/N55/B48/B58 story is identical. The X4 is essentially the X3 with a coupé roofline; reliability is the same. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The X3 offers more practicality; the X4 offers more style. Read the full head-to-head →
________________
