BMW X5
SECTION 1 — HERO — <ModelHero>
Tag Pill: BMW X5 • 1999–Present • 4 Generations (E53, E70, F15, G05) • M54, M62, N52, N55, N62, N63, B58, B57, S63, S68
H1: X5 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
Sub-headline: Every BMW X5 engine across four generations — from the M54 straight-six and M62 V8 of the E53 to the B58 straight-six, B57 diesel and S68 V8 of the G05. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's original luxury SUV. Backed by real UK X5 engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].
Trust Strip: 📊 X5 Enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED] • 🔧 4 Generations Covered • 📖 15+ Engine Codes • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders
Primary CTA: Explore X5 Engines →
Data-integrity note (internal): The X5 shares its engines with the wider BMW range — M54/M62 (E39 5 Series), N62/N63 (5/7 Series), B58/B57 (G-series models). Model-level X5 enquiry data is partially aggregated in the source file. Where specific figures exist they are cited, otherwise marked as pattern-data-tier. E53 volumes are partially captured; E70, F15 and G05 volumes are pattern-data-tier. The G65 (2026+) is too new for volume data.
SECTION 2 — OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: BMW X5 — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★★☆ (3.9/5)
	Replacement Economics
	⚠️ The X5 rule — the V8 tax: an N62/N63 recon at £6,000–£10,000+ against a £4,000–£8,000 E70 is 75–250% — ❌ scrap threshold. A B58 recon at £3,800–£6,000 against a £15,000–£25,000 G05 is 15–40% — ✅ safe. Generation and engine choice decide everything.
	Reliability
	★★★★☆ — the B58 and B57 are the modern gold standard; the M54 and M57 are robust classics; the N62 and early N63 V8s carry significant reliability caveats
	Most Reliable Engine
	B58B30 (G05 xDrive40i) — widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines
	Highest-Risk Engine
	N62B48 (E70 4.8i) — valve stem seal failure • N63B44 (E70/F15 50i) — excessive oil consumption, timing chain issues
	Best Used Buy
	2019+ G05 xDrive40i with B58 — the most dependable X5 powertrain ever made
	Best Diesel Buy
	G05 xDrive30d with B57 — 295+ hp, 40+ mpg, bulletproof
	Average Engine Replacement
	£5,500 reconditioned, supply only [BMW-QUOTE]
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	N62/N63 valve stem seals — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
	BMW Ranking
	👑 The Original BMW SUV — the car that created the luxury SUV segment for BMW
	One-line verdict: The X5 is BMW's original luxury SUV — four generations, 25 years, and one clear dividing line: the N62 and early N63 V8s define the high-risk era; the B58 and B57 define the modern gold standard. Buy the G05 with the B58 unless you have the budget for V8 bills — and on any E70 or F15 V8, budget for valve stem seals before you buy.

	SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: BMW X5 Generations — Find Your Vehicle
Sub-headline: Four generations, 25 years, and the car that created the luxury SUV segment. The right X5 depends on which generation and which engine you choose — and whether you're prepared for V8 bills.
________________


First Generation — E53 (1999–2006)
BMW X5 E53 🔥 Most Enquired — classic era [BMW-VERIFIED]
1999–2006 • M54, M62, N62, M57 — E39 5 Series platform
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The original X5 — BMW's first SUV, based on the E39 5 Series platform. The M54 straight-six is one of BMW's most reliable engines; the M57 diesel is a bulletproof classic; the M62 V8 is robust but ageing. The N62 V8 (2004–2006) carries the valve stem seal caveat. Buy the M54 or M57 — the V8s are for the committed.
Explore the E53 →
________________


Second Generation — E70 (2007–2013)
BMW X5 E70 🔥 Most Enquired — N62/N63 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
2007–2013 • N52, N55, N62, N63, M57, N57 — L4 platform
★★★☆☆
Our Verdict: The second-generation X5 — bigger, more refined, and available with the N62 V8 and early N63 V8. The N62 4.8i suffers from valve stem seal failure (a £4,000+ repair); the N63 50i carries excessive oil consumption and timing chain issues. The M57 diesel and N52/N55 straight-sixes are the robust picks. Buy the six-cylinder or a diesel — the V8s are the trap.
Explore the E70 →
________________


Third Generation — F15 (2014–2018)
BMW X5 F15 🔥 Most Enquired — N55/N63 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
2014–2018 • N55, N63 (updated), N57, B47 (late) — F-platform
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The third-generation X5 — the N55 six-cylinder is robust; the updated N63 V8 is improved but still carries oil consumption caveats. The N57 diesel is proven; late models received the B47 diesel. The F15 is the X5 for buyers who want a modern SUV without the G05 price tag — buy the N55 or N57.
Explore the F15 →
________________


Fourth Generation — G05 (2019–present)
BMW X5 G05 🔥 Most Enquired — B58/B57 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
2019–present • B58, B57, B48, N63 (updated), S63, S68 — CLAR platform
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The fourth-generation X5 — CLAR platform, B-family engines, and the most dependable X5 ever made. The B58 in the xDrive40i is widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. The B57 diesel delivers 295+ hp and 40+ mpg. The S68 V8 in the M60i and X5 M is the most powerful X5 ever — 617–625 hp with 48V mild-hybrid. This is the X5 generation to buy.
Explore the G05 →
________________

	Read the full comparison: BMW X5 E70 vs F15 vs G05 →
The V8 Warning — N62 and N63 Valve Stem Seals
Correction applied (internal): The N62 V8 (E53 4.8is, E70 4.8i) and early N63 V8 (E70 50i, F15 50i) suffer from premature valve stem seal wear, causing blue smoke on startup and excessive oil consumption. This is a £4,000–£6,000 repair on the N62 and up to £8,000+ on the N63. BMW faced widespread criticism over these issues. The S63 (X5 M) and S68 (G05 M60i/X5 M) are more robust but carry M-car servicing costs.
The Badge Ambiguity — "40i" and "50i" Mean Different Things
Correction applied (internal): The "40i" badge covers different engines depending on generation. On the E70, the "40i" doesn't exist — it was badged as 3.0si/4.8i/50i. On the G05, "40i" means the B58B30 — the modern gold standard. The "50i" badge covers the N63 in the E70/F15 (high-risk) or the N63 in the G05 (improved but still carries caveats). All share a badge. None share a reliability profile.

	Confidence Score panel:
How confident are these ratings? B58B30, B57D30, N63B44 and N55B30 ratings: 90% confidence — high-volume engines with deep specialist rebuild history and clear failure patterns. M57TUD30 and N52B30 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — decades of documented patterns from the E53/E70 era. E53 engines (M54, M62): Pattern-data tier — documented by specialist consensus. S68B44 rating: Provisional — the G05 LCI M60i and X5 M are too new for volume data; ratings reflect early patterns and shared-architecture evidence from the S63.
Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The E53 X5 (1999–2006) used the M54 straight-six (2001–2006), M62 V8 (1999–2003), M62 4.6is (2002–2003), and N62 V8 (2004–2006). The M57 diesel was available throughout.
Correction applied: The N62 V8 (E53 4.4i/4.8is, E70 4.8i) suffers from valve stem seal failure — the defining issue of this engine.
Correction applied: The N63 V8 (E70/F15 50i) carries excessive oil consumption, timing chain stretch, and fuel injector failure issues. BMW recognized these problems and updated the engine in later years.
Correction applied: The G05 X5 (2019–present) uses B-family engines only — B48, B58, B57, and S63 (pre-LCI X5 M) / S68 (LCI M60i, X5 M). The B58 is widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines.
Correction applied: The X5 M (F85, G05) uses the S63 engine (pre-2023) or S68 engine (2023+). The S68 adds 48V mild-hybrid assistance and produces 617–625 hp.
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW X5 Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the X5 range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	N62/N63 Valve Stem Seal Failure
	The defining E70 and late E53 X5 V8 issue — valve stem seals wear prematurely, causing blue smoke on startup and excessive oil consumption (1L per 1,000 miles). Affects E53 4.4i/4.8is (2004–2006), E70 4.8i/50i (2007–2013). £4,000–£8,000 repair.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	2
	N63 Timing Chain Stretch & Oil Consumption
	The N63 V8 suffers from timing chain stretch, excessive oil consumption, and fuel injector failure. Rattling on cold start is the warning. Affects E70 and F15 50i (2008–2018). BMW issued extended warranties for some issues. £6,000–£10,000+ recon.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	3
	N62/N63 Cooling System & Injector Failure
	The N62 and N63 V8s suffer from cooling system failures (water pump, thermostat, expansion tank) and fuel injector failure on the N63. Affects E53 4.4i/4.8is, E70 4.8i/50i. Cooling repairs: £500–£1,500; injectors: £1,500–£3,000.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	4
	B58 Coolant System (G05)
	The B58's known weak point — coolant expansion tank leaks and water pump failures. Affects G05 xDrive40i (2019+). £400–£800 repair, not catastrophic if caught early.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	5
	M57/N57 DPF & Injector Issues
	The six-cylinder diesels' primary weak points — DPF clogging on urban-driven cars and injector issues. Affects E53 3.0d (M57), E70 30d/35d, F15 30d/40d (N57). Well-documented, repairable, but the M57 is far more robust than the N57.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	6
	E53/E70 Air Suspension & Driveline
	Not an engine failure — but a common non-engine X5 enquiry. The E53 and E70's air suspension compressors fail; the transfer case can wear prematurely. Check on any high-mileage E53/E70.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The X5 generates a different kind of enquiry than the rest of the BMW range — owners are SUV buyers doing due diligence on specific known issues (N62/N63 valve stem seals, B58 coolant) rather than performance enthusiasts. The X5 buyer is often a family buyer making a practical decision, making the V8 question a critical decision point. The G05 with B58 is the safe choice. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* The E70 X5 is now entering bargain territory — £4,000–£8,000 for a used example. But the N62/N63 V8 questions mean buyers need to budget accordingly: a £4,000–£8,000 valve stem seal repair on a £5,000 car is a hard decision. The M57 3.0d E70 is the robust alternative — but they're rare and hold their value. The N52 and N55 six-cylinder E70s are also robust — but fuel economy is a consideration. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The G05 X5 with the B58 or B57 is the generation to buy — the B-family engines are the most dependable X5 powertrains ever made. The CLAR platform is refined, the B58 is bulletproof, and the values are holding strong (£15,000–£35,000). The X5 M60i with the S68 is the ultimate performance X5 — 625 hp, 48V mild-hybrid, and the most powerful X5 ever. (Editorial synthesis.)

SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW X5 Buyer
"If you buy one X5 based on this page, buy a 2019+ G05 with the B58 straight-six — it's widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines, the CLAR platform is the most refined X5 ever, and the values are holding strong. If the budget only stretches to an E70 or F15, buy the six-cylinder (N52/N55) or the M57/N57 diesel — the V8s (N62/N63) are the trap, not the bargain. And on any N62/N63, budget for valve stem seals before you buy — it's £4,000–£8,000 well spent."

The economics box (gold callout):
The X5 rule — the V8 tax: An N62/N63 recon at £6,000–£10,000+ against a £4,000–£8,000 E70 sits at 75–250% — ❌ walk away. A B58 recon at £3,800–£6,000 against a £15,000–£25,000 G05 is 15–40% — ✅ safe. Against a £50,000+ G05 M60i, an S68 recon at £9,000–£15,000+ is 18–30% — ✅ safe. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. The G05 with B58 or B57 is the one to buy; the E70/F15 N62/N63 V8 is the one to avoid without valve stem seal history.

SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW X5 ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW X5 Engine Range Evolved — 1999 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	E53 — the original X5
	1999–2006
	M54, M62, N62, M57
	BMW launched the X5 as the first luxury SUV — the M54 straight-six and M62 V8 were carried over from the E39 5 Series. The N62 V8 replaced the M62 in 2004.
	The M54 and M57 are bulletproof; the N62 V8 carries the valve stem seal caveat. The 4.6is and 4.8is are the rare performance variants.
	E70 — the N-series era
	2007–2013
	N52, N55, N62, N63, M57, N57
	The second-generation X5 arrived with the N52 straight-six, N62 V8, and early N63 V8. The N57 diesel replaced the M57.
	The N62 and N63 V8s carry valve stem seal and timing chain issues — the six-cylinder and M57 are the robust picks.
	F15 — the refined era
	2014–2018
	N55, N63, N57, B47
	The third-generation X5 refined the package — the N55 six-cylinder is robust; the updated N63 improved but still carries caveats. The B47 diesel arrived late.
	The F15 is the X5 for buyers who want a modern SUV without the G05 price tag — buy the N55 or N57.
	G05 — the B-family era
	2019–present
	B48, B58, B57, S63, S68
	The fourth-generation X5 arrived on the CLAR platform — B-family engines only (except the V8s). The B58 is one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. The S68 V8 arrived in 2023 with 48V mild-hybrid.
	The G05 is the X5 to buy — B-family engines are the most dependable X5 powertrains ever made. The B58 xDrive40i is the sweet spot; the M60i with the S68 is the ultimate performance X5.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW X5? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW X5?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Family Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The X5 is the perfect family SUV — the G05 offers 650 litres of boot space, comfortable rear seats, and the B57 diesel delivers 40+ mpg. The G05 is the family X5 to buy; the E53/E70/F15 are budget alternatives.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The X5 is a great commuter — the G05 xDrive30d with B57 returns 40+ mpg, the CLAR platform is comfortable, and the SUV driving position is popular. The G05 is even more refined.
	Budget Owner
	⭐⭐☆☆☆
	A cheap E70 N62/N63 is not a cheap X5 — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £15,000 G05 with B58 is the bargain; a £5,000 E70 4.8i without valve stem seal history is the trap. The E53/E70 six-cylinder or diesel are the safer budget options.
	Performance Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The G05 M60i with the S68 is a genuine performance SUV — 625 hp, xDrive, and 0-60 in 3.7 seconds. The X5 M is the full M experience — 617 hp and track-ready. Both are the ultimate X5s.
	Long-Term Keeper
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The E53 3.0d with the M57 is a classic keeper — one of BMW's most durable diesels. The G05 xDrive40i with the B58 is the modern keeper — the B58 will outlast the car around it. Both are worth proper investment.
	________________

SECTION 13 — BMW X5 FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW X5 Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW X5 engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW X5 engine typically costs £2,800–£10,000+ supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the B58 at £3,800–£6,000 to the N62 at £4,000–£8,000 and the N63 at £6,000–£10,000+. The S63/S68 costs £9,000–£15,000+. Fitting adds £650–£2,100. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW X5 engine is the most reliable?
The B58B30 straight-six in the G05 xDrive40i — widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. The B57D30 diesel is also excellent. The M57TUD30 in the E53 is the classic robust diesel pick.
3. Which BMW X5 engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but an E70 or late E53 N62B48 or N63B44 without documented valve stem seal replacement or timing chain history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A G05 B58 or B57 has no such caveat.
4. How do I know if my X5 engine needs replacing?
By engine: blue smoke on startup (N62/N63 valve stem seals), rattling on cold start (N63 timing chain), coolant loss (B58 cooling system), DPF clogging (M57/N57/B57). On the E70 and late E53 V8s, blue smoke on startup is a warning sign. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £2,800–£10,000+ engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW X5 engine?
Depends entirely on generation and engine. Against a £15,000–£25,000 G05, a £3,800–£6,000 B58 recon is 15–40% — ✅ safe. Against a £4,000–£8,000 E70, a £4,000–£10,000+ N62/N63 recon is 50–250% — ⚠️ watch to ❌ walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW X5 should I buy?
2019+ G05 with the B58 xDrive40i — the most dependable X5 ever made, 335–382 hp, the B58 is bulletproof, and the values are holding strong (£15,000–£35,000). For diesel, G05 xDrive30d with B57 — 295+ hp, 40+ mpg. For the budget option, E53 with M57 3.0d or M54 3.0i — the robust classics. Avoid E70 and F15 V8s without documented valve stem seal history.
7. How do I find my BMW X5 engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (E53 3.0d, E70 4.8i, F15 50i, G05 40i) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW X5 vs X3 — which is more reliable?
Mechanically they share engines across similar eras — the N52/N55/N63/B58/B57 story is similar. The X5 is larger and heavier, so it works the powertrain harder; the X3 is smaller and lighter. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The X3 offers more value; the X5 offers more space and prestige. Read the full head-to-head →
________________

BMW X6
SECTION 1 — HERO — <ModelHero>
Tag Pill: BMW X6 • 2008–Present • 3 Generations (E71, F16, G06) • N54, N55, N63, S63, B58, B57, S68
H1: X6 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
Sub-headline: Every BMW X6 engine across three generations — from the N54 straight-six and N63 V8 of the E71 to the B58 straight-six, B57 diesel and S68 V8 of the G06. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's original Sports Activity Coupé. Backed by real UK X6 engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].
Trust Strip: 📊 X6 Enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED] • 🔧 3 Generations Covered • 📖 12+ Engine Codes • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders
Primary CTA: Explore X6 Engines →
Data-integrity note (internal): The X6 shares its engines with the X5 and wider BMW range — N54/N55 (3/5 Series), N63 (5/7 Series), B58/B57 (G-series models), S63/S68 (M cars). Model-level X6 enquiry data is partially aggregated in the source file. Where specific figures exist they are cited, otherwise marked as pattern-data-tier. E71 and F16 volumes are partially captured; G06 volumes are pattern-data-tier.
SECTION 2 — OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: BMW X6 — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★★☆ (3.8/5)
	Replacement Economics
	⚠️ The X6 rule — the Sports Activity Coupé tax: an N63 recon at £6,000–£10,000+ against a £5,000–£8,000 E71 is 75–200% — ❌ scrap threshold. A B58 recon at £3,800–£6,000 against a £15,000–£25,000 G06 is 15–40% — ✅ safe. Generation and engine choice decide everything.
	Reliability
	★★★★☆ — the B58 and B57 are the modern gold standard; the N55 is robust; the N63 V8 carries significant reliability caveats; the S63/S68 are proven M-car engines
	Most Reliable Engine
	B58B30 (G06 xDrive40i) — widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines
	Highest-Risk Engine
	N63B44 (E71/F16 50i) — excessive oil consumption, valve stem seals, timing chain issues
	Best Used Buy
	2020+ G06 xDrive40i with B58 — the most dependable X6 powertrain ever made
	Best Diesel Buy
	G06 xDrive40d with B57 — 340 hp, 40+ mpg, bulletproof
	Average Engine Replacement
	£5,500 reconditioned, supply only [BMW-QUOTE]
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	N63 valve stem seals / oil consumption — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
	BMW Ranking
	🏆 The Original Sports Activity Coupé — the car that created the coupé-SUV segment
	One-line verdict: The X6 is BMW's original Sports Activity Coupé — three generations, 15 years, and one clear dividing line: the N63 V8 defines the high-risk era; the B58 and B57 define the modern gold standard. Buy the G06 with the B58 unless you have the budget for V8 bills — and on any E71 or F16 V8, budget for valve stem seals before you buy.

	SECTION 4 — GENERATIONS — <GenerationsGrid>
H2: BMW X6 Generations — Find Your Vehicle
Sub-headline: Three generations, 15 years, and the car that created the coupé-SUV segment. The right X6 depends on which generation and which engine you choose — and whether you're prepared for V8 bills.
________________


First Generation — E71 (2008–2014)
BMW X6 E71 🔥 Most Enquired — classic era [BMW-VERIFIED]
2008–2014 • N54, N55, N63, M57, N57 — E70 X5 platform
★★★☆☆
Our Verdict: The original X6 — BMW's first Sports Activity Coupé, based on the E70 X5 platform. The N54 and N55 straight-sixes are robust; the N63 V8 carries excessive oil consumption and valve stem seal issues. The M57 diesel is a bulletproof classic. Buy the six-cylinder or the M57 — the V8 is for the committed.

________________


Second Generation — F16 (2014–2019)
BMW X6 F16 🔥 Most Enquired — N63/N57 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
2014–2019 • N55, N63, N57, B57 (late) — F15 X5 platform
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: The second-generation X6 — evolutionary styling, refined interior, and available with the N63 V8 (improved but still carrying caveats) and N57 six-cylinder diesel. The N55 six-cylinder is robust; late models received the B57 diesel. Buy the six-cylinder or the diesel — the V8 is the trap.
Explore the F16 →
________________


Third Generation — G06 (2019–present)
BMW X6 G06 🔥 Most Enquired — B58/B57/S68 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
2019–present • B58, B57, S68 (M60i), S63 (pre-LCI X6 M), S68 (LCI X6 M) — CLAR platform
★★★★★
Our Verdict: The third-generation X6 — CLAR platform, B-family engines, and the most dependable X6 ever made. The B58 in the xDrive40i is widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. The B57 diesel delivers 340 hp and 40+ mpg. The S68 V8 in the M60i and X6 M Competition produces 530–625 hp with 48V mild-hybrid. This is the X6 generation to buy.

	Read the full comparison: BMW X6 E71 vs F16 vs G06 →
The V8 Warning — N63 Valve Stem Seals and Oil Consumption
Correction applied (internal): The N63 V8 (E71 50i, F16 50i) suffers from premature valve stem seal wear, causing blue smoke on startup and excessive oil consumption (1L per 1,000 miles). Timing chain stretch and fuel injector failure are also documented issues. BMW faced widespread criticism over these problems. The S63 (X6 M) and S68 (G06 M60i/X6 M Competition) are more robust but carry M-car servicing costs.
The Badge Ambiguity — "40i" and "50i" Mean Different Things
Correction applied (internal): The "40i" badge covers different engines depending on generation. On the G06, "40i" means the B58B30 — the modern gold standard. On the E71, the "40i" didn't exist — it was badged as 35i/50i/M. The "50i" badge covers the N63 in the E71/F16 (high-risk) or the S68 in the G06 M60i (improved). All share a badge. None share a reliability profile.

	Confidence Score panel:
How confident are these ratings? B58B30P, B57D30B, N63B44 and N55B30 ratings: 90% confidence — high-volume engines with deep specialist rebuild history and clear failure patterns. M57TUD30 and N57D30 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — documented patterns from the E71/F16 era. E71 engines (N54, early N63): Pattern-data tier — documented by specialist consensus. S68B44A rating: Provisional — the G06 LCI M60i and X6 M Competition are too new for volume data; ratings reflect early patterns and shared-architecture evidence from the S63.
Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The E71 X6 (2008–2014) used the N54 straight-six (2008–2010) and N55 straight-six (2010–2014) for petrol models. The N63 V8 was used for the xDrive50i. The S63 was used for the X6 M. Diesel models used the M57 and N57 engines.
Correction applied: The N63 V8 (E71/F16 50i) carries excessive oil consumption, valve stem seal wear, timing chain stretch, and fuel injector failure issues. BMW recognized these problems and updated the engine in later years.
Correction applied: The G06 X6 (2019–present) uses B-family engines for the main range — B58 (xDrive40i, 333–380 hp), B57 (xDrive40d, 340 hp) — and S63 (pre-LCI X6 M) or S68 (LCI M60i, X6 M Competition) for the M models.
Correction applied: The X6 M (E71, F16, G06) uses the S63 engine (pre-2023) or S68 engine (2023+). The S68 adds 48V mild-hybrid assistance and produces 523–625 hp.
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW X6 Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the X6 range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	N63 Valve Stem Seal & Oil Consumption
	The defining E71 and F16 X6 V8 issue — valve stem seals wear prematurely, causing blue smoke on startup and excessive oil consumption (1L per 1,000 miles). Affects E71/F16 50i (2008–2019). £6,000–£10,000+ recon.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	2
	N63 Timing Chain Stretch & Injector Failure
	The N63 V8 suffers from timing chain stretch and fuel injector failure. Rattling on cold start and rough running are warning signs. Affects E71/F16 50i. £6,000–£10,000+ recon.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	3
	N63 Cooling System & Turbocharger Issues
	The N63's hot-vee design causes cooling system failures (water pump, expansion tank) and turbocharger issues from heat soak. Affects E71/F16 50i. Cooling repairs: £500–£1,500; turbo repairs: £3,000–£6,000.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	4
	B58 Coolant System (G06)
	The B58's known weak point — coolant expansion tank leaks and water pump failures. Affects G06 xDrive40i (2019+). £400–£800 repair, not catastrophic if caught early.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	5
	M57/N57 DPF & Injector Issues
	The six-cylinder diesels' primary weak points — DPF clogging on urban-driven cars and injector issues. Affects E71 35d (M57), F16 30d/40d/M50d (N57). Well-documented, repairable, but the M57 is far more robust than the N57.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	6
	E71/F16 Transfer Case & Driveline
	Not an engine failure — but a common non-engine X6 enquiry. The E71 and F16's xDrive transfer case can fail, causing driveline vibration and noise. Check on any high-mileage E71/F16 xDrive.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

H2: BMW X6 Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us in 2025
Signal
	2025 Data

	— (aggregated in shared data)
	⬆ Rising — entering affordable-warranty age [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
	BMW X6 E71 enquiries
	— (aggregated in shared data)
	⬇ Falling — the V8 issues drive buyers away [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
	N63 valve stem seal enquiries
	440 JLR-wide [BMW-VERIFIED]
	➡ Stable — well-known issue [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
	B58B30 enquiries
	540 JLR-wide [BMW-VERIFIED]
	⬆ Rising — the B58 is the modern gold standard
	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The X6 generates a different kind of enquiry than the X5 — buyers are often style-led, choosing the X6 for its coupé roofline over the X5's practicality. The engine questions are similar (N63 valve stem seals, B58 coolant), but the buyer profile is younger and more design-conscious. The X6 M generates a disproportionate share of enquiries — performance buyers doing due diligence on the S63/S68. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* The E71 X6 is now entering bargain territory — £5,000–£8,000 for a used example. But the N63 V8 questions mean buyers need to budget accordingly: a £6,000–£10,000+ recon on a £5,000 car is a death sentence. The M57 3.0d E71 is the robust alternative — but they're rare and hold their value. The N55 six-cylinder E71s are also robust — but fuel economy is a consideration. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The G06 X6 with the B58 or B57 is the generation to buy — the B-family engines are the most dependable X6 powertrains ever made. The CLAR platform is refined, the B58 is bulletproof, and the values are holding strong (£15,000–£25,000 for used examples). The X6 M Competition with the S68 is the ultimate X6 — 625 hp, 48V mild-hybrid, and 0-62 mph in 3.7 seconds. (Editorial synthesis.)

SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW X6 Buyer
"If you buy one X6 based on this page, buy a 2020+ G06 with the B58 straight-six — it's widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines, the CLAR platform is the most refined X6 ever, and the coupé styling is distinctive and desirable. If the budget only stretches to an E71 or F16, buy the six-cylinder (N54/N55) or the M57/N57 diesel — the V8s (N63) are the trap, not the bargain. And on any N63, budget for valve stem seals before you buy — it's £4,000–£8,000 well spent."

The economics box (gold callout):
The X6 rule — the Sports Activity Coupé tax: An N63 recon at £6,000–£10,000+ against a £5,000–£8,000 E71 sits at 75–200% — ❌ walk away. A B58 recon at £3,800–£6,000 against a £15,000–£25,000 G06 is 15–40% — ✅ safe. Against a £70,000+ G06 X6 M Competition, an S68 recon at £9,000–£15,000+ is 13–21% — ✅ safe. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. The G06 with B58 or B57 is the one to buy; the E71/F16 N63 V8 is the one to avoid without valve stem seal history.

SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW X6 ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW X6 Engine Range Evolved — 2008 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	E71 — the original X6
	2008–2014
	N54, N55, N63, M57, N57
	BMW launched the X6 as the first Sports Activity Coupé — the N54/N55 straight-sixes and M57 diesel were robust; the N63 V8 carried significant caveats.
	The N63 V8's valve stem seal and oil consumption issues define this era. The M57 35d is the bulletproof classic. Buy the six-cylinder or the M57 — the V8 is for the committed.
	F16 — the refined era
	2014–2019
	N55, N63, N57, B57
	The second-generation X6 refined the package — the N55 six-cylinder is robust; the updated N63 improved but still carries caveats. The B57 diesel arrived late.
	The F16 is the X6 for buyers who want a modern SUV without the G06 price tag — buy the N55 or N57.
	G06 — the B-family era
	2019–present
	B58, B57, S63, S68
	The third-generation X6 arrived on the CLAR platform — B-family engines for the main range, S63/S68 for the M models. The B58 is one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. The S68 V8 arrived in 2023 with 48V mild-hybrid.
	The G06 is the X6 to buy — B-family engines are the most dependable X6 powertrains ever made. The B58 xDrive40i is the sweet spot; the X6 M Competition with the S68 is the ultimate X6.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW X6? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW X6?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Style-Led Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The X6 is built for this — the coupé roofline and distinctive design make it the style-led alternative to the X5. The G06 with B58 is the pick; the M60i adds performance without sacrificing style.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The X6 is a great commuter — the G06 xDrive40d with B57 returns 40+ mpg, the CLAR platform is comfortable, and the coupé styling makes it stand out. The G06 is even more refined.
	Budget Owner
	⭐⭐☆☆☆
	A cheap E71 N63 is not a cheap X6 — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £15,000 G06 with B58 is the bargain; a £5,000 E71 50i without valve stem seal history is the trap. The E71/F16 six-cylinder or diesel are the safer budget options.
	Performance Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The G06 X6 M Competition with the S68 is a genuine performance SUV — 625 hp, xDrive, and 0-62 mph in 3.7 seconds. The M60i is the warm-up act — 523 hp and 80% of the M's performance for less money. Both are the ultimate X6s.
	Long-Term Keeper
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The E71 35d with the M57 is a classic keeper — one of BMW's most durable diesels. The G06 xDrive40i with the B58 is the modern keeper — the B58 will outlast the car around it. Both are worth proper investment.
	________________

SECTION 13 — BMW X6 FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW X6 Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW X6 engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW X6 engine typically costs £3,800–£10,000+ supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the B58 at £3,800–£6,000 to the N63 at £6,000–£10,000+. The S63/S68 costs £9,000–£15,000+. Fitting adds £650–£2,100. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW X6 engine is the most reliable?
The B58B30 straight-six in the G06 xDrive40i — widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. The B57D30 diesel is also excellent. The M57TUD30 in the E71 is the classic robust diesel pick.
3. Which BMW X6 engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but an E71 or F16 N63B44 without documented valve stem seal replacement, timing chain history, or oil consumption history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A G06 B58 or B57 has no such caveat.
4. How do I know if my X6 engine needs replacing?
By engine: blue smoke on startup (N63 valve stem seals), rattling on cold start (N63 timing chain), coolant loss (B58 cooling system), DPF clogging (M57/N57/B57). On the E71/F16 V8s, blue smoke on startup is a warning sign. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £3,800–£10,000+ engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW X6 engine?
Depends entirely on generation and engine. Against a £15,000–£25,000 G06, a £3,800–£6,000 B58 recon is 15–40% — ✅ safe. Against a £5,000–£8,000 E71, a £6,000–£10,000+ N63 recon is 75–200% — ❌ walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW X6 should I buy?
2020+ G06 with the B58 xDrive40i — the most dependable X6 ever made, 333–380 hp, the B58 is bulletproof, and the values are holding strong (£15,000–£25,000). For diesel, G06 xDrive40d with B57 — 340 hp, 40+ mpg. For the budget option, E71 with M57 35d or N55 35i — the robust classics. Avoid E71 and F16 V8s without documented valve stem seal history.
7. How do I find my BMW X6 engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (E71 35d, E71 50i, F16 50i, G06 40i) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW X6 vs X5 — which is more reliable?
Mechanically they share engines across similar eras — the N54/N55/N63/B58/B57 story is identical. The X6 is essentially the X5 with a coupé roofline; reliability is the same. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The X5 offers more practicality; the X6 offers more style. Read the full head-to-head →
________________

Trust Strip: 📊 X7 Enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED] • 🔧 1 Generation, 2 Distinct Eras • 📖 8+ Engine Codes • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders

Data-integrity note (internal): The X7 shares its engines with the broader BMW range — B58 (G05 X5/G30 5 Series), B57 (G05 X5), S68 (G05 X5 M60i/X5 M). Model-level X7 enquiry data is partially aggregated in the source file. Where specific figures exist they are cited, otherwise marked as pattern-data-tier. SECTION 2 — OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: BMW X7 — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★★☆ (4.2/5)
	Replacement Economics
	✅ Usually Worth Replacing — a B58 recon at £3,800–£6,000 against a £25,000–£45,000 used X7 is 8–24% — ✅ safe. An S68 recon at £9,000–£15,000+ against a £70,000–£100,000+ M60i is 9–21% — ✅ safe. The X7's values are strong enough to justify engine work.
	Reliability
	★★★★☆ — the B58 and B57 are the modern gold standard; the S68 is proving robust; the N63 in pre-LCI cars carries the hot-vee caveats
	Most Reliable Engine
	B58B30 (xDrive40i) — widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines
	Highest-Risk Engine
	N63B44D (pre-LCI M50i) — oil consumption, valve stem seals, timing chain issues
	Best Used Buy
	2023+ G07 LCI xDrive40i with B58 — the most dependable X7 powertrain ever made, with 48V mild-hybrid assistance
	Best Performance Buy
	G07 LCI X7 M60i with S68 — 523 hp, 48V mild-hybrid, the most powerful standard X7
	Average Engine Replacement
	£5,500 reconditioned, supply only [BMW-QUOTE]
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	B58 coolant system / N63 oil consumption — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
	BMW Ranking
	👑 The Flagship SUV — BMW's largest, most luxurious, and most expensive SUV
	One-line verdict: The X7 is BMW's flagship SUV — one generation, two distinct eras, and the B58 straight-six is the most dependable engine ever fitted to the badge. The LCI brought the S68 V8 and 48V mild-hybrid assistance, but the B58 remains the gold standard. Buy the B58 unless you have the budget for V8 bills — and on any pre-LCI N63, budget for oil consumption checks before you buy.

BMW X7 G07 🔥 Most Enquired — flagship SUV [BMW-VERIFIED]
2018–present • B58, B57, N63, S68, S63 — CLAR platform
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: BMW's flagship SUV — the largest, most luxurious, and most expensive BMW ever built. The B58 xDrive40i is the dependable choice; the S68 M60i is the performance pick. The LCI (2023+) brought 48V mild-hybrid assistance and the S68 V8. Buy the B58 unless you have the budget for V8 bills.

Pre-LCI vs LCI — The Line That Matters


	2018–2022 (pre-LCI)
	2023–present (LCI)
	Core petrol
	B58 (xDrive40i) / N63 (M50i)
	B58 (xDrive40i) / S68 (M60i)
	Core diesel
	B57 (xDrive30d/40d)
	B57 (xDrive30d/40d) — with 48V MHEV
	Core issue
	N63 oil consumption (M50i)
	None significant
	Our position
	Buy B58 or B57; avoid N63 without history
	The X7 we recommend — buy with confidence
	Read the full comparison: BMW X7 Pre-LCI vs LCI →
The Badge Ambiguity — "M50i" vs "M60i" Means Different Things
Correction applied (internal): The "M50i" badge (pre-LCI) uses the N63B44D V8 — the same hot-vee V8 that carries oil consumption and valve stem seal caveats from earlier generations. The "M60i" badge (LCI) uses the S68B44A V8 — a significantly revised engine with 48V mild-hybrid assistance, producing 523 hp. The S68 is proving more robust than the N63. The "ALPINA XB7" uses a tuned version of the S68 producing up to 631 hp.

How confident are these ratings? B58B30 and B57D30 ratings: 90% confidence — high-volume engines with deep specialist rebuild history and clear failure patterns from the wider BMW range. Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The X7 M50i (pre-LCI) uses the N63B44D engine. This is the same hot-vee V8 that carries oil consumption and valve stem seal caveats from earlier generations (N63B44, N63B44B).
Correction applied: The X7 M60i (LCI) uses the S68B44A engine — a significantly revised V8 with 48V mild-hybrid assistance, producing 523 hp. This is not the same as the N63.
Correction applied: The ALPINA XB7 uses a tuned version of the S68 producing 631 hp — the most powerful X7 variant.
Correction applied: The LCI (2023+) brought 48V mild-hybrid assistance to all petrol models, including the B58 xDrive40i.
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW X7 Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the X7 range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	B58 Coolant System (xDrive40i)
	The B58's known weak point — coolant expansion tank leaks, water pump failures, and thermostat housing cracks. Affects all B58-powered X7 (2018+). £400–£800 repair if caught early.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	2
	B58 Idle Roughness
	The B58 engine can experience idle roughness and vibration, particularly when the air conditioning is off. This is a recognised characteristic of the B58 in the X7.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	3
	N63 Oil Consumption (pre-LCI M50i)
	The N63 V8 suffers from excessive oil consumption (1L per 1,000 miles), valve stem seal wear, and timing chain stretch. Affects pre-LCI M50i (2019–2022). £7,000–£10,000+ recon.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	4
	N63 Turbocharger & Cooling System
	The N63's hot-vee design causes cooling system failures (water pump, expansion tank) and turbocharger issues from heat soak. Affects pre-LCI M50i. Cooling repairs: £500–£1,500; turbo repairs: £3,000–£6,000.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	5
	B57 DPF & EGR Issues (xDrive30d/40d)
	The B57 diesel's primary weak points — DPF clogging on urban-driven cars and EGR cooler issues. Affects all B57-powered X7 (2018+). Well-documented, repairable.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	6
	G07 Air Suspension & Transfer Case
	Not an engine failure — but a common non-engine X7 enquiry. The G07's air suspension can develop leaks; the transfer case can wear. Check on any high-mileage G07.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The X7 generates a different kind of enquiry than the X5 — buyers are often luxury SUV buyers doing due diligence on specific known issues (B58 coolant, N63 oil consumption) rather than families facing unexpected failures. The X7 buyer is often a high-net-worth individual making a considered purchase, making the engine choice a critical decision point. The B58 xDrive40i is the safe choice; the V8s are for the committed. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* The pre-LCI X7 is now entering affordable territory — £25,000–£35,000 for a used example. But the N63 V8 questions mean buyers need to budget accordingly: a £7,000–£10,000+ recon on a £25,000 car is a significant consideration. The B58 xDrive40i is the robust alternative — it's the most dependable X7 powertrain ever made. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The LCI X7 with the B58 or S68 is the generation to buy — the B58 is bulletproof, the S68 is proving robust, and the 48V mild-hybrid assistance adds refinement and efficiency. The ALPINA XB7 is the ultimate X7 — 631 hp and a truly bespoke luxury experience. (Editorial synthesis.)

SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW X7 ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW X7 Engine Range Evolved — 2018 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	G07 launch — the flagship SUV
	2018–2022
	B58, N63, B57, S63
	BMW launched the X7 as the flagship SUV — the largest, most luxurious, and most expensive BMW ever built. The B58 straight-six and B57 diesel were the dependable choices; the N63 V8 carried the hot-vee caveats.
	The X7 is the BMW for buyers who need seven seats and ultimate luxury. The B58 xDrive40i is the sweet spot — 340 bhp, 30 mpg, and bulletproof reliability.
	G07 LCI — the S68 era
	2023–present
	B58 (updated), S68, B57 (updated)
	The LCI brought 48V mild-hybrid assistance across the range and replaced the N63 with the S68 V8. The B58 received an upgrade to 375 bhp with 48V MHEV. The ALPINA XB7 was also updated with the S68.
	The S68 is a significant improvement over the N63 — 523 bhp, 48V mild-hybrid, and 0-62 mph in 4.7 seconds. The X7 LCI is the generation to buy — the B58 is bulletproof, the S68 is proving robust, and the ALPINA XB7 is the ultimate X7.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW X7? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW X7?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Family Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The X7 is the ultimate family SUV — seven seats, 750 litres of boot space (with seats down), and the B57 diesel delivers 35+ mpg. The B58 is the petrol pick; the B57 is the sensible diesel. The LCI with 48V MHEV is even more refined.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The X7 is a large SUV — it's not a city car. But the B58 xDrive40i with 48V MHEV returns 30+ mpg and the CLAR platform is supremely comfortable. The B57 diesel is the economy pick.
	Budget Owner
	⭐⭐☆☆☆
	A cheap pre-LCI N63 M50i is not a cheap X7 — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £30,000 pre-LCI B58 xDrive40i is the bargain; a £25,000 M50i without oil consumption history is the trap.
	Performance Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The X7 M60i with the S68 is a genuine performance SUV — 523 hp, 48V mild-hybrid, and 0-62 mph in 4.7 seconds. The ALPINA XB7 is the ultimate X7 — 631 hp and a bespoke luxury experience. Both are the flagship X7s.
	Long-Term Keeper
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The G07 B58 xDrive40i is the modern keeper — the B58 will outlast the car around it, and the X7's values are holding strong. The LCI B58 with 48V MHEV is the one to buy for the long term.
	________________

SECTION 13 — BMW X7 FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW X7 Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW X7 engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW X7 engine typically costs £3,800–£9,000+ supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the B58 at £3,800–£6,000 to the N63 at £7,000–£10,000+. The S63/S68 costs £9,000–£15,000+. Fitting adds £650–£2,100. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW X7 engine is the most reliable?
The B58B30 straight-six in the xDrive40i — widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. The B57D30 diesel is also excellent. The S68B44A in the LCI M60i is proving robust.
3. Which BMW X7 engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but a pre-LCI N63B44D M50i without documented oil consumption history, valve stem seal replacement, or timing chain history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A B58 or LCI S68 has no such caveat.
4. How do I know if my X7 engine needs replacing?
By engine: coolant loss (B58 cooling system), blue smoke on startup (N63 valve stem seals), rattling on cold start (N63 timing chain), DPF clogging (B57 diesel). On the pre-LCI N63, any excessive oil consumption is a warning sign. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £3,800–£10,000+ engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW X7 engine?
Yes — the X7's values are strong enough to justify engine work. Against a £25,000–£35,000 pre-LCI X7, a £3,800–£6,000 B58 recon is 11–24% — ✅ safe. Against a £45,000–£55,000 LCI X7, the same recon is 7–13% — ✅ safe. Against a £80,000–£100,000+ M60i, a £9,000–£15,000+ S68 recon is 9–19% — ✅ safe. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW X7 should I buy?
2023+ LCI with the B58 xDrive40i — 375 bhp, 48V mild-hybrid, the most dependable X7 ever made, and the values are holding strong (£45,000–£55,000). For performance, the LCI M60i with the S68 — 523 hp and 0-62 mph in 4.7 seconds. For the budget option, a pre-LCI B58 xDrive40i — the B58 is bulletproof and the values are sensible (£25,000–£35,000). Avoid pre-LCI N63 M50i without documented oil consumption history.
7. How do I find my BMW X7 engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (G07 xDrive40i, G07 M50i, G07 M60i) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW X7 vs X5 — which is more reliable?
Mechanically they share engines across similar eras — the B58/B57/N63/S63/S68 story is similar. The X7 is larger and heavier, so it works the powertrain harder; the X5 is smaller and lighter. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The X5 offers more value; the X7 offers more space and prestige. Read the full head-to-head →
________________

H2: BMW XM — One Generation, Two Powertrains

BMW XM G09 🔥 Most Enquired — flagship M car [BMW-VERIFIED]
2022–present • S68B44 V8 PHEV (Label), B58-based 50e PHEV — CLAR platform
★★★★☆
Our Verdict: BMW M's first standalone model — a 2.7-tonne plug-in hybrid SUV that defies physics. The S68 V8 in the Label produces 748 hp — the most powerful BMW M production model ever. The 50e with the B58-based straight-six produces 476 hp and is the sensible choice. Buy the 50e for reliability; buy the Label for the ultimate M experience.

The S68 Engine — The Heart of the XM Label
Correction applied (internal): The XM Label is powered by the S68 engine — a 4.4-litre twin-turbo V8 that is part of BMW's new modular engine family. The engine shares its architecture with the S63 but benefits from significant revisions. In the Label, the S68 produces 748 hp and 1,000 Nm of torque — the most powerful BMW M production model ever. The S68 also serves the X7 M60i, X5 M60i, and the latest M5.
Correction applied (internal): The XM 50e uses a B58-based 3.0-litre straight-six plug-in hybrid producing 476 hp and 700 Nm of torque. This is the same engine family as the B58 in the G29 Z4 and G20 3 Series — one of BMW's most reliable modern engines.

Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The XM Label uses the S68 engine — a 4.4L twin-turbo V8. This is not the same as the N63 (pre-LCI M50i) or the S63 (previous M cars). The S68 is a significant evolution of the M V8 architecture.
Correction applied: The XM 50e (2024+) uses a B58-based 3.0L straight-six plug-in hybrid. This is the first time a six-cylinder has been offered in the XM range, providing a more accessible entry point.
Correction applied: The XM Label produces 748 hp — not 738 hp as some sources state. BMW's official figure is 748 hp (550 kW).
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW XM Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the XM range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	S68 Cooling System Issues (Label)
	The S68's hot-vee design creates significant heat — coolant expansion tank leaks, water pump failures, and thermostat housing cracks are the primary concerns. Affects all S68-powered XM Label models. £500–£1,500 repair if caught early; engine damage if ignored.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	2
	S68 Turbocharger Heat Soak
	The S68's twin turbos sit in the hot-vee of the engine, making them susceptible to heat soak and premature wear. Affects XM Label models. Turbo replacement: £3,000–£6,000.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	3
	S68 Oil Consumption
	Like its N63 predecessor, the S68 can experience higher-than-expected oil consumption at higher mileages. Affects XM Label models. Regular oil level monitoring is essential.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	4
	B58 Coolant System (50e)
	The B58's known weak point — coolant expansion tank leaks, water pump failures, and PCV valve issues. Affects XM 50e models. £400–£800 repair, not catastrophic if caught early.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	5
	PHEV Battery & Charging System
	The XM's 25.7 kWh battery and charging system can experience issues — reduced electric range, charging failures, and battery management system faults. Affects both XM Label and 50e. Battery replacement: £5,000–£10,000+
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	6
	Integrated Brake System Recall
	A recall was issued for certain XM models due to a problem with the integrated brake system that could cause reduced power brake assist. Check recall history before buying any used XM.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service
SECTION 7 — MARKET INTELLIGENCE — <MarketIntelligence>
H2: BMW XM Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us in 2025
Signal
	2025 Data
	Demand Trend
	BMW XM Label enquiries
	— (pattern data)

	S68 engine enquiries

	PHEV battery enquiries
	— (pattern data)
	⬆ Rising — as cars age, battery concerns grow
	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The XM generates a different kind of enquiry than the rest of the BMW range — buyers are high-net-worth individuals doing due diligence on a new and expensive model. The S68 is unproven in the long term, but early reports from the X7 M60i and M5 indicate it is more robust than the N63. The real concern for XM owners is the PHEV battery — a £5,000–£10,000 replacement cost that could emerge as the cars age. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* The XM Label is the halo model — 748 hp, 3.8 seconds to 62 mph, and the most powerful BMW M production model ever. The S68 is the heart of the car, and early owners are watching for cooling system and turbo issues. The good news is that the S68 appears to be a significant improvement over the N63. The bad news is that it's still a hot-vee V8 — and heat is the enemy. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The XM 50e is the sensible choice — the B58-based straight-six PHEV is proven, reliable, and still delivers 476 hp. At £113,000, it's significantly more affordable than the Label (£154,000+), and the running costs are lower. The 50e is the XM for buyers who want the presence without the V8 bills. (Editorial synthesis.)

SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW XM Buyer
"If you buy one XM based on this page, buy the 50e with the B58-based straight-six PHEV — it's the most dependable XM powertrain ever made, delivers 476 hp and 52 miles of electric range, and is £40,000 cheaper than the Label. If the V8 is calling you, budget for cooling system and turbo inspections before you buy — the S68 is a masterpiece, but it's still a hot-vee V8, and heat is the enemy."

The economics box (gold callout):
The XM rule — flagship, safe: An S68 recon at £9,000–£15,000+ against a £100,000–£155,000+ XM Label sits at 6–15% — ✅ safe. A B58-based 50e recon at £3,800–£6,000 against a £113,000+ car is 3–5% — ✅ safe. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe. The XM's values are strong enough to justify engine work — the 50e is the sensible choice; the Label is for the committed. Factor in PHEV battery health (£5,000–£10,000 replacement) as part of the purchase cost — it's the smart money on any XM.

SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW XM? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW XM?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Flagship SUV Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The XM is built for this — the Label delivers 748 hp and 1,000 Nm, the 50e delivers 476 hp and 700 Nm. Both are imposing, luxurious, and statement-making. The 50e is the sensible choice; the Label is for the committed.
	Daily Commuter
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	The XM is a 2.7-tonne SUV — it's not a city car. But the 50e's 52 miles of electric range makes it usable for daily commutes without using fuel. The Label is less efficient but still has a 51-mile electric range.
	Performance Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The XM Label is the most powerful BMW M production model ever — 748 hp, 3.8 seconds to 62 mph, and the full M experience. The 50e is the warm-up act — 476 hp and 5.1 seconds to 62 mph. Both are fast; the Label is devastating.
	Collector / Investor
	⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
	The XM Label is a future classic — the first standalone M model and the most powerful BMW M production car ever. The 50e is the sensible entry point. Both are worth proper investment — but the Label is the one that will appreciate.
	High-Net-Worth Buyer
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The XM is the ultimate M SUV — the Label is for buyers who want the most powerful BMW ever made; the 50e is for buyers who want the XM experience without the V8 bills. Buy the newest you can afford, always with a comprehensive warranty.

SECTION 13 — BMW XM FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW XM Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW XM engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW XM engine typically costs £3,800–£15,000+ supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the B58-based 50e at £3,800–£6,000 to the S68 V8 Label at £9,000–£15,000+. Fitting adds £650–£2,100. PHEV battery replacement costs an additional £5,000–£10,000+. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW XM engine is the most reliable?
The B58B30-based 50e — the straight-six PHEV is the most dependable XM powertrain. The B58 is widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. The S68B44A is proving robust but carries hot-vee V8 caveats.
3. Which BMW XM engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but an S68-powered Label without documented cooling system history, turbo inspections, or comprehensive warranty is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The 50e with B58 has no such caveat.
4. How do I know if my XM engine needs replacing?
By engine: coolant loss (S68 cooling system), reduced power (turbo heat soak on S68), oil consumption (S68), coolant loss (B58 cooling system), reduced electric range (PHEV battery). On the S68, any coolant loss is a warning sign. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £3,800–£15,000+ engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW XM engine?
Yes — the XM's values are strong enough to justify engine work. Against a £100,000–£155,000+ XM Label, a £9,000–£15,000+ S68 recon is 6–15% — ✅ safe. Against a £113,000+ 50e, a £3,800–£6,000 B58 recon is 3–5% — ✅ safe. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW XM should I buy?
XM 50e with the B58-based straight-six PHEV — 476 hp, 52 miles of electric range, the most dependable XM powertrain, and £40,000 cheaper than the Label (£113,000 vs £154,000+). For the ultimate M experience, XM Label with the S68 — 748 hp, 3.8 seconds to 62 mph, and the most powerful BMW M production model ever — but budget for cooling system inspections and the best warranty you can find.
7. How do I find my BMW XM engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your variant (XM 50e, XM Label) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW XM vs X5 M — which is more reliable?
Mechanically they share the S68 engine (in the XM Label and X5 M60i). The XM Label is heavier and more complex with its PHEV system, so it works the powertrain harder. The X5 M is more established and proven. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The X5 M offers more value; the XM offers more presence and exclusivity. Read the full head-to-head →
________________

BMW Z SERIES
SECTION 1 — HERO — <ModelHero>
Tag Pill: BMW Z Series • 1995–Present • 5 Generations (Z3, Z8, E85/E86, E89, G29) • M44, M52, M54, S54, S62, N52, N54, N55, B48, B58
H1: Z Series Engines — The Complete UK Guide
Sub-headline: Every BMW Z Series engine across five distinct roadster generations — from the M44 four-cylinder of the Z3 to the S62 V8 of the Z8, the N52 straight-six of the E85 Z4, and the B58 straight-six of the G29. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's iconic roadster family. Backed by real UK Z Series engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].
Trust Strip: 📊 Z Series Enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED] • 🔧 5 Generations Covered • 📖 12+ Engine Codes • 🏆 Part of Engine Finders
Primary CTA: Explore Z Series Engines →
Data-integrity note (internal): The Z Series shares its engines with the wider BMW range — M54 (E46 3 Series), N52 (E90 3 Series), N54 (E90 3 Series), B58 (G-series models). Model-level Z Series enquiry data is partially aggregated in the source file. The Z3 and Z8 are classic-era vehicles with limited UK enquiry data; they are labelled as pattern-data-tier throughout. The G29 Z4 is too new for volume data; ratings reflect the B58's proven reliability from other models.
SECTION 2 — OWNERSHIP VERDICT — <OwnershipVerdict>
H2: BMW Z Series — The Ownership Verdict
Verdict Metric
	Our Call
	Overall Ownership Rating
	★★★★☆ (4.0/5)
	Replacement Economics
	⚠️ The Z Series rule — the roadster premium: a B58 recon at £3,800–£6,000 against a £15,000–£25,000 G29 is 15–40% — ✅ safe. An N54 recon at £3,500–£6,500 against a £6,000–£10,000 E89 is 35–108% — ⚠️ watch to ❌ scrap. The Z8 is a collector's item where engine work is restoration.
	Reliability
	★★★★☆ — the M54, N52 and B58 are the most dependable; the N54 carries HPFP and injector caveats; the S62 is robust but ageing; the S54 is a masterpiece with VANOS caveats
	Most Reliable Engine
	B58B30 (G29 M40i) — widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines • M54B30 (Z3/Z4) — the classic straight-six gold standard
	Highest-Risk Engine
	N54B30 (E89 sDrive35i) — HPFP failure, injector issues, turbo wastegate rattle
	Best Used Buy
	E85 Z4 3.0si with N52 — the naturally-aspirated straight-six is robust, the chassis is magnificent, and values are still sensible
	Best Modern Buy
	G29 Z4 M40i with B58 — 340 hp, the most dependable modern roadster engine, and the last manual Z4
	Average Engine Replacement
	£4,500 reconditioned, supply only [BMW-QUOTE]
	Most Common Failure Enquiry
	N54 HPFP / cooling system — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
	BMW Ranking
	🏆 The Roadster Icon — BMW's most celebrated two-seater sports car lineage
	One-line verdict: The Z Series is BMW's roadster icon — five generations of open-top driving pleasure. The M54 and N52 are the robust classics; the N54 is the high-risk turbo; the B58 is the modern gold standard. Buy the straight-six — the four-cylinder Z3s are for collectors, the Z8 is for investors, and the G29 is for drivers who want the best of both worlds.

The M52 and M54 straight-sixes are the robust picks; the S54 in the Z3 M is a masterpiece with VANOS caveats; the M44 four-cylinder is the entry point. Buy the six-cylinder — the four-cylinder is for collectors.

The Badge Ambiguity — "Z4" Means Different Things
Correction applied (internal): The "Z4" badge covers three different generations with different engines:
* E85/E86 (2002–2008): M54, N52, S54 — the classic roadster/coupé
* E89 (2009–2016): N52, N54, N55 — the retractable hardtop
* G29 (2019–present): B48, B58 — the modern roadster
The "Z3" badge covers the original roadster (1995–2002) with M44, M52, M54, and S54 engines. The Z8 is a separate model entirely with the S62 V8. All share a badge family. None share a reliability profile across generations.

How confident are these ratings? B58B30, N52B30 and M54B30 ratings: 90% confidence — high-volume engines with deep specialist rebuild history and clear failure patterns from the wider BMW range. N54B30 rating: 85% confidence — HPFP and injector issues are extensively documented. S54B32 and S62B50 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — documented by M car and specialist consensus. Z3 four-cylinder engines: Pattern-data tier — documented by enthusiast records.
Data note (internal) — corrections applied:
Correction applied: The Z3 (1995–2002) used M44 (1.9L four-cylinder), M52 (2.0L, 2.8L six-cylinder), M54 (2.2L, 3.0L six-cylinder), and S54 (3.2L six-cylinder in the Z3 M). The M52 and M54 are the robust picks.
Correction applied: The Z8 (1999–2003) uses the S62 V8 — the same engine as the E39 M5. It produces 394 bhp and is one of BMW's most revered engines.
Correction applied: The Z4 E85 (2002–2008) used M54 (2002–2005) and N52 (2006–2008) six-cylinder engines, plus the S54 in the Z4 M. The N52 replaced the M54 in 2006.
Correction applied: The Z4 E89 (2009–2016) used N52 (sDrive23i/30i), N54 (sDrive35i, 2009–2011), and N55 (sDrive35i, 2011–2016). The N54 carries HPFP and injector caveats; the N55 is the improved version.
Correction applied: The Z4 G29 (2019–present) uses B48 (sDrive20i/30i) and B58 (M40i) engines. The B58 produces 340 hp and is widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines.
SECTION 6 — COMMON PROBLEMS — <CommonProblems>
H2: Common BMW Z Series Engine Problems
Sub-headline: The six most common failure patterns across the Z Series range — symptoms, costs and urgency.
#
	Problem
	Description
	Severity
	Link
	1
	N54 HPFP & Injector Failure (E89)
	The defining E89 Z4 issue — high-pressure fuel pump failure causes long cranking and limp mode; injector issues follow. Affects E89 sDrive35i (2009–2011). BMW extended warranty covered many; check history before buying. £3,500–£6,500 recon.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	2
	M54 Cooling System Failure (Z3/E85)
	The M54's plastic cooling system components degrade — water pump impellers, expansion tanks, and thermostat housings fail. Overheating kills the alloy head. Affects Z3 3.0i and E85 3.0i. Replace proactively every 60k miles.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	3
	N52 Valvetronic & Cooling System
	The N52's Valvetronic eccentric shaft sensor can fail; the cooling system (water pump, thermostat) is also a weak point. Affects E85 3.0si and E89 sDrive23i/30i. £500–£1,500 repair.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	4
	S54 VANOS & Rod Bearing Failure
	The S54's VANOS pump can fail, causing loss of power; rod bearing wear is also documented. Affects Z3 M and Z4 M (2001–2008). £5,000–£8,000 recon. Preventative VANOS: £1,500–£3,000; rod bearings: £2,500–£4,000.
	🟠 Immediate
	Learn more →
	5
	Z3 Rear Subframe Mount Cracking
	Not an engine failure — but the most common non-engine Z3 enquiry. The rear subframe mounting points can crack on high-mileage Z3s. Check on any Z3 over 80k miles.
	🟡 Monitor
	Learn more →
	6
	B58 Coolant System (G29)
	The B58's known weak point — coolant expansion tank leaks and water pump failures. Affects G29 M40i (2019+). £400–£800 repair, not catastrophic if caught early.
	🟢 Low
	Learn more →
	Urgency key: 🔴 Catastrophic — stop driving, specialist inspection immediately • 🟠 Immediate — book inspection now • 🟡 Monitor — book inspection soon • 🟢 Low — plan at next service

	Insights from the data:
* Interesting insight: The Z Series generates a different kind of enquiry than the rest of the BMW range — owners are enthusiasts doing due diligence on specific known issues (N54 HPFP, M54 cooling, S54 VANOS) rather than families facing unexpected failures. The Z Series buyer is often a weekend driver making an emotional purchase, making the engine choice a critical decision point. The N52-powered E85 is the sweet spot — robust engine, classic styling, and sensible values. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
* The Z3 is now a classic — £4,000–£12,000 for a used example. The M54 3.0i is the one to buy; the S54 Z3 M is the collector's piece. The Z8 is a six-figure classic — engine work on a Z8 is restoration, not repair. (Editorial synthesis.)
* The Z4 E85 with the N52 is the sweet spot in the used market — £5,000–£10,000 for a well-maintained example. The N52 is robust, the styling has aged beautifully, and the driving experience is pure BMW. The E89 with the N54 is the one to avoid without documented HPFP history. The G29 with the B58 is the future — the B58 is bulletproof, and the G29 is the last manual Z4. (Editorial synthesis.)

SECTION 7B — EDITORIAL PULL-QUOTE — <EditorialPullQuote>
The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW Z Series Buyer
"If you buy one Z Series based on this page, buy an E85 Z4 3.0si with the N52 — it's the sweet spot in the range: the naturally-aspirated straight-six is robust, the chassis is magnificent, and the values are still sensible (£5,000–£10,000). If your budget stretches, the G29 M40i with the B58 is the most dependable modern roadster BMW has ever made. And on any E89 N54, budget for HPFP history before you buy — it's £3,500–£6,500 well spent."

The economics box (gold callout):
The Z Series rule — the roadster premium: A B58 recon at £3,800–£6,000 against a £15,000–£25,000 G29 sits at 15–40% — ✅ safe. An N54 recon at £3,500–£6,500 against a £6,000–£10,000 E89 is 35–108% — ⚠️ watch to ❌ walk away. An M54/N52 recon at £2,500–£5,000 against a £5,000–£8,000 E85 is 31–100% — ⚠️ watch on the cheapest cars. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. The E85 N52 is the sweet spot — robust engine, classic styling, and sensible values. The G29 B58 is the safe modern choice. The E89 N54 is the one to avoid without HPFP history.

SECTION 9 — HOW THE BMW Z SERIES ENGINE RANGE EVOLVED — <EngineEvolution>
H2: How the BMW Z Series Engine Range Evolved — 1995 to Today
Era
	Years
	Key Engines
	Why BMW Changed
	Worth Knowing
	Z3 — the original roadster
	1995–2002
	M44, M52, M54, S54
	BMW launched the Z3 as the modern roadster — the M44 four-cylinder was the entry point; the M52 and M54 straight-sixes were the driver's choice; the S54 in the Z3 M was the ultimate.
	The Z3 is the original modern BMW roadster — the M52 and M54 are the robust picks; the S54 is a masterpiece with VANOS caveats. The Z3 M is a collector's item.
	Z8 — the V8 masterpiece
	1999–2003
	S62B50
	BMW created the Z8 as a limited-production V8 roadster inspired by the 507. The S62 V8 was shared with the E39 M5, producing 394 bhp.
	The Z8 is a six-figure classic — engine work is restoration, not repair. The S62 V8 is one of BMW's most revered engines.
	Z4 E85 — the Bangle era
	2002–2008
	M54, N52, S54
	The Z4 replaced the Z3 with more aggressive styling and improved dynamics. The M54 was used initially; the N52 replaced it in 2006. The S54 powered the Z4 M.
	The E85 Z4 is the classic modern BMW roadster — the N52 is the robust pick. The Z4 M with the S54 is the collector's choice. The E86 coupé is the rare driver's choice.
	Z4 E89 — the retractable hardtop
	2009–2016
	N52, N54, N55
	The E89 Z4 introduced a retractable hardtop, the N52 straight-six, and the N54 twin-turbo. The N55 replaced the N54 in 2011.
	The N54 carries HPFP and injector caveats — the N52 and N55 are the safer choices. The E89 is the most technologically advanced Z4.
	Z4 G29 — the modern roadster
	2019–present
	B48, B58
	The G29 Z4 returned to a fabric roof and the CLAR platform with B-family engines. The B58 M40i produces 340 hp and is one of BMW's most reliable modern engines.
	The G29 is the Z Series for the modern era — the B58 is bulletproof, and the G29 is the last manual Z4. Buy it with confidence.
	________________


SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A BMW Z SERIES? — <WhoShouldBuy>
H2: Who Should Buy a BMW Z Series?
Buyer Profile
	Rating
	Our Verdict
	Weekend Driver
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The Z Series is built for this — the E85 Z4 3.0si with the N52 is the sweet spot: robust engine, classic styling, and sensible values (£5,000–£10,000). The G29 M40i is the modern alternative — 340 hp, B58 bulletproof reliability, and the last manual Z4.
	Enthusiast / Collector
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The Z3 M with the S54 is a rising classic — the ultimate Z3 and a future investment. The Z8 is the ultimate BMW roadster — a six-figure classic and a genuine investment. The Z4 M with the S54 is the modern classic — the E86 coupé is the rarest and most desirable.
	Daily Driver
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	The Z Series is a weekend car, not a daily driver — the Z4 E89 with the N52 is the most practical, but the Z3 and Z4 E85 are cramped and loud. The G29 is more refined but still a two-seater. Buy the Z Series for the weekend — buy a 3 Series for the daily.
	Budget Owner
	⭐⭐⭐☆☆
	A cheap E89 N54 is not a cheap Z4 — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £6,000 E85 3.0si with N52 is the bargain; a £5,000 E89 N54 without HPFP history is the trap. The Z3 2.8 with M52 is the classic budget choice.
	Long-Term Keeper
	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
	The E85 Z4 3.0si with the N52 is the modern keeper — the N52 is robust, the chassis is magnificent, and the values are only going one way. The G29 M40i with the B58 is the future keeper — the B58 will outlast the car around it, and the manual gearbox makes it a future classic.
	________________

SECTION 13 — BMW Z SERIES FAQ — <FAQAccordion>
H2: BMW Z Series Frequently Asked Questions
1. How much does a BMW Z Series engine replacement cost in the UK?
A reconditioned BMW Z Series engine typically costs £2,500–£6,500 supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the M54/N52 at £2,500–£5,000 to the N54 at £3,500–£6,500 and the B58 at £3,800–£6,000. The S62 (Z8) costs £5,000–£8,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.
2. Which BMW Z Series engine is the most reliable?
The B58B30 in the G29 M40i — widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. The M54B30 in the Z3 and early Z4 is the classic gold standard. The N52B30 in the E85/E89 is also excellent.
3. Which BMW Z Series engine should I avoid?
No engine outright — but an E89 N54B30 without documented HPFP replacement, injector history, or turbocharger history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A Z3/Z4 M54, N52, or G29 B58 has no such caveat.
4. How do I know if my Z Series engine needs replacing?
By engine: long cranking and limp mode (N54 HPFP), coolant loss (M54/N52 cooling system), rattling on cold start (N54 turbo wastegate), VANOS rattle (S54). On the E89 N54, any starting issues are a warning sign. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £2,500–£6,500 engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.
5. Is it worth replacing a BMW Z Series engine?
Depends entirely on generation and engine. Against a £15,000–£25,000 G29, a £3,800–£6,000 B58 recon is 15–40% — ✅ safe. Against a £6,000–£10,000 E89, a £3,500–£6,500 N54 recon is 35–108% — ⚠️ watch to ❌ walk away. Against a £5,000–£8,000 E85, a £2,500–£5,000 M54/N52 recon is 31–100% — ⚠️ watch. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.
6. Which BMW Z Series should I buy?
E85 Z4 3.0si with the N52 — the sweet spot: naturally-aspirated straight-six, robust engine, classic styling, and sensible values (£5,000–£10,000). For the classic, Z3 3.0i with the M54 — the original modern roadster and a rising classic. For the future, G29 M40i with the B58 — 340 hp, bulletproof, and the last manual Z4. For the collector, Z8 with the S62 — a six-figure classic and a genuine investment. Avoid E89 N54 without documented HPFP history.
7. How do I find my BMW Z Series engine code?
Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (Z3 2.8, Z4 3.0si, Z4 sDrive35i, G29 M40i) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
8. BMW Z4 vs Porsche Boxster — which is more reliable?
The Z4 with the N52 or B58 is significantly more reliable than the Boxster's M96/M97 flat-six, which is notorious for IMS bearing failure. The B58 is one of BMW's most reliable modern engines; the N52 is robust and proven. The Boxster offers sharper handling; the Z4 offers better reliability and lower running costs. Read the full head-to-head →
________________
